Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 1
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
Small-Capital Opportunities Manual
Enhanced Edition — 50 Equity Research Profiles
Prepared: April 16, 2026 Classification: CONFIDENTIAL
About This Document
This manual presents 50 equity ideas identified through a systematic screen of 10,391 publicly traded companies
using SEC EDGAR XBRL data. Each company profile includes:
• Analyst Summary — Deep narrative analysis with filing-level insights
• What A Screen Can't See — Qualitative alpha invisible to quantitative tools
• Munger Checklist — 10-criterion scoring framework (0-100 total)
• 10-Year Financial History — Revenue, earnings, margins, returns, and cash flows from EDGAR
The ideas are organized into five tiers based on the structural edge that creates the investment opportunity.
Table of Contents
• Tier 1: Institutional Orphans (7 companies) — Zero coverage, massive insider ownership, too small for funds
• Tier 2: Special Situations (4 companies) — Forced selling, activist arbitrage, tender offers
• Tier 3: Quality at Panic Prices (9 companies) — Filing-verified businesses at historically cheap multiples
• Tier 4: Quantitative Survivors (11 companies) — Passed all Munger criteria from 10,391-company EDGAR
screen
• Tier 5: Additional Ideas (19 companies) — Directionally correct, needs filing verification

TIER 1 — INSTITUTIONAL ORPHAN | Score: 78/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
CompX International is the single most "Buffett at the Moody's Manual" stock we found in this entire
10,391-company screen. It manufactures security locks for cabinets, data centers, vending machines, mailboxes,
and medical carts, plus marine exhaust headers and gauges for recreational boats. The company is 87.3% owned
by NL Industries, which is controlled by the Harold Simmons estate through Contran Corporation. Zero sell-side
analysts publish research on this company. The float is so small that institutions literally cannot build a position
without moving the stock.
What makes this exceptional is the combination of quality metrics at an orphan valuation. Revenue grew 8.5% to
$158.3M in FY2025 with net income up 17.5% to $19.5M. The company holds $54.1M in cash with absolutely
zero debt — a fortress balance sheet. The Marine segment rebounded with 36% YoY sales growth in Q3 2025 as
recreational boating demand recovered. ROIC is approximately 14%, which means the business is earning well
above its cost of capital and compounding value in silence.
The bear case is governance: this is a controlled subsidiary where minority shareholders have essentially zero
influence. The Simmons family could theoretically extract value through related-party transactions, though there
is no evidence of this. The company pays regular dividends but does not aggressively buy back stock. The lack of
any catalyst or IR effort means this stock could remain cheap indefinitely — but at 14.7x earnings with zero debt
and $54M cash (nearly 20% of market cap), you are being paid to wait.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
From the 10-K: The Security Products segment has relationships with OEMs in cabinets, data centers, and
vending that create specification-based switching costs. Once CompX's lock is designed into a cabinet platform,
the OEM doesn't switch for cost savings of pennies per unit. The Marine segment's recovery to 36% growth

signals pent-up replacement demand that may persist for multiple quarters.

2. OFLX — Omega Flex

TIER 1 — INSTITUTIONAL ORPHAN | Score: 72/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Omega Flex holds a near-monopoly position in corrugated stainless steel tubing (CSST) for gas distribution, sold
under the TracPipe and CounterStrike brands. The product is embedded in building codes across most US
jurisdictions — meaning it is literally required by law in new construction and renovation. 65% insider ownership
by the founding family means almost no float, and effectively zero traditional sell-side coverage exists.
The financial profile is extraordinary for a $298M market cap company: 60.6% gross margins, $49.3M in cash
against only $4.9M in debt (net cash of $44.4M or $4.40/share), and ~19% ROE. The company operates with the
margins of a software business while selling physical plumbing products. This is because CSST is a specialty
product with significant testing, certification, and code-adoption barriers — competitors cannot simply replicate
the regulatory approvals.
The concern is declining revenue: $98.3M in FY2025, down approximately 3% as housing and renovation
activity softened. At 20x P/E, you're paying a premium for a company with a shrinking topline. The thesis
requires housing construction recovery or code adoption in additional jurisdictions to reignite growth. The 65%
insider ownership provides alignment but also means the founding family controls the capital allocation decisions
with no outside pressure.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
CSST has faced litigation around lightning strikes causing gas leaks. Omega Flex developed the CounterStrike
product specifically to address this — it has a conductive jacket that carries lightning energy to ground. Building
codes increasingly specify CounterStrike or equivalent, which further entrenches Omega Flex's position. The
litigation history actually strengthened the moat by forcing a product upgrade that competitors haven't matched.

TIER 3 — QUALITY AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 82/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Veeva is the dominant vertical SaaS platform for the global life sciences industry, serving pharmaceutical
companies with clinical trial management, regulatory submissions, quality systems, and commercial CRM. From
the actual 10-K filing (FY2026, ended January 31, 2026): the company holds $6.56 billion in cash and short-term
investments with absolutely zero debt. Free cash flow margin is 43.4% and expanding — up from 37.4% two
years ago. The stock is down 54% from its all-time high of $343.
The R&D; and Quality Solutions segment is the growth engine — subscription revenue of $1.43 billion growing
at 20.9% annually, now representing 53.1% of total subscription revenue. This is the segment NOT affected by
the Salesforce CRM competition that the market fears. Commercial Solutions (where CRM lives) is still growing
13.8% despite Salesforce defections. The combined business generated $1.39 billion in free cash flow on $3.20
billion in revenue — a FCF margin that most software companies can only dream of.
The key risk from the actual filing: Salesforce has "announced that certain large Veeva CRM customers have
committed to purchasing its CRM solutions." This is real. But at 14.3x ex-cash free cash flow for a business
growing FCF at 30% annually, the market is pricing in permanent impairment that the numbers don't support. The
$2 billion share repurchase program (authorized January 2026) has already begun reducing the share count, and
net dilution from stock-based compensation is only 0.24% annually after buybacks.
WHAT THE 10-K REVEALS (THAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE)
1. Salesforce wind-down timeline: Agreement expired Sept 1, 2025. During wind-down (to Sept 2030), Veeva
can't sell Salesforce-based apps to NEW customers, and existing seats capped at 150% of Sept 2025 levels. After
2030, no Salesforce-based sales at all. 2. Contracts are ONE YEAR with termination for convenience — but
$1.49B in deferred revenue (up 16.9%) proves nobody is actually leaving. 3. EU Data Act (effective Sept 2025)

lets EU customers cancel without cause — Europe is 29.4% of subscription revenue. 4. AI acquisition: Bought
Ostro (AI-driven patient engagement) for ~$100M in March 2026 — signaling the AI strategy. 5. SBC declining:
14.8% of revenue (down from 16.7% two years ago) — dilution burden shrinking.
MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
Criterion 	Score 	Note

costs from validation requirements.

Balance Sheet 	10 	$6.56B cash, zero debt. Fort Knox.
Margin of Safety 	7 	14.3x ex-cash FCF for a 30% grower. Not
screaming cheap but well below fair value.
Catalyst 	6 	Vault CRM migration proving out. AI
products. Buyback acceleration. Gradual, not

down). Upside: $240+ (20x, 50% up). ~4:1
ratio.
Business Quality 	9 	43% FCF margin, 80%+ gross margin, 100%

TIER 3 — QUALITY AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 76/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Qualys is a cloud-based cybersecurity platform providing vulnerability management, web application scanning,
and compliance solutions to over 10,000 enterprise customers including a majority of the Forbes Global 100.
From the EDGAR XBRL data: FY2025 revenue was $669.1M (up 10.1%), GAAP net income $198.3M, and free
cash flow $304.4M — a 10% FCF yield on the current $3.0B market cap. The company has zero debt.
What separates Qualys from the pack of beaten-down cybersecurity stocks is profitability. The company has a
33% GAAP operating margin and 83% gross margin — metrics that most cybersecurity peers (CrowdStrike,
Tenable, Rapid7, SentinelOne) cannot match because they're still losing money or barely breaking even. The SaaS
selloff of 2025-2026 compressed all cybersecurity multiples indiscriminately, but Qualys was dragged down
alongside money-losing peers despite being fundamentally different.
The moat is in the deployment model: Qualys deploys lightweight agents across enterprise IT infrastructure —
servers, endpoints, cloud instances. Once embedded across tens of thousands of assets, ripping it out means
re-deploying an alternative across every endpoint, re-integrating with SIEM/SOAR systems, re-training security
teams, and rebuilding the vulnerability baseline history. This is a 6-12 month migration that no CISO undertakes
lightly. Net dollar retention of 103% confirms customers are staying and spending more.
WHAT THE FILING REVEALS
Revenue by product area shows subscription at ~100% of total — there is no hardware, no professional services
distortion. The $304M in FCF against $669M revenue means Qualys converts 45% of every revenue dollar to free
cash flow. With zero debt and no capex intensity ($5M total capex), this is an asset-light cash machine being
valued like a commodity.

TIER 3 — QUALITY AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 75/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
The market sees a declining textbook publisher. The 10-K reveals a research publishing tollbridge generating
$345M in EBITDA at 32% margins, plus a new $40M AI licensing revenue stream growing 74% year-over-year.
From the actual filing (FY2025, ended April 30, 2025): total adjusted EBITDA was $398M on $1.68B revenue.
The Research segment alone — which includes subscriptions to thousands of peer-reviewed scientific journals —
produced $345M in EBITDA. Researchers MUST publish in peer-reviewed journals to advance their careers.
Universities MUST subscribe to access that research. This is a tollbridge on global academic knowledge.
The sum-of-parts math from the filing: Research segment at a conservative 12x EBITDA (RELX/Elsevier trades
at 20x+) = $4.1B. Learning segment at 8x its $219M EBITDA = $1.75B. AI licensing optionality at 5x = $200M.
Subtract $714M net debt and $72M pension liability. Equity SOTP = $5.14B versus $2.0B market cap — a 60%
discount.
Critical finding from the filing: AI licensing revenue is recognized "at a point in time which is upon acceptance"
— meaning these are one-time deal-based transactions, NOT recurring subscriptions. The $40M could grow or it
could evaporate. But even without AI, the Research tollbridge at 12x EBITDA alone is worth double the entire
company. The Learning segment is NOT dying — it grew 1.7% in FY2025 and 5.2% in FY2024, powered by
digital courseware (WileyPLUS, zyBooks = 33% of Academic revenue).
WHAT THE 10-K REVEALS
1. 87% of Research Publishing revenue comes from journal subscriptions, Transformational Agreements, and
Open Access — these are multi-year institutional contracts with near-zero churn. 2. Debt maturity wall: $777M
of $800M total debt matures in November 2027 (2.5 years away). At 1.8x net leverage with $500M unused
revolver capacity, refinancing is achievable but will cost more at current rates. 3. Open access article output

grew 16% — the shift from "readers pay" to "authors/institutions pay" is actually GOOD for Wiley because
Transformational Agreements lock in multi-year revenue. 4. AI licensing split: Research contributed ~$11M,
Learning contributed ~$29M. Learning's backlist content is the bigger AI demand driver.
MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN

company. The two segments have different

Not a fortress.

Business Quality 	7 	Research at 32% EBITDA margin is
excellent. Learning at 37% margin is
surprisingly strong.
Executability 	7 	$2B market cap. Adequately liquid.
Inversion (Bear Case) 	6 	Open-access mandates could pressure

TIER 3 — QUALITY AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 76/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
From the actual 10-K (FY2025): Arch Capital produced record net income of $4.40 billion on $17.07 billion in
net premiums earned, with a consolidated combined ratio of 82.8% — meaning the company earns nearly 17
cents of underwriting profit on every dollar of premium before investment income. Return on equity was 20.1%.
The company repurchased $1.89 billion in stock (6% of shares) at an average price of $89.26, and has bought
back 455 million shares for $7.8 billion since 2007.
The investment portfolio is $46.5 billion with an average credit quality of AA-/Aa3 and duration of 3.34 years —
conservative and high-quality. Prior year reserve releases of $600M ($507M in 2024, $538M in 2023)
demonstrate consistent reserving conservatism — reserves keep proving to be more than adequate. The reserve
simulation shows an 18.4% cushion between recorded reserves and the 90th percentile adverse scenario.
At 8.3x trailing earnings for a 20% ROE specialty insurer buying back 6% of its shares annually, the market is
applying the permanent "insurance discount" to a business that compounds intrinsic value faster than most tech
companies. Buffett built Berkshire Hathaway on exactly this model — using insurance float to invest while
underwriting generates its own profits. Arch is executing the same playbook.
WHAT THE 10-K REVEALS
1. Reinsurance segment (47.6% of premiums): 80.8% combined ratio — printing money. 2. Mortgage segment:
14.6% combined ratio. Essentially a license to collect premiums. 3. Long-tail adverse development: $82M in
2025 from casualty (2021-2024 accident years) — a yellow flag worth monitoring but small relative to $24.5B
total reserves. 4. $702M remaining buyback authorization as of Feb 24, 2026. Post year-end: 4.3M additional
shares bought for $405M.

Dividend Yield
—
TIER 4 — QUANTITATIVE SURVIVOR | Score: 78/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Acadia sells two monopoly drugs: NUPLAZID (the ONLY FDA-approved treatment for Parkinson's disease
psychosis) and DAYBUE (the ONLY FDA-approved treatment for Rett syndrome). Combined 2025 revenue
exceeded $1 billion with 92% gross margins, 32% ROE, and 28% ROIC. The stock trades at 9.5x trailing
earnings — a multiple typically reserved for declining commodity businesses, not pharmaceutical companies with
regulatory monopolies on orphan diseases.
The monopoly economics are extraordinary. NUPLAZID has no competing approved therapy. Patents extend
through 2030, with settlements against Teva and Lupin securing exclusivity to February 2035. DAYBUE
launched in 2023 and is ramping — an oral formulation (STIX) is broadening the patient reach. Orphan drug
designation provides additional regulatory protection. Management targets $1.7B in revenue by 2028 — a 70%
increase from current levels.
The market prices ACAD cheaply because of biotech sector rotation (growth investors abandoned the sector),
single-disease concentration risk, and the upcoming remlifanserin Phase 2 readout for Alzheimer's psychosis
(August-October 2026). If remlifanserin works, ACAD has a third monopoly drug in an enormous market. If it
fails, the stock still has two monopoly products generating $1B+ at 92% gross margins for under $4B market cap.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "only-in-class" designation creates a unique pricing dynamic. Generic competition is economically
unattractive for orphan diseases with small patient populations — the cost of developing a generic and running
clinical trials exceeds the revenue opportunity. This means NUPLAZID and DAYBUE face less generic erosion
than typical branded pharmaceuticals even after patent expiry.

TIER 4 — QUANTITATIVE SURVIVOR | Score: 74/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Twenty million tax returns per year. The most recession-proof business in America — people file taxes in every
economic condition. From the 10-K: FY2025 revenue was $3.76B with net income of $606M and free cash flow
of $599M. The stock trades at 7.1x trailing earnings and 5.3x forward earnings — representing a 45% discount to
its own 10-year average P/E. Management is buying back stock at the most aggressive pace in company history,
having retired 47% of shares outstanding since 2016.
The AI disruption narrative is what's keeping the stock cheap. TurboTax has deployed AI chatbots. The IRS
launched Direct File for simple returns. The bear case says AI will make tax preparation obsolete. The filing tells
a different story: revenue grew 4.2% in FY2025, and the assisted tax preparation model (humans helping humans
in offices) has gained share in 3 of the last 4 years. Tax complexity is INCREASING — gig economy 1099-K
forms, cryptocurrency reporting, multi-state filing requirements — all of which drive consumers TOWARD
professional help, not away from it.
The total shareholder yield is approximately 15%+ annually (10% buyback + 5% dividend). At 5.3x forward
earnings, the company can retire another 15-20% of its shares in the next two years even if the business never
grows. This is a See's Candies-style cash extraction play with a defined lifespan — collect the 15% yield for 5-7
years before AI becomes a material threat.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Wave small business platform (acquired 2019) adds a second revenue stream beyond tax preparation —
payroll, accounting, invoicing for small businesses. Management describes it as a "landmark year" but doesn't
break out specific revenue. This is the growth option the market isn't pricing.

TIER 3 — QUALITY AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 70/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
From the actual 10-K (fiscal year ended February 1, 2025): Build-A-Bear generated $496.4M in revenue with
$51.8M in net income, 55.0% retail gross margin (expanding for three consecutive years), and absolutely zero
long-term debt. The company retired 6.5% of its shares outstanding in a single year through aggressive buybacks,
with an additional $85M remaining on a $100M authorization.
The hidden story is the Commercial segment. Revenue grew from $18.5M to $25.4M to $31.4M over three years
— a 70% cumulative increase — at a 57.2% gross margin that is HIGHER than the retail segment. This is pure
brand monetization: licensing the Build-A-Bear brand to third-party retail partners (Carnival Cruise Line, Great
Wolf Lodge, Girl Scouts), wholesale accounts, and entertainment ventures. The contribution margin is $16.8M on
$31.4M revenue — essentially free money flowing from the brand's recognition.
The stock dropped 49% from its September 2025 high of ~$75 to ~$38. At 8.8x trailing P/E with zero debt and a
growing licensing business, the market is pricing this as a dying mall retailer. But Build-A-Bear has no real
competitor in experiential children's retail. The brand creates emotional attachment — children literally build their
own stuffed animals. The third-party retail locations grew from 70 to 92 to 138 in three years, demonstrating that
partners are paying to associate their venues with the Build-A-Bear experience.
WHAT THE 10-K REVEALS
1. Store productivity: Net retail sales per square foot $492 (FY2024) — strong for specialty retail. 2.
Third-party retail: 138 partner locations (up from 70 two years ago) including Carnival Cruise, Great Wolf
Lodge, Landry's. 3. International franchise: 83 locations across South Africa (22), Australia (21), Gulf States
(20), Chile (12), China (8). 4. Risk Factor #3: Dependence on licensors — specifically names Disney, Pokemon,
NBCUniversal, Nintendo, Sanrio. If any major IP partner pulls licensing, traffic would drop. 5. Share count:

TIER 2 — SPECIAL SITUATION | Score: 75/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Highland Opportunities & Income Fund trades at a 43-55% discount to its reported net asset value of $11.38 per
share, with the stock at approximately $5-6. This is the widest CEF discount in our entire screen. Saba Capital —
the most prolific and successful closed-end fund activist in the United States — has filed a Schedule 13D
disclosing a 5.05% stake and explicitly stated they may pursue "discount-to-NAV, governance, capitalization,
open- versus closed-end structure, or liquidation timing" changes.
The Saba playbook is well-documented and effective: buy CEFs at deep discounts, push for board changes
through proxy fights, force tender offers at or near NAV, or push for conversion to open-end structure (which
eliminates the discount entirely). Saba has executed this playbook successfully at dozens of funds including
BlackRock trusts, Pioneer funds, and others. The settlement record shows tender offers at 98-99.5% of NAV are
the typical outcome.
The risk is real: the discount exists because the fund holds some illiquid assets, and the NAV may be overstated if
those assets are marked above true realizable value. NexPoint Asset Management runs the fund, and management
has historically been resistant to shareholder-friendly actions. But with Saba on the register at 5%+ and actively
filing proxy materials, the probability of some form of discount-closing action within 12-18 months is
meaningful.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Saba 13D filing language is explicit about intentions. This is not passive investment — it is an activist
campaign with a proven playbook. Saba's track record shows a ~70%+ success rate at CEF campaigns. The
monthly distribution of $0.0385/share provides income while waiting. Even if the discount only narrows from
50% to 25% (not full closure), that represents a 50% return on the current price.

TIER 4 — CASH MACHINE | Score: 73/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Buckle is one of the most quietly profitable specialty retailers in America, operating 440+ stores concentrated in
secondary and tertiary markets across the Midwest, South, and Mountain West — towns where a Buckle store is
often the best denim shopping experience within a 45-minute drive. The company has built a remarkably durable
niche selling premium denim and casual apparel with a commission-based sales model that turns store associates
into relationship managers. At $2.9 billion market cap with a 13.1x P/E, zero debt, 49% gross margins, and a 49%
ROE, this is a business generating returns on equity that most tech companies would envy — from selling jeans.
The private-label brands (BKE, Buckle Black, Outpost Makers, and others) now comprise 43-48% of total sales,
and this is the structural advantage the market underappreciates. Private-label denim carries significantly higher
margins than third-party brands, creates exclusivity that prevents direct price comparison with Amazon or
department stores, and gives Buckle control over its supply chain. The commission-based sales associates develop
genuine relationships with repeat customers, knowing their sizes, preferences, and purchase history. This creates a
customer experience that online retailers simply cannot replicate, particularly in markets where fashion retail
options are limited.
Buckle is a cash machine that has regularly paid special dividends on top of its regular quarterly payout, returning
massive amounts of capital to shareholders. Revenue grew 7% in the most recent period, demonstrating that this is
not a declining retailer — it is a niche operator still finding pockets of growth. The zero-debt balance sheet means
the company could survive virtually any retail downturn. The bear case is that same-store sales will eventually
plateau as the secondary-market advantage erodes, but at 13.1x earnings with nearly 50% ROE, the business can
afford to be boring and still compound shareholder wealth at attractive rates.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 25 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 26
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The commission-based compensation model creates a fundamentally different incentive structure than typical
retail. Buckle associates earn commissions on individual sales, which means they are motivated to build personal
client books, follow up on customer preferences, and drive repeat visits. Turnover among top-performing
associates is low because their earnings are directly tied to their customer relationships. This creates a
self-reinforcing loop: experienced associates sell more, earn more, stay longer, and build deeper customer loyalty.
It is the retail equivalent of an insurance agent's book of business — and it does not show up in any financial line
item.
MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN

geographic niche. Real but not impregnable.
Balance Sheet 	10 	Zero debt. Massive cash generation. Special
dividends prove it.

quality of the business.

dividends and buybacks.

returns. Upside from continued execution.

economics for a retailer.

build a position.

cushion.

TIER 3 — ORPHAN DRUG MONOPOLY | Score: 72/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Catalyst Pharmaceuticals owns the only FDA-approved treatment for Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome
(LEMS), a rare autoimmune disorder affecting approximately 3,000-5,000 patients in the United States.
FIRDAPSE (amifampridine) received orphan drug designation and FDA approval, granting Catalyst a regulatory
moat that combines patent protection through 2035 with orphan drug exclusivity. At $3.1 billion market cap, the
stock trades at 14.7x earnings with 85% gross margins and 54% ROIC — financial metrics that reflect the pricing
power of a monopoly in a disease with no alternative therapies.
The beauty of the orphan drug model is the economics: FIRDAPSE treats a small patient population, which
means limited commercial infrastructure is required, no massive sales force is needed, and the FDA's orphan drug
incentives (tax credits, extended exclusivity, reduced regulatory burden) dramatically improve the return on
invested capital. Revenue grew 20% in the trailing period as Catalyst continues to identify and enroll new LEMS
patients — many of whom were previously undiagnosed or misdiagnosed. The 85% gross margin converts
efficiently to free cash flow because the cost structure is lean and the drug is manufactured by a contract
manufacturer.
The risk profile centers on single-product concentration. FIRDAPSE is essentially the entire revenue base, which
means any patent challenge, generic entry, or competitive therapy would be existential. The patents extending to
2035 provide a long runway, but Paragraph IV challenges from generic manufacturers are always possible in
orphan drug markets. The company has been building a pipeline through acquisitions, but until a second
commercial product reaches meaningful revenue, Catalyst remains a one-drug company priced as a growth story.
At 14.7x earnings, the valuation is not demanding if FIRDAPSE maintains its trajectory — but the binary risk of
patent challenges means position sizing matters.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 28 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 29
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
Lambert-Eaton syndrome is chronically underdiagnosed because its symptoms (muscle weakness, fatigue)
overlap with dozens of other conditions. Catalyst invests in disease awareness campaigns targeting neurologists,
which serves the dual purpose of helping patients and expanding the addressable market. Every newly diagnosed
LEMS patient is essentially a lifetime customer — there is no alternative therapy, so once a patient begins
FIRDAPSE, they remain on it indefinitely. The patient identification runway is longer than the market assumes
because the diagnosed patient pool is still a fraction of the estimated total prevalence.
MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN
Criterion 	Score 	Note
Understanding 	7 	Orphan drug for rare disease. Simple business
model, complex science.
Moat 	9 	Only FDA-approved treatment. Patents to
2035. Orphan drug exclusivity.
Balance Sheet 	7 	Clean balance sheet, strong cash generation.
Not fortress-level but healthy.
Margin of Safety 	6 	14.7x PE is fair, not cheap, for a 20% grower
with 85% margins.
Catalyst 	6 	Continued patient identification. Pipeline
acquisitions. Steady, not explosive.
Risk/Reward Asymmetry 	6 	Upside from patient growth. Downside from
patent challenge is binary.
Business Quality 	9 	85% GM, 54% ROIC, 20% growth.
Monopoly economics.

Inversion (Bear Case) 	5 	Single-product risk is real. Generic challenge
would be devastating.

TIER 3 — REINSURANCE TURNAROUND | Score: 72/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
SiriusPoint is a Bermuda-based specialty reinsurer undergoing a genuine turnaround under CEO Scott Egan, who
took the helm and has systematically cleaned up an underwriting book that was previously plagued by excessive
risk-taking and poor reserve development. At approximately $2.5 billion market cap, the stock trades at just 5.9x
earnings — a valuation that reflects the market's lingering distrust of the company's legacy issues rather than the
current trajectory. The 20.9% ROE and 91.7% combined ratio demonstrate that the underwriting discipline is real
and producing results.
The most compelling capital allocation signal is the share retirement program: SiriusPoint has repurchased and
retired 28% of its outstanding shares, a staggering level of buyback activity that reflects management's conviction
that the stock is deeply undervalued. When a reinsurer with a 91.7% combined ratio (meaning it earns an
underwriting profit before investment income) is retiring shares at 5.9x earnings, the math becomes extremely
favorable for remaining shareholders. Each dollar of book value is being purchased for roughly 60 cents, and the
underlying book value is growing at 20%+ annually.
The pending AM Best rating upgrade would be a significant catalyst. Reinsurance is a business where credit
ratings directly determine the volume and quality of business you can write — an upgrade would allow
SiriusPoint to access higher-quality cedants and command better pricing. The bear case is that Bermuda reinsurers
have a history of blowing up during catastrophic events, and SiriusPoint's past includes exactly the kind of
undisciplined underwriting that leads to those outcomes. The question is whether Egan's reforms are structural or
temporary — the 91.7% combined ratio across multiple quarters suggests the former, but reinsurance cycles can
mask deterioration until a major loss event reveals it.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 31 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 32
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The 28% share retirement is not a routine buyback program — it represents a fundamental restructuring of the
capital base. In reinsurance, float leverage means that reducing the share count while maintaining the same
premium volume amplifies per-share economics dramatically. SiriusPoint is effectively concentrating its float into
fewer shares, which means each remaining share has a larger claim on both underwriting profits and investment
income from the float. The AM Best upgrade, if it materializes, would create a virtuous cycle: better rating leads
to better business, which leads to better results, which supports further rating improvement.
MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN

Moat 	5 	Reinsurance has scale advantages but no
durable moat at this size. Rating upgrade
would help.
Balance Sheet 	7 	Adequate capital ratios for the business. Not
overleveraged.
Margin of Safety 	9 	5.9x PE on a 20.9% ROE reinsurer. Deep
value if the turnaround holds.
Catalyst 	7 	AM Best upgrade pending. Continued
buybacks. Clear timeline.
Risk/Reward Asymmetry 	8 	Downside: reversion to poor underwriting.
Upside: re-rating to 10x+ PE. 3:1 or better.
Business Quality 	7 	91.7% combined ratio is strong. 20.9% ROE
is excellent for a reinsurer.
Executability 	7 	$2.5B cap. Liquid enough for most portfolios.
Inversion (Bear Case) 	5 	History of poor underwriting. One
catastrophic quarter could erase gains.
Macro Independence 	6 	Reinsurance is somewhat counter-cyclical but
exposed to catastrophe risk.

TIER 3 — BRAND TURNAROUND AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 70/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Abercrombie & Fitch has executed one of the most remarkable brand turnarounds in retail history, transforming
from a toxic brand associated with exclusionary marketing into a modern, inclusive retailer that resonates with
millennials and Gen Z. At $4.1 billion market cap, the stock trades at 8.7x earnings with 73% gross margins and
36% ROE — and is down 31% from its recent highs, creating a potential entry point for a business that has
proven its reinvention is real. The Hollister brand, which targets teens and young adults, continues to perform
strongly and provides diversification beyond the flagship Abercrombie nameplate.
The 73% gross margin is the financial fingerprint of a brand that has regained pricing power. This is not a
company discounting its way to relevance — it is charging full price for product that consumers genuinely want.
The 36% ROE demonstrates that the turnaround is not just a topline story but has translated into genuine
profitability improvement. Management has invested in direct-to-consumer digital channels, rightsized the store
fleet, and repositioned the brand aesthetic from dark, perfume-soaked stores to clean, welcoming retail
environments that reflect current consumer preferences.
The tariff risk is the headline concern: an estimated $90 million annual impact from tariffs on imported apparel,
primarily from Asia. This is real and material — it represents a meaningful percentage of operating income.
However, Abercrombie has levers to pull: sourcing diversification, selective price increases (which the 73%
margin provides room for), and product mix shifts. At 8.7x earnings and 31% below highs, the market appears to
be pricing in worst-case tariff scenarios while ignoring the structural improvement in the business. The question is
whether the brand momentum can sustain through a period of cost inflation — the Hollister strength suggests the
answer is more likely yes than no.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 34 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 35
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The brand transformation required a complete overhaul of the company's cultural DNA, not just its marketing
materials. The old Abercrombie was built on exclusivity and aspiration through physical perfection; the new
Abercrombie is built on inclusivity and self-expression. This cultural shift permeates everything from hiring
practices to store design to social media strategy. What a financial screen cannot capture is the depth of this
transformation — it shows up as "73% gross margin" in the numbers, but behind that number is a brand that has
successfully navigated one of the most difficult repositioning challenges in consumer retail. The Hollister brand's
continued strength suggests this is not a one-time phenomenon but a competency in brand management.

TIER 3 — FIRST-IN-CLASS NEPHROLOGY | Score: 70/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Aurinia Pharmaceuticals owns LUPKYNIS (voclosporin), the first and only FDA-approved oral treatment
specifically for lupus nephritis — a severe kidney inflammation that affects approximately 60,000-100,000
patients in the United States and is a leading cause of kidney failure in young women. At $2.0 billion market cap
and 6.8x earnings, the stock offers access to a first-in-class drug with 88% gross margins and patents extending
through the late 2030s. This is a rare combination of clinical leadership and value pricing in a biotech sector
where most companies with first-in-class drugs trade at premiums to the market.
LUPKYNIS addressed a massive unmet medical need. Prior to its approval, lupus nephritis was treated with
off-label immunosuppressants that lacked specific FDA approval for the indication, meaning physicians were
improvising treatment protocols without robust clinical trial data. LUPKYNIS was approved based on the
AURORA trial, which demonstrated statistically significant superiority in achieving complete renal response
compared to standard of care. The 88% gross margin reflects the pricing power of a drug with no direct
FDA-approved competitor in its specific indication, and the growth trajectory suggests the drug is still in the
early-to-mid stages of its commercial ramp.
The single-product risk is the dominant concern. Aurinia is essentially LUPKYNIS and nothing else — if the drug
faces unexpected safety signals, generic challenges, or competitive entry from a superior molecule, the company's
entire revenue base is at risk. The patent protection through the late 2030s provides a meaningful runway, but
biotech investors have learned that patents can be challenged and unexpected adverse events can emerge
post-approval. At 6.8x earnings, the market is applying a significant discount for this concentration risk, which
means the stock is either a deep value opportunity in a monopoly therapeutic or a single-event risk that justifies
the discount. Position sizing should reflect this binary nature.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 37 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 38
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
Lupus nephritis disproportionately affects young women of color, a demographic that is historically underserved
in clinical trials and commercial drug development. LUPKYNIS was specifically studied in a diverse patient
population, which strengthens both its clinical evidence base and its commercial relevance to the actual patient
population. The nephrology prescriber base is concentrated — a relatively small number of nephrologists manage
the majority of lupus nephritis patients — which means Aurinia can achieve high prescriber penetration with a
focused sales force. This concentrated prescriber base also creates a natural information network where positive
clinical experiences spread quickly among peers.

risk.

Pipeline additions. Gradual.

TIER 2 — SEE'S CANDIES ECONOMICS | Score: 71/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Prestige Consumer Healthcare is a portfolio of #1 or #2 OTC healthcare brands — Monistat, Dramamine, Clear
Eyes, BC Powder, Luden's, and others — that collectively generate $1.1 billion in revenue with only $8 million in
annual capital expenditure. Read that ratio again: $8M in capex on $1.1B in revenue. This is a business that
requires virtually no reinvestment to maintain its revenue base because it does not manufacture anything — it
outsources 100% of production to contract manufacturers and spends its management attention on brand
stewardship and distribution. At $2.7 billion market cap and 12.7x P/E with 56% gross margins, this is the
consumer healthcare equivalent of a royalty stream.
The "See's Candies economics" comparison is precise. Warren Buffett has described See's as the ideal business:
one that requires minimal capital reinvestment, generates enormous free cash flow relative to its asset base, and
has brand loyalty that sustains pricing power across economic cycles. Prestige Consumer Healthcare shares every
one of these characteristics. Monistat is the #1 OTC yeast infection treatment. Dramamine is the #1 motion
sickness brand. Clear Eyes is the #1 redness-relief eye drop. These are category-dominant brands where the
consumer reaches for the trusted name without price comparison — and since Prestige does not own factories,
nearly all of the gross margin flows through to free cash flow.
The acquisition strategy is the growth engine: Prestige buys orphaned OTC brands from large pharma companies
(which divest them as non-core) at reasonable multiples, applies its lean operating model, and extracts decades of
steady cash flow. The debt taken on for acquisitions is the primary balance sheet risk, but the predictability and
durability of OTC healthcare cash flows make this debt manageable. At 12.7x earnings, you are paying a modest
premium for a business with the cash conversion characteristics of a software company and the recession
resilience of a healthcare company.

-- 40 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 41
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The $8 million capex figure is the single most important number in the entire financial profile, and it is easily
overlooked on a screen focused on earnings multiples and revenue growth. Virtually every dollar of EBITDA
converts to free cash flow because Prestige does not own manufacturing facilities, does not invest in R&D; for
new molecules, and does not face technology obsolescence risk. The brands themselves are the assets, and they do
not depreciate — Dramamine has been the leading motion sickness brand for decades and requires no capital
investment to maintain that position. The contract manufacturing model also means Prestige can switch suppliers
if costs rise, creating competitive tension that a vertically integrated competitor cannot replicate.

TIER 3 — QUALITY AT PANIC PRICE | Score: 69/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Duolingo is the world's most popular language-learning platform with over 100 million daily active users, a
number that exceeds the populations of most countries and represents a network effect that no competitor can
replicate. The stock has collapsed 80% from its all-time high of $545, creating the rare opportunity to buy a
high-growth consumer tech company at 10.3x earnings with 72% gross margins and 43% ROIC. Revenue grew
39% in the trailing period, and the DAU figure continues to climb — this is not a company in decline; it is a
company in a market-wide growth stock selloff being priced as though its growth has already ended.
The gamification engine is Duolingo's true competitive advantage. The company has built what is arguably the
most sophisticated engagement loop in consumer software — streaks, leaderboards, hearts, gems, and social
features create an addictive daily habit that drives retention metrics more comparable to social media than to
education. This engagement translates directly to monetization: Duolingo converts free users to Super Duolingo
subscribers at an increasing rate, and the 100M+ DAU base provides an enormous conversion funnel. The 72%
gross margin reflects the operating leverage of a digital platform — incremental users cost essentially nothing to
serve.
Management has explicitly communicated a willingness to sacrifice near-term profitability for growth, which is
the correct strategic decision at this stage but creates the earnings volatility that value investors dislike. The AI
competition fear — that ChatGPT and other LLMs will replace dedicated language-learning apps — is the
market's primary concern and the reason for the 80% drawdown. This fear is overstated: AI chatbots can help with
language practice, but they cannot replicate the structured curriculum, gamified engagement, and progressive
difficulty that make Duolingo effective for beginners and intermediate learners. At 10.3x earnings for a 39%
grower with 100M+ DAU, the market is pricing Duolingo as though AI will destroy the business — if that thesis
is wrong, the re-rating potential is enormous.

-- 43 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 44
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 100 million DAU figure understates the competitive position because of the engagement quality. Duolingo
users open the app daily as a habit, not as a task — the streak mechanic creates a psychological commitment that
makes churning feel like a personal failure. This engagement depth means Duolingo's user base is not comparable
to apps with similar DAU numbers but lower session frequency or duration. The company has also expanded
beyond language learning into math and music education, creating optionality that the market is assigning zero
value to at the current price. The AI threat narrative also ignores that Duolingo itself is one of the most
sophisticated AI users in consumer tech — its Birdbrain AI engine personalizes lessons for each user, and the
company has integrated GPT-4 into its premium tier to enhance, not replace, the core product.

TIER 4 — HIDDEN FINTECH TRANSITION | Score: 67/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
OneSpan provides digital identity verification and authentication solutions to banks and financial institutions,
with a customer base that includes 60% of the world's top 100 banks. At $400 million market cap, this is a
micro-cap fintech company trading at 8.4x earnings with 74% gross margins — and analysts have an average
price target of $16.67, implying approximately 60% upside from current levels. The company is in the midst of a
critical strategic transition from legacy hardware authentication tokens (the physical devices banks give to
corporate clients) to cloud-based software solutions, and this transition is the key to understanding both the
opportunity and the risk.
The hardware-to-software transition mirrors what happened in dozens of technology companies over the past
decade, from Cisco to Adobe: recurring software revenue replaces one-time hardware sales, temporarily
depressing growth rates as the legacy business declines while the software business ramps. For OneSpan, this
means a period of optically messy financials — hardware revenue declining, software revenue growing, and the
combined topline looking stagnant — that obscures the dramatic improvement in business quality happening
underneath. Software revenue carries higher margins, greater predictability, and longer customer lifetimes than
hardware tokens.
The 60% penetration of top-100 global banks is the embedded competitive advantage. Replacing a security and
authentication vendor at a major bank is an extraordinarily painful process involving regulatory approvals,
integration testing, employee retraining, and audit trail migration. These switching costs mean OneSpan's existing
customer base is essentially captive, and the hardware-to-software transition is being conducted with these same
customers — upgrading existing relationships rather than winning new ones. At 8.4x earnings with 74% gross
margins and a clear upgrade path within an entrenched customer base, OneSpan offers asymmetric upside if the
software transition executes as planned.

-- 46 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 47
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The regulatory compliance dimension of bank authentication is the hidden moat. Banks do not choose
authentication vendors based purely on features or price — they choose vendors whose products have been
audited, certified, and approved by regulators in every jurisdiction where the bank operates. OneSpan has
accumulated decades of regulatory certifications across dozens of countries, creating a compliance moat that no
startup can shortcut. A new entrant would need years and millions of dollars in regulatory approval processes
before a major bank would even consider them for a pilot program. This compliance infrastructure is invisible on
the balance sheet but is arguably the company's most valuable asset.

TIER 4 — CONDITIONAL PICK | Score: 65/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Amphastar is a specialty pharmaceutical company focused on complex injectable drugs and inhalation products,
holding over 100 patents and anchored by BAQSIMI, a nasal glucagon powder for severe hypoglycemia with
patent protection extending to 2041. At $970 million market cap and 9.3x P/E, the stock appears optically cheap
— but this is a conditional pick because the business is currently experiencing meaningful deterioration: revenue
is declining and earnings have fallen 38% from peak levels. The thesis requires a stabilization and recovery that is
not yet visible in the financial trend.
The complex injectables niche is structurally attractive because the manufacturing complexity and regulatory
requirements create barriers to entry that simple generic oral drugs do not possess. Injectable products require
specialized aseptic manufacturing facilities, stability testing that can take years, and FDA inspections that are
more rigorous than for oral generics. Amphastar has invested decades in building this manufacturing capability,
and the 100+ patent portfolio protects specific formulations and delivery mechanisms. BAQSIMI, the nasal
glucagon product, represents the highest-value asset with its 2041 patent date providing nearly two decades of
protection.
The 38% earnings decline is the central concern and the reason this is classified as conditional. The decline
reflects competitive pressures in some of Amphastar's legacy injectable products, pricing erosion in the generic
pharmaceutical market, and potentially some one-time factors that need to be disaggregated from structural
trends. At 9.3x earnings on depressed numbers, the stock could be a deep value opportunity if earnings stabilize
— but if the decline continues, 9.3x on a falling earnings base is not cheap, it is a value trap. Investors should
require evidence of earnings stabilization before committing significant capital, with BAQSIMI growth and
pipeline launches as the key monitoring metrics.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 49 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 50
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The 100+ patent portfolio and BAQSIMI's 2041 expiration create a long-duration asset base that the current
earnings decline may be obscuring. Complex injectables are not like commodity generics — each product
requires its own ANDA filing with product-specific bioequivalence data, and the manufacturing requirements
create natural supply constraints that prevent the aggressive price competition seen in oral generics. Amphastar's
integrated manufacturing model (it produces its own products rather than outsourcing) gives it cost control and
quality oversight that contract-manufactured competitors lack. The earnings decline may be cyclical rather than
structural, but confirming this requires tracking the trajectory over the next two to three quarters.

TIER 3 — HIDDEN ASSET WITH COMPLEXITY | Score: 69/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Innospec is a specialty chemical company with a split personality: the Fuel Specialties division is a high-margin
gem with 20.6% operating margins protected by EPA registration requirements that take years and millions of
dollars to replicate, while the Oilfield Services segment has collapsed 43% and drags down the consolidated
financials. At approximately $1.2 billion market cap and 10.1x P/E, the stock offers a fortress balance sheet —
zero debt with $293 million in cash — that provides both downside protection and strategic optionality. The
combined picture is a company cheaper than it should be because the market is averaging a great business with a
terrible one.
The Fuel Specialties segment is the crown jewel. EPA registration for fuel additives is an arduous, expensive,
multi-year process that effectively grandfathers existing registered products and creates an impenetrable barrier
for new entrants. Innospec's fuel additives are registered for specific formulations in specific geographies, and
customers (refineries, fuel blenders) cannot easily switch to unregistered alternatives without violating
environmental regulations. This is as close to a government-granted monopoly as exists in the specialty chemical
space, and the 20.6% operating margin reflects this competitive insulation.
The TEL (tetraethyl lead) phaseout risk is the long-tail concern: Innospec is the sole global supplier of TEL for
leaded aviation gasoline, but the global phase-out timeline targeting 2030 means this revenue stream has a defined
end date. The Oilfield segment's 43% collapse reflects the cyclicality of energy services spending, and while
recovery is possible, the timing is tied to oil prices and drilling activity that are inherently unpredictable. At 10.1x
earnings with zero debt and $293M in cash (nearly 25% of market cap), you are buying the Fuel Specialties
business at a significant discount to its standalone value, getting the Oilfield recovery optionality for free, and
holding a cash balance that provides years of strategic flexibility.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 52 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 53
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The EPA registration moat in Fuel Specialties is genuinely invisible to quantitative screens because it does not
manifest as a patent, trademark, or any other balance sheet asset. It exists in the regulatory files at the EPA —
specific chemical formulations registered to Innospec for specific end-use applications. A competitor cannot
simply reverse-engineer the product and sell it; they must independently register their formulation through a
process that involves toxicology testing, environmental impact assessment, and EPA review cycles measured in
years. The $293 million cash pile with zero debt also gives Innospec the optionality to acquire additional
registered formulations from competitors who may exit the specialty fuel additives market, further consolidating
its regulatory moat.

TIER 3 — ROYALTY STREAM AT SCALE | Score: 68/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Restaurant Brands International is the parent company of Burger King, Tim Hortons, Popeyes, and Firehouse
Subs, operating a nearly pure franchise model across 33,041 restaurants in 126 countries. The business is
fundamentally a royalty collection operation: $1.977 billion in pure royalty revenue flows from franchisees to
RBI with minimal capital requirements, creating a recurring revenue stream with the predictability of a toll road.
Seth Klarman's Baupost Group holds a $551 million position, lending value investor credibility to the thesis. At
approximately $37 billion market cap and roughly 20x P/E, the stock is fairly valued rather than cheap — the
opportunity here is in the quality and durability of the cash flows, not a screaming bargain.
The franchise model economics are exceptional: RBI collects royalties (typically 4-5% of gross sales) from
33,041 locations without bearing the capital expenditure, lease obligations, labor costs, or operational complexity
of running restaurants. This is a capital-light model that generates enormous free cash flow relative to its invested
capital base. The 126-country footprint provides geographic diversification that few consumer brands can match,
and the multi-brand portfolio (Burger King for burgers, Tim Hortons for coffee, Popeyes for chicken, Firehouse
for subs) reduces dependence on any single consumer trend or competitive dynamic.
The $13.4 billion debt load is the elephant in the room and the reason the stock is "fairly valued" rather than
"compellingly cheap" at 20x earnings. RBI operates with private-equity-style leverage, a legacy of the 3G
Capital-backed acquisition structure that created the company. The debt is manageable given the royalty stream's
predictability, but it limits financial flexibility, amplifies downside during systemwide sales declines, and reduces
the margin of safety that value investors typically demand. At 20x earnings, you are paying a fair price for a
high-quality royalty stream — this is a "hold and collect" position for investors who want steady,
inflation-protected cash flows rather than a deep value opportunity with significant upside.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 55 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 56
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The royalty model creates a natural inflation hedge that does not appear in any financial metric. When menu
prices increase (as they inevitably do with food cost inflation), RBI's royalty revenue increases proportionally
without any corresponding cost increase. A 5% menu price increase across 33,041 restaurants flows directly to
RBI's topline with no additional expense. This inflation pass-through mechanism means that in inflationary
environments, RBI's real purchasing power actually increases — the royalty stream grows with nominal GDP
while the cost base remains largely fixed. Klarman's $551 million position suggests he values this
inflation-protected royalty characteristic, which is essentially a real asset wrapped in a corporate structure.

Franchise network effects. Strong but
competitive industry.

PE-style leverage reduces flexibility.

TIER 2 — MONOPOLY ASSET AT DISCOUNT | Score: 73/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
FactSet operates the financial data and analytics platform used by investment professionals globally, with a 95%
client retention rate that speaks to the depth of workflow integration and switching costs. But the real hidden asset
is FactSet's ownership of the CUSIP numbering system — the universal identifier for North American securities.
Every bond trade, every equity settlement, every derivative contract references a CUSIP number, and FactSet
(through its CUSIP Global Services subsidiary) is the sole authorized source of these identifiers. At $8.1 billion
market cap and 13.8x P/E, the stock trades 60% below its five-year median P/E, creating a rare opportunity to buy
a monopoly asset at a significant discount to historical norms.
The 95% retention rate is the financial manifestation of FactSet's moat. Financial analysts, portfolio managers,
and risk professionals build their entire workflow around FactSet's platform — custom screens, automated
reports, compliance monitoring, and portfolio analytics. Switching to a competitor (Bloomberg, Refinitiv, S&P;
Capital IQ) requires retraining entire teams, rebuilding custom analytics, and re-validating compliance processes.
This is not like switching from Pepsi to Coke; it is like switching from Windows to Linux — technically possible
but operationally agonizing. The retention rate has remained at or above 95% for years, proving that this
switching cost is durable even through periods of financial industry cost-cutting.
The material weakness disclosure (IT controls, confirmed not fraud-related) is the headline reason for the selloff
and the source of the 60% discount to the five-year median P/E. Material weakness disclosures terrify institutional
investors who cannot risk holding a position that may have unreliable financials, triggering mechanical selling
that has nothing to do with the underlying business quality. The company has stated the issue involves IT general
controls — access management, change management — not financial misstatement. This is fixable within one to
two audit cycles, and the remediation itself is a catalyst: once the material weakness is resolved, the institutional
selling pressure reverses and the multiple re-expands toward historical norms.

-- 58 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 59
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The CUSIP monopoly is the most underappreciated asset in FactSet's portfolio. CUSIP numbers are not optional
— they are required for regulatory reporting, trade settlement, and portfolio accounting across the entire North
American financial system. There is no alternative identifier with equivalent regulatory acceptance and market
adoption. FactSet acquired CUSIP Global Services from S&P; Global as part of a regulatory remedy (the
S&P-IHS; Markit merger required divestiture), and it now sits inside a company trading at 60% below its
historical multiple. The material weakness discount is creating a temporary window to buy a permanent monopoly
at a price that will likely not recur once the IT control remediation is complete.

TIER 1 — COMPOUNDING MACHINE | Score: 74/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Fairfax Financial is the Canadian insurance and investment conglomerate run by Prem Watsa, often called the
"Warren Buffett of Canada," who has compounded book value per share at an 18.7% CAGR over 40 years — a
track record that places him among the most successful capital allocators in the history of public markets. At
approximately $36.5 billion market cap and 8.2x P/E, the company just reported record earnings of $4.8 billion
while managing a $40.8 billion insurance float that generates investment income funding further acquisitions and
equity investments. This is a compounding machine operating at the peak of its earning power, available at a
single-digit earnings multiple because it is listed in Canada and structured in a way that confuses traditional
screens.
The $40.8 billion float is the engine of Fairfax's compounding. Like Berkshire Hathaway, Fairfax collects
insurance premiums today and invests them for years before claims are paid — generating investment returns on
other people's money at zero cost. With 18% ROE and record earnings, the float is being deployed at returns well
above the cost of equity, meaning every dollar of float creates incremental shareholder value. The record $4.8
billion earnings demonstrate that Watsa's investment portfolio (which includes significant equity positions, fixed
income, and private investments across multiple geographies) is performing at a level that justifies the
conglomerate structure.
The Canadian listing is both a risk and an opportunity. Fairfax trades on the Toronto Stock Exchange, which
means it is excluded from most US institutional mandates, US index funds, and US small-cap screens. This
structural exclusion creates a permanent valuation discount relative to what an identical business listed on the
NYSE would command. At 8.2x earnings and 18% ROE, Fairfax would likely trade at 12-15x earnings if it were
a US-listed company — the Canadian listing discount alone represents 30-45% potential upside. The bear case is
conglomerate complexity: Fairfax's investment portfolio is opaque, the insurance subsidiaries span multiple
jurisdictions, and Watsa's investment style (concentrated, contrarian, sometimes early) can produce volatile

-- 61 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 62
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
results in individual quarters.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 18.7% BVPS CAGR over 40 years is the single most important data point, and it does not appear on any
standard financial screen. This track record encompasses multiple interest rate cycles, insurance hard and soft
markets, financial crises, and investment booms and busts — and through all of them, Watsa has compounded
book value at nearly 19% annually. The $40.8 billion float is essentially a zero-cost leverage facility that no bank
would provide at this scale and cost. Fairfax also holds significant positions in Indian financial services (through
ICICI Lombard and other entities) that provide emerging market growth exposure within a developed-market
insurance wrapper — a structural advantage that is invisible to screens focused on Canadian or US-listed equities.

TIER 4 — CLOSED-END FUND ARBITRAGE | Score: 71/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
The Mexico Fund is a closed-end fund trading at a 15% discount to its net asset value, which consists of a
portfolio of liquid, publicly traded Mexican equities that can be independently verified. At approximately $400
million market cap with a 7.2% dividend yield, this is not a speculative bet on opaque assets — the underlying
holdings are large-cap and mid-cap Mexican stocks whose prices are publicly quoted on the Bolsa Mexicana de
Valores. The 15% discount means you are buying a dollar of Mexican equities for 85 cents, with a 7.2% yield
paid while you wait for the discount to narrow.
The activist catalyst is Saba Capital, which has accumulated an 8.35% position and has a well-documented track
record of agitating closed-end funds to narrow their discounts — typically through tender offers, share buybacks,
or conversion to open-end structures. Saba's involvement transforms this from a passive discount capture to an
active catalyst situation with a defined playbook. Closed-end fund activism is one of the most reliable arbitrage
strategies in public markets because the discount to NAV is mathematically verifiable and the mechanisms to
close it (tender offers, liquidation, open-ending) are well-established legal and financial tools.
The Mexico macro thesis is the secondary consideration but adds to the opportunity. Mexico is benefiting from
nearshoring trends as US companies diversify supply chains away from China, and the Mexican economy has
structural tailwinds from demographic dividends, USMCA trade integration, and infrastructure investment. At a
15% discount to NAV with a 7.2% yield and an activist catalyst, you are getting paid to hold Mexican equity
exposure at a discount during a period when the fundamental case for Mexican equities is arguably strengthening.
The risk is primarily currency (MXN/USD) and Mexican equity market risk — but since you are buying at 85
cents on the dollar, you have a 15% cushion before experiencing any actual loss of invested capital.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

-- 63 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 64
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
The verifiability of the NAV is what distinguishes MXF from other discount-to-NAV situations. Some closed-end
funds invest in illiquid private assets where the NAV is an estimate — MXF's NAV is calculated from the closing
prices of publicly traded Mexican stocks, meaning the discount is mathematically precise, not estimated. Saba
Capital's 8.35% stake and activist track record create a ticking clock that standard screens cannot detect. Saba has
successfully campaigned at dozens of closed-end funds, typically forcing board actions within 12-18 months of
building a significant stake. The combination of verifiable discount + activist catalyst + yield carry makes this one
of the lowest-risk arbitrage situations in the current market.

concerns. NAV is verifiable.

NAV. Mathematical margin of safety.

TIER 5 — SPECIAL SITUATION | Score: 67/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
AstroNova is a $60 million market cap company that manufactures specialty printers for aerospace (cockpit
printers 	for 	flight 	data 	recording) 	and 	industrial 	applications 	(product 	labeling, 	packaging, 	and
test-and-measurement printing). The company has formally announced that it is exploring strategic alternatives,
which in small-cap corporate finance is the polished euphemism for "we are looking for a buyer." At $60 million,
this is so far below the radar of institutional investors, sell-side analysts, and even most small-cap funds that the
announcement has generated virtually no market attention — which is precisely why the opportunity exists.
The strategic alternatives process is the entire thesis. When a $60 million company with real revenue, real
products, and a defensible niche in aerospace printing announces it is for sale, the probability of an acquisition at
a premium to the current market price is significant. Private equity firms, strategic acquirers in the aerospace
electronics space, and industrial conglomerates that specialize in roll-up strategies all represent potential buyers.
The aerospace cockpit printer business is particularly attractive because it is embedded in aircraft platforms
through FAA certification — once AstroNova's printer is designed into a cockpit, it remains there for the life of
the aircraft, creating an aftermarket parts and service stream that a buyer would value highly.
The math on a micro-cap acquisition is straightforward: any reasonable acquisition premium (20-40%) applied to
a $60 million base generates outsized percentage returns on a small absolute investment. A 30% premium would
mean $78 million enterprise value — a rounding error for most aerospace acquirers but a meaningful return for
investors who established positions before the announcement gained attention. The risk is that the strategic
alternatives process yields no acceptable offer and the company remains independent at the current valuation —
but even in that scenario, you own a profitable niche manufacturer with aerospace certification revenue at a
micro-cap valuation. The downside case is "business as usual at $60M," while the upside case is an acquisition
premium that could be 20-50%+ above current levels.

-- 65 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — April 2026 	Page 66
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL 	CONFIDENTIAL
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The FAA certification embedded in AstroNova's aerospace products creates asset value that does not appear on
the balance sheet. Cockpit printers installed in commercial and military aircraft are certified as part of the overall
avionics suite — replacing them requires recertification of the entire system, which costs millions and takes years.
This means AstroNova's installed base generates a locked-in aftermarket revenue stream (replacement parts, ink,
maintenance) that a potential acquirer would value using a recurring revenue multiple, not a cyclical manufacturer
multiple. The formal exploration of strategic alternatives at this market cap suggests the board believes the public
market is significantly undervaluing these assets — and at $60 million, they are almost certainly correct.

EXTENDED WATCHLIST | Sector: Consumer / Publishing
SNAPSHOT
Scholastic Corporation surfaced from the stage-1 quantitative filter on the basis of its return-on-capital,
balance-sheet, and revenue-trend profile. The 5-year chart and live financial snapshot above provide the starting
point. The items below are the open questions a full write-up would need to answer.
OPEN QUESTIONS
• What is the durable moat? (Switching costs, regulatory, scale, brand?)
• How is capital being allocated — buybacks, dividends, M&A;, or reinvestment?
• What is the bear case from the most recent 10-K risk factors?
• What is the catalyst, if any, and on what timeline?

1. IMPP — Imperial Petroleum Inc.
Market Cap
$158.3M

$179.1M

$0.00

-$12.7M

$161.0M

$46.6M

P/E (TTM)
3.4x
EV/EBITDA
-0.2x
Gross Margin
44.8%
Dividend Yield
—
CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 53/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Imperial Petroleum is a Marshall Islands–incorporated tanker and drybulk owner spun off from StealthGas in
December 2021. Per the 2024 20-F, the on-the-water fleet at year-end was twelve vessels: seven MR product
tankers, two suezmaxes, and three handysize drybulk carriers, generating voyage revenues of $147.5 million in
2024 (down from $183.7 million in 2023 as product-tanker rates normalized). Net income was $50.2 million in
2024 versus $71.1 million in 2023. The headline reason it screens cheap: the company carries $206.7 million of
cash and time deposits at 12/31/2024 against essentially zero bank debt, while the equity market cap is roughly
$158 million — the cash alone exceeds market cap, and the fleet is essentially being thrown in for free.
Subsequent 2025 disclosures expanded the fleet to 19 vessels averaging in Q4 with a 26-vessel target, funded out
of internal cash.
Why is it cheap? Three issues stack up. First, governance: Chairman/CEO Harry Vafias controls 49.99% of voting
power via Series B preferred and pays his family-owned Stealth Maritime Corporation S.A. for technical,
commercial, and crewing management — virtually every dollar of opex flows through related parties. Second,
dilution scars: IMPP did multiple equity offerings and warrant issuances in 2022–2024 plus a 1-for-15 reverse
split in April 2023, leaving long-term holders torched. Third, fleet age and cyclicality: average tanker age sits
above the global average; product-tanker spot rates have rolled off the 2023 highs as Russia-related dislocations
partially normalize. Common shareholders also receive zero dividend — only the 8.75% Series A preferred is
paid.
The bull case is straightforward asset arbitrage: cash of $206.7M plus a fleet whose stated book value (and likely
fair-market value) exceeds another $200M, on a $158M cap. Capital allocation has finally turned
shareholder-friendly: a $10M common buyback authorized in early 2024 was nearly exhausted by April 2025
($8.4M repurchased, plus $1.5M of warrant repurchases). Recent 2025 announcements show fleet growth funded
out of cash without re-issuing equity — a meaningful tone change. To close the gap an investor needs (a) Vafias

-- 118 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 4
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
to keep buying back stock instead of issuing more, (b) tanker rates to hold above breakeven through 2026, and (c)
no value-destroying related-party newbuild orders. Net asset coverage gives a hard floor most of the way down.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Series B preferred is the entire governance story. Issued to Vafias-controlled Flawless Management Inc. on
October 21, 2022, it carries no economic rights — par $0.01, no dividend, no liquidation premium beyond par —
but each share votes with the common. The structure mathematically locks the CEO at exactly 49.99% of voting
power regardless of how much common is issued or repurchased. That means even a successful asset-arbitrage
thesis cannot produce a hostile bid, a board flip, or a forced wind-down distribution. Holders are entirely
dependent on Vafias's discretion to keep returning cash. Combined with the related-party management contract at
Stealth Maritime, every accretive action also enriches the family vehicle — a structure that explains why the
discount persists despite the cash pile.
MUNGER CHECKLIST BREAKDOWN

roster — easy to model.
Moat 	2 	None; spot tankers are pure commodity
capacity.

2. CAAS — China Automotive Systems, Inc.
Market Cap
$129.4M

VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 47/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
China Automotive Systems is a Hubei-headquartered tier-1 supplier of power-steering components and
increasingly electronic power steering ("EPS") systems, operating through sixteen joint ventures and wholly
owned subsidiaries via its Genesis holding company. Per the FY2024 10-K, net product sales reached $650.9
million, up 12.9% year-over-year, with the Henglong segment alone contributing $325.9 million (+20.0%). Net
sales to China were $686.3 million versus $239.1 million to foreign markets. The company supplies most major
Chinese OEMs — including Geely, Chery, Changan, SAIC, and FAW — and has shipped to Stellantis since 2009
and Ford since 2016. Cash and short-term investments totaled $84.5 million at year-end (down from $125.7M),
with working capital of $146.2 million. At a $129M market cap, that's a P/B near 0.34 against a profitable,
growing, EV-exposed steering franchise.
Why is it cheap? The discount is almost entirely about jurisdiction and structure rather than operations. The five
largest customers account for 56.9% of sales — heavy concentration in price-pressured Chinese OEMs that
aggressively negotiate annual price-downs. Subsidiary cash is trapped: PRC subsidiaries must allocate 10% of
after-tax profit to a statutory reserve fund until it reaches 50% of registered capital, and dividend repatriation to
U.S. holders requires SAFE approval and withholding tax. The Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act
creates ongoing delisting risk if PCAOB inspection access is revoked. In September 2025 the company completed
a redomiciliation merger to the Cayman Islands (new CIK 0002037400), partially restructuring the holding
architecture but not removing the PRC operating-subsidiary cash-trap problem. Receivables aging in China
auto-supply is notoriously stretched.
The bull case rests on a real growth franchise hidden inside the discount. The R&D; Testing Center is a Hubei
provincial-level technical center developing intelligent and high-torque eRCB steering motors targeted at the EV
segment (mass production scheduled late 2025). Margins held up despite RMB price pressure because of mix shift
toward EPS. With $84.5M cash + working capital of $146M against zero net debt, balance-sheet collapse is

-- 120 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 6
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
implausible. The redomiciliation removes one technical overhang and arguably positions for a future
capital-return policy (the company has paid no regular cash dividend since the 2019 buyback program). To close
the gap a holder needs (a) Beijing to refrain from delist-trigger sanctions, (b) at least one meaningful
capital-return signal post-Cayman migration, and (c) Chinese OEM end-market demand to remain stable through
2026 EV oversupply.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The minority-interest line is enormous and distorts the headline P/B. CAAS owns sixteen separate joint ventures
and subsidiaries, several of them only majority-owned (notably the Henglong-KYB JV and other technical
partnerships). A material slice of book equity belongs to local Chinese partners — and their dividend rights take
priority on a per-entity basis before any cash can be upstreamed to U.S. holders. Combine that with the PRC
statutory reserve requirement and the SAFE approval gate, and the "tangible book per ADS" you see on a screen
overstates the cash that could ever realistically be distributed to a New York shareholder. The redomiciliation to
the Cayman Islands does not change this: the operating subsidiaries remain PRC entities subject to SAFE
controls. This is the single biggest gap between screen value and recoverable value.
