BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 60/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Dynagas LNG Partners is a Marshall Islands MLP that owns six ice-class LNG carriers, all on multi-year time
charters to investment-grade-tier counterparties. Per the FY2025 20-F (filed April 2026), 2025 net income was
$61.6 million on adjusted EBITDA of $109.2 million; charters are with SEFE Marketing & Trading (the German
entity formerly Gazprom Marketing & Trading), Equinor, Yamal Trade, and Rio Grande LNG. The Arctic Aurora
Equinor charter expires September 2026, with a follow-on seven-year charter already contracted. Three vessels
serve the Yamal LNG project on long-tenor charters extending into 2028. Cash distributions resumed at modest
levels — common $0.05/quarter (paid Feb 2026) and the 42nd sequential Series A 8.75% preferred distribution of
$0.5625/unit. With a $145M equity cap against book value of roughly $400M+ and a young, fully contracted
fleet, the partnership trades at 0.36x book and roughly 2.9x earnings.
Why is it cheap? Three structural overhangs. First, charterer concentration: SEFE/Yamal exposure means a
Russian-related-flag risk premium even though SEFE itself was nationalized by Germany and Yamal cargoes
flow legally. Second, MLP structure with a controlling general partner held by the Prokopiou family ("Sponsor")
— minority unitholders have no governance levers, the GP receives incentive distribution rights, and the Series A
preferreds sit ahead of common in the cash waterfall. Third, refinancing/maturity risk: the senior credit facility
was extensively refinanced in 2025 (interest expense recognized on a sponsor facility from June 27 to July 25,
2025), and unitholders bear the credit-spread mark on the post-refi term loan. Fourth-tier worry: U.S.-China
port-fee retaliation suspended through November 2026 but unresolved, potentially affecting LNG carrier port
economics.
The bull case is "fully contracted asset at half book." All six vessels carry forward charter cover into 2026–2028,
generating predictable cash. The newly extended $10M common buyback (through November 2026) signals
capital-return intent — 148,933 units repurchased in Q4 2025 alone — and the common distribution though tiny
is sequentially restored. LNG shipping rates remain structurally supported by the global LNG export build-out

-- 122 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 8
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
(Plaquemines, Rio Grande, Corpus Christi Stage III all coming online). Ice-class capability is a moat for Arctic
loading windows. To close the gap an investor needs (a) the Arctic Aurora to roll cleanly to its contracted Equinor
seven-year follow-on, (b) no defaults or sanctions hit on Yamal-trade counterparties, and (c) common distribution
to keep stepping back up. Even at current $0.20 annualized common distribution the yield exceeds 5%, with
significant room to grow as the term loan amortizes.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 20-F discloses that the partnership relied on a Sponsor (Prokopiou-family) bridge facility between June 27
and July 25, 2025, recognizing $902.3 thousand of interest expense to the related party during a 28-day window
— a clear sign that the senior debt refinancing executed in mid-2025 had a tight closing. That kind of
related-party bridge is not a long-term financing; it tells you the public capital markets remained shut to DLNG
until the refi closed and that the GP family is the lender of last resort. Two read-throughs: (1) the GP has every
incentive to keep the partnership solvent and pay the preferreds because it's also a creditor, but (2) the same
family controls the GP, the manager, and now apparently the bridge financing — minority unitholders are riding
entirely on Prokopiou-family alignment, which has been historically conservative on common distributions but
has at least stopped issuing equity at the bottom.

VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 51/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
ACCO Brands is a portfolio of legacy office-products and consumer-brand businesses — Five Star, Mead,
Swingline, Quartet, GBC, Kensington, Leitz, Marbig, NOBO, Rapid, Rexel, Tilibra, plus the higher-growth
PowerA gaming-accessories label. Per the FY2025 10-K (filed March 2026), the company reported net sales of
$1,524.7 million (down 8.5% from $1,666.2M in 2024), a 32.8% gross margin, and net income of $41.3 million
($0.44/share) — a swing back to profit after the prior year's $101.6M net loss that was driven by goodwill
impairment charges. Two reportable segments: ACCO Brands Americas (~59% of sales) and ACCO Brands
International (~41%). Operating cash flow was $68.7M in 2025 (down from $148.2M in 2024 on working-capital
timing). At a $299M cap and 0.44x book, the screen flags it as cheap; FY2026 guidance calls for sales flat to +3%
with adjusted EPS of $0.84–$0.89 and adjusted FCF of $75–$85M.
Why is it cheap? The market is pricing secular decline in office and education products. Top-customer
concentration with Amazon, Walmart, and the big-box channel creates margin pressure as private-label and
direct-import alternatives proliferate. Total debt of $840.9M against trailing EBITDA leaves consolidated
leverage at ~4.1x at year-end 2025 — well above the 3.0x range investors typically want for this kind of mature
business, with a target of 3.7x–3.9x by year-end 2026. Brazilian operations (Tilibra) carry a long-running tax
assessment ($3.0M near-term plus ongoing dispute). Management has just consummated the EPOS acquisition,
which adds another integration burden ($7M of restructuring planned in 2026 for $15M targeted synergies). And
the Americas reporting unit took a goodwill impairment in Q4 2023 — a reminder that the carrying value of these
legacy brands is fragile.
The bull case is "self-help compounder at a low multiple." Management has delivered $35M of cost savings in
2025 and is on track for $100M cumulative by year-end 2026. Capital allocation is rational: prioritize debt
paydown, sustain a covered quarterly dividend, opportunistic M&A.; No debt maturities until 2029. The $467.5M
revolving credit facility ($164.6M drawn) plus ~$292M available capacity gives ample liquidity. FCF guide of

-- 124 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 10
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
$75–$85M against $299M cap is roughly 25%+ FCF yield. PowerA gaming accessories provide a credible growth
offset to declining school-and-office staples. To close the gap an investor needs (a) leverage to drift below 3.7x
without forcing a dividend cut, (b) PowerA mix to keep growing through 2026 console refresh cycles, and (c) the
EPOS integration to deliver the targeted synergies on schedule. A re-rating to 9–10x EPS would be a double.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K discloses Approximately 200 U.S. manufacturing/distribution employees covered by collective
bargaining agreements, plus government-mandated collective bargaining in multiple international jurisdictions —
a meaningful operational drag in any cost-rationalization program. More importantly, the supplemental
disclosures show $96.7M of operating-lease obligations and $116.9M of purchase obligations in addition to the
$840.9M of nominal debt, bringing total contractual obligations to roughly $1,268M. Against shareholders' equity
in the $675M zone (implied by a 0.44x P/B at ~$299M cap), the off-balance-sheet items add another ~30% to the
effective leverage that does not appear in the simple debt/equity ratio screens use. The restructuring-charge line
has been a recurring annual item, not a one-off — readers who normalize earnings by adding back restructuring
are likely overstating run-rate profitability by $20–30M per year.

CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 56/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Amplify Energy is a small upstream E&P; that, after a sweeping 2025 portfolio cleanup, owns just two core asset
packages: the Bairoil CO2-flood waterflood in Wyoming and the Beta field in federal waters offshore Southern
California (the asset behind the 2021 San Pedro Bay pipeline incident). Per the FY2025 10-K (filed March 2026),
the company divested its non-operated Eagle Ford position (July 2025), East Texas/North Louisiana assets
(December 2025), and Oklahoma assets (December 2025). Total revenues fell to $263.4M (from $294.7M)
reflecting both lower volumes and a $42.5M impairment charge. Net income was reported but cash flow was the
headline: divestiture proceeds of roughly $250M were used to retire all credit-facility borrowings, leaving the
company with no debt and approximately $61M of cash at year-end 2025. PV-10 of remaining proved reserves
was $376.4M; standardized measure $335.1M. At a $227M cap that's a 0.49x P/B and 5.3x earnings on a
now-debt-free, simplified portfolio.
Why is it cheap? Beta is the controversial asset. The 2021 oil spill from the San Pedro Bay pipeline (operated by
Amplify subsidiary San Pedro Bay Pipeline Company, ~17.5 miles) created multi-year litigation, ongoing
regulatory scrutiny from BSEE, and reputational baggage that depresses the multiple. A contemplated January
2025 merger with Juniper Capital portfolio companies (NPOG and COG, which would have added Rockies
acreage) was terminated mid-2025 — leaving the company without the diversified scale management originally
pursued. Bairoil is mature with declining production. Capital intensity is meaningful — 2026 guidance is
$45–$65M of capex, more than 95% concentrated at Beta on 5–8 D-Sand wells, against guided adjusted EBITDA
of only $20–$45M. Executive transition: former CEO Willsher transitioned to "Special Advisor" effective July
22, 2025.
The bull case is "debt-free single-asset levered call on Beta drilling success." With zero debt and $61M cash on a
$227M cap, downside is genuinely cushioned. The remaining proved reserves' standardized measure of $335M
plus cash exceeds market cap before crediting Beta drilling upside. Beta's D-Sand interval is highly economic at

-- 126 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 12
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
strip prices and the 2026 program is the first real capital cycle since the 2021 incident. Bairoil offers optionality
on CCUS / Section 45Q monetization — one of the few existing CO2-flood operators well-positioned for
tax-credit-backed expansion. To close the gap an investor needs (a) the 2026 Beta D-Sand wells to deliver
type-curve results, (b) no new Beta operational incidents, and (c) Section 45Q clarity to monetize Bairoil CO2
infrastructure. The terminated merger is in the rearview; capital allocation has visibly turned conservative and
asset-focused.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Beta pipeline incident liability machinery still runs in the background even though the cash settlements have
largely been booked. The 10-K discloses the BetaPipelineIncident as a tagged accounting line — meaning
ongoing accruals, indemnity coverage analysis, and regulatory-monitoring costs are still flowing through G&A.;
More substantively: 65% of total proved reserves are concentrated in (presumably) Beta — a level of single-asset
concentration that's unusual for a small-cap E&P; and is the direct consequence of the 2025 divestiture program.
Translation: a single regulatory event at Beta — a permit revocation, a BSEE production curtailment, a fresh leak
— would disproportionately impair the entire reserve report. Counterbalancing this, the divestitures eliminated
the corresponding G&A; and lease-operating overhead, so the residual cost structure is much more variable than
legacy Amplify. The G&A; line did spike to $52.1M in 2025 (from $35.9M in 2024), reflecting transaction costs,
executive separation, and merger-termination expenses — most of which should not recur in 2026.

ANALYST SUMMARY
Diana Shipping is a Marshall Islands-domiciled, Greek-managed dry bulk owner that operates 36 vessels in

around 4.1 million tons and a weighted-average fleet age of 12.3 years. The fleet spans Ultramax through
Newcastlemax classes and is chartered out on staggered time charters to counterparties including Cargill, Stone
Shipping, NYK and others. FY2025 time-charter revenues were $213.5M, down from $228.2M in 2024 and
$262.1M in 2023 as average TCE slipped to $15,454/day. Net income was $17.8M and net income to common
$12.1M after $5.8M of Series B preferred dividends. The company trades around 0.55x book on a $309M market
cap with $502M of common equity, fitting the textbook below-book shipping profile.
The bear case is the dry bulk cycle. Substantially all revenue comes from one charter market and the 10-K
candidly flags "cyclicality of the dry bulk shipping industry" and "volatility in charter hire rates" as the primary
structural risk. Costs are a problem too — interest expense and finance costs were $43.0M in 2025 against
$42.2M of operating income, meaning the equity earns very little once leverage is paid. Around 30% of operating
expenses and half of G&A; are denominated in Euros while revenue is in dollars, so a weaker dollar squeezes the
cost base. The 8.875% Series B Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred sits ahead of common holders and
will keep absorbing $5.8M annually regardless of common dividends, which were token at $0.04 per common
share in 2025.
The bull case is a Capesize-led tightening into 2026–2027 paired with disciplined capital return. Recent
disclosures show DSX has fixed a Capesize at $21,650/day for March 2026–April 2027 and locked in roughly
50% of 2026 revenue days at attractive rates, with implied $17,102/day on unfixed days using FFA references.
The early-2025 $23M tender at $2.00/share retired 11.4M shares, the $100M repurchase authorization remains
unused, and management put $0.04/share through the year while the Series B yields 8.875%. With NAV well
above market, every up-cycle vessel sale (gain on sale of vessels was $3.7M in 2025, $5.8M in 2024) directly

it embeds the equity-securities gain.

ANALYST SUMMARY
Seven Hills Realty Trust is an externally managed commercial-mortgage REIT whose entire portfolio is 24
floating-rate 	first-mortgage 	loans 	on 	transitional 	middle-market 	CRE 	properties, 	with 	aggregate 	loan

reserve were $676.9M, secured financing facilities totaled $487.7M and shareholders' equity was $328.7M
against 22.6M shares outstanding (book value ~$14.55). FY2025 net income was $15.4M ($1.00 per share) on
$26.9M of net interest income from loans plus $2.5M of REO income. The REIT distributed $1.26 per share in
2025 and recently set the Q1 2026 dividend at $0.28 ($1.12 annualized), a 22% step down following the
December 2025 rights offering.
The bear case is in the watch list. Of 24 loans, four (17% of portfolio amortized cost, $115.5M) are risk-rated 4 —
meaning higher than expected risk of loss — and three of those four are Office collateral in Dallas, Plano and
Carlsbad with 72%–78% LTVs and 2026 maturities. Office is also the second-largest property type at 19% of the
book. The CRE bridge lending book is by definition a "transitional CRE" book, which the 10-K defines as
properties undergoing redevelopment that may or may not stabilize on schedule. Fee leakage to RMR/Tremont
(base management $4.4M plus incentive $0.6M plus reimbursed shared services $2.0M = $7.0M, or ~24% of total
revenue) is a permanent drag relative to internally managed peers.
The bull case is the recapitalized balance sheet at a discount to book. The fully-backstopped December 2025
rights offering raised $61.4M in net proceeds at $8.65, lifted share count from 14.9M to 22.6M, and equipped
Tremont with capacity to deploy more than $200M of new loans into a market where competitors are wounded.
Cash on hand jumped from $70.8M to $123.5M. Adam Portnoy/ABP Trust and Tremont participated in the
offering, signaling sponsor alignment. With shares trading around $8.40 versus book of $14.55, you pay 0.58x
book for a portfolio yielding nearly 8% with $36.9M of unfunded commitments to redeploy as rates and credit

spreads grind.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
SEVN is small and concentrated to a degree the screen does not display: 24 loans means a single risk-4 office
defaulting at, say, 25% loss-given-default would consume roughly two years of net income at a stroke. The Dallas
Office loan ($46.8M / $44.2M funded, 72% LTV, August 2026 maturity) alone is 6.5% of the portfolio. The
CECL reserve at $8.8M (1.28% of loans) is thinner than peers carrying meaningful office exposure. The external
manager structure also has a real-money cost: the November 2025 RMR Group transaction shows the REIT
acquired loans from an affiliated transaction, demonstrating the related-party flow of paper between SEVN and
the manager. That dependency has historically helped origination volume but is a governance item the screen
would never flag.

HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 64/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Security National Financial is a Utah-based three-segment conglomerate: a life insurance and annuity carrier, a
residential mortgage originator (SecurityNational Mortgage, 85 retail offices in 25 states), and an irreplaceable
cemetery and mortuary footprint of 11 mortuaries plus 5 cemeteries in Utah, 1 cemetery in California, and 1
cemetery and 4 mortuaries in New Mexico. FY2025 consolidated revenues were $344.6M with net earnings of
$32.2M. By segment: Life Insurance generated $29.4M of segment net earnings on $208.3M of revenue, the
Cemetery/Mortuary segment generated $6.6M on $33.7M, and the Mortgage segment lost $3.9M on $110.4M of
revenue. Total assets were $1.56B and segment assets show the life insurance business carries $1.41B. Trading at
0.58x book and 7.7x earnings on a $251M market cap, the screen identifies this as a profitable Berkshire-mini
selling for a fraction of replacement.
The bear case begins with the mortgage segment, which lost money in 2025 and remains structurally exposed to
the residential origination cycle, with cost-to-fund mortgage loans of $6.6M and commissions of $42.4M against
thin secondary-market gains. The life insurance book is dominated by acquired blocks of business stretching back
to acquisitions like Capital Investors Life (1994), Civil Service Employees Life (1999), Acadian Life (2002), and
on through Kilpatrick Life and others — these are run-off books with concentrated state-regulator and
reserve-adequacy risk. Insurance-segment investments include exposure to commercial mortgage portfolios
across LTV and DSCR buckets, and the holding company carries cemetery perpetual care obligations that must be
funded from restricted assets. The dual-class share structure (Class A and Class C) and stock-dividend-only
payout policy mean common holders never receive cash.
The bull case is the cemetery moat plus optionality on the other two segments. Cemetery and mortuary revenue of
$33.7M and segment earnings of $6.6M imply ~20% segment margin — nothing else in the portfolio comes
close. Cemeteries are a finite-supply asset with multi-decade demand visibility and pre-need contract economics
that compound. Strip out the mortgage drag and the steady-state earnings power of life plus cemetery is roughly

-- 132 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 18
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
$36M, or ~7x adjusted earnings. Capital allocation has been disciplined: management runs an annual stock
dividend (5% scrip) rather than cash, builds book value, and the Bradley/insider ownership (Charles E. Bradley Jr.
as CEO context applies to CPSS, but SNFCA insider ownership at the Quist/Pinegar level is similarly aligned). At
0.58x book the market is pricing the mortgage segment at zero or worse, with the cemetery moat thrown in for
free.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The cemetery segment is the asymmetric kicker no screener captures. Inventory disclosure shows specific
developed-versus-undeveloped acreage at named properties — Memorial Estates' Lakeview, Mountain View and
Redwood cemeteries in the Salt Lake corridor, Holladay Memorial Park, Deseret Memorial's Lake Hills, plus the
New Mexico locations — and pre-need cemetery and funeral receivables are funded into trust through the
insurance investment portfolio. The "Cemetery Perpetual Care Obligation" is a regulated trust asset bucket that
backs the perpetual maintenance of these grounds and is invested in Treasuries and state/political subdivision
securities; this is real, segregated, hard-asset value that does not appear in any traditional valuation framework.
Together these holdings represent decades of pre-sold revenue against a single-digit-percent-margin headline
business — a free option the market gives away at this multiple.

VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 42/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Consumer Portfolio Services is a Las Vegas-headquartered indirect subprime auto lender that purchases retail
installment contracts from a national dealer network and funds the portfolio almost entirely through asset-backed
securitization — 107 term securitizations originated since 1994, with 19 still outstanding. FY2025 results: total
revenues of $434.5M (interest income $422.7M plus a $6.5M positive fair-value mark on receivables and $5.3M
of other income), against $232.0M of interest expense and a NEGATIVE $2.9M provision for credit losses (a
benefit), producing $19.3M of net income or $0.88 basic / $0.80 diluted EPS. The total managed portfolio reached
$3.78B against $3.86B in total assets and $309.5M of shareholders' equity (book value ~$14.20). The capital
stack carries $2.99B securitization trust debt, $324.9M warehouse, $143.0M residual financing and $29.0M
subordinated renewables. At 0.60x book and 10.6x earnings on a $187M market cap, this is a quintessential
subprime credit value play.
The bear case is the credit cycle and concentrations. Net charge-offs as a percentage of average managed portfolio
rose to 7.8% in 2025 from 7.6% in 2024 and 6.5% in 2023, and Q3 2025 standalone annualized charge-offs hit
8.01% with total delinquencies (including repossession inventory) at 13.96%. Total dollar delinquencies grew to
$447.0M (11.8% of $3.78B) from $422.8M, and amount-in-repossession reached $111.2M. The business runs on
continuous warehouse and securitization access — a single closed window would force balance-sheet shrinkage
and book-value erosion. The Compensation Committee itself notes the inherent conflict that growing the book by
underpricing risk is the easiest way to look good short-term. Customer concentration is geographic (Texas, Ohio,
California, Illinois are top-four dealer states) and macroeconomically tied to subprime employment.
The bull case is securitization franchise and steady earnings. CPSS reported its 14th consecutive profitable year
and just closed its 41st consecutive securitization where the senior tranche received triple-A ratings from at least
two agencies — that program access is a real competitive advantage few subprime peers retain. CEO Charles E.
Bradley Jr. has been at the helm since 1992 and his executive team carries deep options-aligned exposure

-- 134 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 20
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
(Bradley alone with options for 1.815M shares plus existing holdings). Capital return is meaningful: $8.7M of
stock repurchased in 2025 on top of $12.8M in 2024 and $20.3M in 2023 — roughly 4–10% of float per year
repurchased through the cycle. Book value grew from $228.4M (2022) to $309.5M (2025).
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The most important number on the income statement is the $2.9M negative provision for credit losses — meaning
CPSS released reserves in 2025. This is a function of the company's adoption of the fair-value election for
receivables originated post-January 1, 2018. For these contracts, CPSS recognizes interest income on a level-yield
basis using the originated IRR and "we do not record an expense for provision for credit losses on these
receivables because such credit losses are included in our computation of the appropriate level yield." Translation:
most of the portfolio runs through a fair-value lens that smooths credit costs prospectively into the yield, while
CECL-style provisions only apply to a shrinking legacy bucket. So when the screen reports 10.6x earnings, that
earnings stream is more sensitive than it looks to the assumed level yield holding up against the rising 7.8%
charge-off line — a small recalibration of fair-value marks could swing several quarters of EPS.

tranches is a real distribution moat.

MELTING ICE CUBE | Score: 43/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Thryv Holdings is the post-Chapter-11 successor to Dex Media — yes, the print Yellow Pages — that has spent
the last several years deliberately reinventing itself as a small-business SaaS platform. The company now reports
two segments: SaaS (its Thryv Platform CRM/marketing/payments suite plus Keap acquired October 2024) and
Marketing Services (legacy print and digital, which management explicitly plans to "exit by the end of 2028").
FY2025 revenue was $785.0M, down 4.7% — SaaS grew 34.2% to $461.0M while Marketing Services collapsed
32.6% to $324.0M. Net income was $0.3M (versus a $74.2M loss in 2024), Adjusted EBITDA was $151.8M, and
SaaS Adjusted EBITDA reportedly rose to $73.8M from $41.2M. Total stockholders' equity was $218.1M against
43.8M shares outstanding (book ~$4.97), with $688.6M of total assets. At 0.66x book, 329x reported P/E and a
$145M market cap, this is a classic melting-ice-cube wager on cash conversion.
The bear case is leverage on a sunsetting cash cow. Total debt sits at $253.5M ($210.9M Term Loan including the
related-party tranche plus $25.1M ABL plus current portion), with pension obligations of another $44.2M. The
Marketing Services segment is the only thing producing real EBITDA today, and it is contractually scheduled to
disappear by end-2028 — every quarter that runs the clock down compresses the cash base used to service the
term loan and to fund the SaaS pivot. Goodwill of $253.8M sits against $1.17B of accumulated impairment losses
and a $572.1M accumulated deficit, evidence of how value-destructive the prior decade was. Stock-based
compensation expense was $25.3M in 2025 (up from $24.1M in 2024) — roughly 17% of market cap annually in
dilution, eating any equity value the cash flows might create. The 2024 Keap acquisition was funded by issuing
5.7M shares at $76.8M in net proceeds, further diluting holders.
The bull case is straightforward: pay 0.66x book for a SaaS business that did $461M of revenue at $73.8M of
segment EBITDA (16% margin, scaling fast) and get the Marketing Services cash run-off as a debt-paydown
engine for free. Management says cash flows from Marketing Services will continue through 2030 even after the
2028 commercial exit, providing four-plus years of de-leveraging visibility. SaaS subscriber base now exceeds

-- 136 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 22
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
100,000 between Thryv and Keap. 2026 guidance calls for SaaS revenue $461M–$471M and SaaS Adjusted
EBITDA $70M–$75M, validating the growth-with-margin trajectory. If the SaaS segment alone is worth even a
modest 2x revenue multiple (~$900M+) by 2028, common equity could meaningfully re-rate even after debt
paydown.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Adjusted EBITDA bridge tells the real story. To get from $0.3M of GAAP net income to $151.8M of
Adjusted EBITDA, management adds back $25.3M of SBC, $28.2M of restructuring and integration expenses,
$8.8M of net periodic pension cost, $52.8M of D&A;, $34.8M of interest expense, $16.7M of tax, and others —
meaning roughly half of "Adjusted EBITDA" relies on add-backs that recur (SBC, pension, restructuring) or on
debt service (interest). The 329x P/E is correct as printed because GAAP earnings barely cleared zero, and a
$1.16B accumulated impairment line plus a $572M accumulated deficit makes clear that book value here is
largely a function of post-bankruptcy fresh-start accounting carried forward — not a fortress NAV. The
Marketing Services exit also embeds working-capital release (deferred costs, contract liabilities, unbilled
receivables — $152.7M of unbilled AR alone), which will be a one-time tailwind that could fund both
deleveraging and SaaS reinvestment over 2026–2028.

community bank serving Essex, Hunterdon, Hudson, Middlesex, Morris, Somerset, Union and Livingston
counties. The Bank was created via the September 19, 2023 mutual-to-stock conversion of Somerset Savings
Bank and the simultaneous acquisition of Regal Bancorp. At June 30, 2025 the company reported total assets of
$1.08 billion, deposits of $846.0 million, and total stockholders' equity of $193.8 million. The screen surfaces
SRBK at P/B 0.71 and P/E 32.7 on a $139M market cap — classic post-conversion thrift pricing where the
company sits on outsized capital but earns a depressed ROE while it deploys the IPO proceeds, integrates the
merger, and amortizes the core deposit intangible from the Regal deal. Net income recovered to $3.7M
($0.44/share) for FY2025 versus a $10.9M loss in FY2024.
The bear case is straightforward: a sub-scale NJ community bank with too much capital, integrating a merger of
equals while NIM gets squeezed by deposit competition. Management itself flags that with the additional capital
raised, "we expect our return on equity to be low, which may reduce our stock price." Loan concentration in CRE
and multi-family (typical NJ thrift mix) creates exposure if the local economy weakens — the 10-K notes a
"prolonged or more severe downturn in the local economy could result in significant increases in non-performing
loans." The IPO loaded the balance sheet with an expensive ESOP ($6.7M contra-equity) and a non-cash
charitable foundation contribution. Allowance for credit losses sits at only $5.4M against ~$800M of loans —
thin if CRE deteriorates. There is no organic growth catalyst beyond rate-cut driven NIM expansion.
The bull case rests on capital return mechanics. SRBK trades at ~71% of tangible book and management has been
aggressive with buybacks: a first 950,793-share program completed in August, then a second 886,137-share
authorization (~10% of outstanding) approved July 2025. A $0.05 quarterly dividend was initiated with the
December 2025 record. Standard mutual-to-stock thrift playbook says: trade for ~3 years post-conversion, then
become an acquisition target — second-step thrifts in NJ have routinely exited at 130-150% of book to a larger

-- 138 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 24
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
acquirer. With $193.8M of equity backing a $139M market cap and a clean credit book, downside is reasonably
bounded. The merger gave the bank meaningful commercial lending expertise it previously lacked, expanding
earnings potential as legacy lower-yielding investment securities reprice.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The reported $32.7 P/E dramatically understates run-rate earnings power because of the conversion's one-time
drag. The 10-K notes the bank realized a "pre-tax realized loss of approximately $4 [million]" on the sale of
lower-yielding investment securities to reposition the book, plus heavy core deposit intangible amortization from
the Regal acquisition still flowing through expenses. The ESOP also creates an ongoing GAAP compensation
expense as shares are released. CECL was only adopted on July 1, 2023 — meaning the FY2024/2025 reserve
build absorbed the day-one accounting hit AND the Regal acquired-loan PCD reserve, both compressing reported
earnings. Look at pre-provision pre-amortization earnings to see what the franchise can really earn once the
conversion noise washes through.

DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 53/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
MITT — formerly AG Mortgage Investment Trust, renamed TPG Mortgage Investment Trust effective December
26, 2025 — is an externally managed residential mortgage REIT focused primarily on residential whole loans,
particularly Non-QM, Home Equity Loans, and Non-Agency RMBS collateralized by these loan types. The 10-K
describes Arc Home, LLC, the company's residential mortgage loan originator in which MITT owns
approximately 66%, as a strategic earnings driver. The manager is AG REIT Management, LLC, "a
wholly-owned subsidiary of TPG (NASDAQ: TPG), a leading global alternative asset management firm." At
December 31, 2025 reported book value was $10.48 per share; total dividends declared in 2025 were $0.85 (a
13.3% increase YoY), and MITT raised its Q1 2026 quarterly common dividend to $0.24 from $0.23. Screen
prints P/B 0.73, P/E 8.7, MCap $248M.
The bear case is mortgage REIT 101 — and amplified by the externally-managed structure. Book value is
"marked-to-market" and exquisitely sensitive to mortgage spreads, prepayment speeds, and the yield curve. The
10-K explicitly flags risks from "changes in interest rates and the fair value of our assets, including negative
changes resulting in margin calls relating to the financing of our assets," changes in the yield curve, increased
default/delinquency rates with decreased recovery rates, and the inherent risk that hedging "may or may not
protect us from interest rate and credit risk volatility." The company also flags integration risk for the Western
Asset Mortgage Capital Corporation ("WMC") acquisition. External management means a fee leakage to TPG
that would otherwise accrue to common holders; preferred dividends ($19.2M in 2024) sit ahead of common.
The bull case is straightforward: a portfolio rotation story under TPG's well-capitalized parent. The new TPG
branding (and Q4 2025 earnings call) emphasized capital rotation toward higher-yielding home equity, exercising
call rights on legacy securitizations, and growing Arc Home's profitability. Trading at ~73% of book with an
~11-12% common dividend yield (recently raised), the discount overstates the credit risk on a portfolio that is
overwhelmingly senior secured first-lien residential mortgages. Total return in 2025 exceeded 42% including

-- 140 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 26
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
dividends and price appreciation. If TPG can both narrow the book-discount and grow EPS via Arc Home and
call-right rotation, the math compounds.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K reveals the under-the-hood Arc Home stake: an approximate 66% ownership in a residential originator
that is a related-party entity, meaning a meaningful chunk of MITT's run-rate "earnings power" is contingent on
origination volumes and gain-on-sale margins at a captive lender, not on mortgage REIT spread mechanics. This
both adds optionality (a lender that produces MITT's own paper at scale) and adds a layer of opaque
mark-to-market exposure: Arc Home values are subjective, can swing on origination conditions, and the 66%
stake means consolidation/equity-method accounting noise. A non-trivial portion of the headline $0.85 in
dividends is supported by Arc Home contribution; if origination volumes contract, the dividend math changes
quickly.

BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 58/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
FB Bancorp is the New Orleans-headquartered holding company for Fidelity Bank, a 1908-vintage Louisiana
community bank with 18 full-service branches across central and southern Louisiana, including its main office,
two drive-up branches and 14 stand-alone ATMs. The bank converted from mutual-to-stock form on October 22,
2024, completing its IPO on the NASDAQ at $10/share and raising $198.4 million from the sale of nearly 19.9
million shares. Stated strategy at IPO was to diversify the loan book, expand activity in Baton Rouge and
Lafayette, and pursue branch/bank M&A.; Screen prints P/B 0.74, P/E 63.5, MCap $192M — a textbook
freshly-converted thrift profile: equity inflated by IPO proceeds, ROE temporarily near-zero while management
deploys the cash. The 10-K describes the lending mix as commercial real estate, residential, and consumer, with
all the standard CECL allowance for credit losses methodology.
The bear case starts with the optically punitive 63.5x trailing P/E — that figure reflects an over-capitalized
post-IPO balance sheet earning low single-digit ROE while sitting on excess proceeds and amortizing
IPO/ESOP/foundation costs. Louisiana has structurally weaker demographics than higher-growth Sun Belt
markets; insurance market dislocation post-Ida and rising property reinsurance costs pressure CRE collateral
values. Management's own forward-looking statements highlight risks from "fluctuations in real estate values,"
demand for loans in the local market, and ability to access cost-effective funding. Hurricane and flood
concentration in Louisiana creates a tail risk that does not exist for, say, a Midwest community bank. There is no
Sun Belt growth tailwind to mask sub-scale operating leverage.
The bull case is the second-step conversion playbook with an above-average jurisdiction premium. On November
13, 2025 FB Bancorp announced a stock buyback authorizing repurchase of up to 1,983,750 shares (~10% of
outstanding), echoing the SRBK template — buy back stock at 0.74x book, return excess capital, then sell to a
regional acquirer in 2-4 years. The book value per share floor (~$13.50 implied at P/B 0.74 on a $10/share IPO) is
meaningful, the loan book is plain-vanilla community lending, and Fidelity Bank's $1.1B in assets and $769M in

deposits is the right size to be either the consolidator or the consolidated party in the gradually-shrinking Gulf
community bank ecosystem. Capital ratios should be very high; tangible book per share should grow as buybacks
shrink the denominator.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K notes that book value of land, buildings, leasehold improvements, furniture, fixtures and equipment was
$57 million — non-trivial real estate exposure for a $192M-market-cap holding company headquartered in
downtown New Orleans (Louisiana 70130 per the cover page). Several Fidelity branches are owned rather than
leased; in a community-bank acquisition, owned premises are typically liquidated post-deal, providing additional
asset-realization optionality not visible in tangible book. Equally important: the IPO's ~19.9M share issuance at
$10 means the entire current public float was created in October 2024 — selling pressure from IPO-allocated
thrift depositor accounts and arbitrage funds is still washing through, which partially explains the persistent
below-book trade. As the lockup-style technical pressure decays, the discount should naturally compress.

DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 60/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
NewLake Capital Partners is a triple-net REIT that owns 34 single-tenant cannabis properties — 15 cultivation
facilities and 19 dispensaries — leased to state-licensed cannabis operators on long-term, sale-leaseback
structures. The 10-K reports 91% of rental income from cultivation facilities and properties held in core
medical/limited-license states. The company is essentially the smaller, less-levered counterpart to IIPR with a
concentrated tenant roster: Curaleaf, Cresco Labs, Trulieve, PharmaCann, Ascend, Verano, and several mid-cap
MSOs comprise the rent roll. Screen prints P/B 0.76, P/E 11.4, MCap $300M. The dividend has been the marquee
feature: a double-digit yield (~12-13%) supported by an AFFO payout ratio reported in the high 70s to low 80s
percent range, on a near-zero-leverage balance sheet.
The bear case is the entire reason NLCP trades below book: federal cannabis prohibition, plus a tenant base
concentrated in MSOs whose own equity prices have collapsed. The 10-K explicitly states "tenant concentration
risk (and related risk of tenant defaults) may be more pronounced in the cannabis industry due to the fact that
some of our tenants have limited operating histories" and that "cannabis remains illegal under federal law." The
top three tenants (Curaleaf, Cresco Labs, Trulieve) reportedly represent ~50% of annualized rent — single-name
credit events meaningfully impair coverage. Rescheduling to Schedule III has been repeatedly delayed;
safe-harbor banking legislation has gone nowhere in Congress. Re-tenanting a vacant 100,000+ sq ft cannabis
cultivation facility in a non-adult-use state is non-trivial, and at least three properties (Massachusetts,
Pennsylvania, Nevada) appear vacant in the property table.
The bull case: triple-net REIT with negligible debt and ~13% yield, trading at 76% of book, where the worst-case
tenant defaults have largely already occurred or been priced in (Revolutionary Clinics and AYR Wellness
situations were managed without dividend cuts). The fortress balance sheet — net cash, no significant
credit-facility draws, no near-term debt maturities — eliminates the financial-leverage risk that has wrecked many
cannabis-adjacent companies. If federal reform happens at any point in the next 24 months (DEA rescheduling,

-- 144 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 30
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
SAFE Banking, or even softer 280E enforcement), MSO tenant credit improves materially, the discount-to-book
compresses, and current shareholders capture a 30-50% re-rating on top of the 13% yield. Even with no reform,
holding to collect 13% annually on book-discounted real estate with hard collateral is a defensible base case.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K's property tables — XBRL tags reveal entries like "Trulieve" in Pennsylvania, multiple "Curaleaf"
properties in Florida and Illinois, "Vacant1Member" in Nevada and "Vacant2Member" in Pennsylvania — show a
real, identifiable list of distressed re-tenanting situations. The unsecured loan to C3 Industries (originated June 10,
2022 per XBRL) is an off-the-radar credit exposure separate from rent — a working-capital-style loan to one of
NLCP's tenants, exactly the kind of related-tenant lending that converts a passive landlord into a credit investor.
Also worth noting: NLCP carries a CECL allowance for credit losses (adopted January 1, 2023) on its receivables
— a non-trivial accounting choice for a REIT and a leading indicator of how management views tenant
deterioration before headline cash rent collection numbers move.

CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 55/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Bassett Furniture is a 122-year-old vertically integrated home-furnishings company operating in two segments:
Wholesale (design, manufacturing and sourcing of upholstery, casegoods and the Lane Venture outdoor brand,
distributed through ~86 company- and licensee-owned BHF stores plus independent retailers) and Retail
(Company-owned BHF stores). Domestic upholstery is manufactured in Newton, North Carolina. At fiscal
year-end November 29, 2025, the balance sheet showed total assets of $323.8 million with cash and cash
equivalents and short-term investments totaling $59.2 million on hand. Net income was modest; FY2025 Q4 saw
consolidated net sales rise 5.1% to $88.7M with gross margin of 56.3%, operating income of $2.3M, and diluted
EPS of $0.18 (vs $0.38 prior-year quarter). Screen prints P/B 0.76, P/E 23.4, MCap $125M.
The bear case is a four-year furniture demand downcycle compounded by structural retail contraction.
Existing-home turnover is at multi-decade lows; new home-buyer cohorts that historically anchor furniture
purchases are deferred. The 10-K flags risks including "fluctuations in the cost and availability of raw materials,
fuel, labor, delivery costs and sourced products," tariff exposure on imported product, "overall retail traffic levels
in stores and on the internet and consumer demand for home furnishings," and "the profitability of the stores
(independent licensees and Company-owned retail stores) which may result in future store closings." Bassett also
acknowledges "the risk of additional asset impairment charges arising from the ongoing efforts to consolidate our
retail warehouses." Noa Home, the Canadian e-commerce experiment, was wound down after determining it "was
not likely to achieve profitability at any time in the foreseeable future."
The bull case: clean balance sheet, restructuring complete, and operating leverage as housing recovers. $59.2M
cash with no debt against a $125M market cap means roughly half of the equity is net cash. Restructuring actions
delivered ~$2.5M of annual savings and 11% headcount reduction; Virginia wood production facilities have been
consolidated. Q4 gross margin of 56.3% (versus prior softer quarters) and 190 bps of full-year gross margin
expansion show pricing/mix discipline. Casegoods sales up 50%+ and custom leather +19% in 2025 indicate

-- 146 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 32
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
product traction in higher-margin custom orders. Capital allocation: regular quarterly dividend (FY2025
dividends totaled $6.9M) plus stock repurchase activity. Real estate owned (book value of approximately $23.5M
flagged in the 10-K) provides additional embedded asset value.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K reveals a meaningful contingent obligation that doesn't show up in book multiples: lease obligations of
licensee operators. Bassett historically guarantees a portion of store leases for independent licensee BHF stores —
when a licensee fails, Bassett is on the hook for the remaining lease term. This is exactly the failure mode that
drove asset impairment and warehouse-consolidation charges referenced in the risk factors. Separately, the
segment disclosure shows Bassett Custom Upholstery generated $76.9M of external sales plus $59.4M
intercompany — the $59.4M of intercompany revenue is a tell that wholesale segment "external" earnings are
partially supported by transfer pricing into retail, where margin is captured. Investors looking at consolidated EPS
may underweight just how much of the operating income relies on the company-owned retail channel maintaining
its current 60-store footprint.

MELTING ICE CUBE | Score: 41/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
trivago operates a hotel meta-search platform that aggregates rates from online travel agencies (OTAs) and direct
hotel sites, monetizing on a cost-per-click (CPC) and cost-per-acquisition (CPA) basis. The company is
incorporated in the Netherlands, has its operating center in Düsseldorf, and trades in New York via American
Depositary Shares (each ADS represents five Class A shares). Expedia Group has been the controlling majority
shareholder since 2013 through Class B super-voting stock. The screen flagged TRVG at P/B 0.79, P/E 15.4 on a
$196M market cap. The 2025 20-F shows a real operational rebound: revenue ¤548.9M (vs. ¤460.8M in 2024,
+19%), net income ¤11.2M (vs. a ¤23.7M loss prior year), Adjusted EBITDA ¤15.8M, and cash & equivalents of
¤130.9M plus ¤11.9M short-term investments — roughly ¤143M in net cash with no funded debt of consequence.
The bear case is structural revenue concentration. Per the 20-F, "we continue to generate the great majority of our
revenue from our largest OTA advertisers, including brands affiliated with Booking Holdings… and Expedia
Group." Two parents (one of them the controlling shareholder) determine the bid environment, and either can
throttle CPC budgets at any moment. trivago has now alternated between profit and loss for several years, with
2024's net loss of ¤23.7M proving this is not a steady-state earner. Expedia's dual-class control means minority
ADS holders cannot vote out management, force a sale, change the dividend policy, or even compel buybacks.
Television-led marketing means costs are committed in advance; if advertiser CPCs drop mid-year, operating
leverage works in reverse. Google's hotel module continues to siphon top-of-funnel intent.
The bull case is that the rebound is real: 19% revenue growth, swing to GAAP profit, ¤143M of net cash on a
¤196M market cap implies the operating business trades for ~$50M, or roughly 3-4x trailing operating cash.
Management has been repurchasing shares opportunistically. Catalysts: continued Booking/Expedia ad-spend
recovery as OTA share wars intensify, possible take-private bid from Expedia at a modest premium (Expedia
would consolidate fully and remove a redundant public listing), and AI-search integrations that could re-rate
meta-search assets. Capital allocation has been disciplined — no major acquisitions, light cost base, balance sheet

-- 148 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 34
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
preserved. With 353M shares outstanding (115.6M Class A + 237.5M Class B), the float is small post-Expedia.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen sees P/B 0.79 and P/E 15.4 but misses three filing-level realities. First, the P/E is on a year that swung
from loss to profit; on the trailing two-year average, EPS is roughly break-even — multiple is meaningless.
Second, the dual-class structure: Class B shares carry super-voting rights and are held almost entirely by Expedia,
so corporate-action optionality belongs to one shareholder. Reserved-matter list in the 20-F includes share
repurchases — trivago cannot buy back stock without Expedia's blessing. Third, the related-party accounts
receivable line of ¤21.8M is from Expedia entities, meaning a meaningful share of working capital is collected
from the controlling parent. If Expedia ever shifts its bidding logic, trivago feels it in cash before P&L.; The
"majority shareholder is also a top customer" pattern is a governance red flag deserving a discount, not a
premium.

BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 58/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Tri-County Financial Group is the holding company for First State Bank, an 85-year-old north-central Illinois
community bank headquartered in Mendota with branches across Batavia, Bloomington, Champaign, Geneva,
LaMoille, McNabb, North Aurora, Ottawa, Peru, Princeton, Rochelle, Shabbona, St. Charles, Streator, Sycamore,
Waterman, and West Brooklyn — eighteen branches plus a residential-mortgage subsidiary (FSM) and an
insurance subsidiary (First State Insurance). The screen flagged TYFG at P/B 0.80 and P/E 9.3 on a $126M
market cap. The 2025 10-K shows ~$1.6B in total assets, return on average assets of 0.88% (vs. 0.68% in 2024),
return on average equity of 9.09% (vs. 7.45%), net interest margin of 3.40% (vs. 2.96%), allowance for credit
losses of 1.14% of loans, nonperforming loans of just 0.43% of loans, Tier 1 leverage capital ratio of 10.51%,
total risk-based capital of 14.91%, and stockholders' equity to total assets of 9.89%. With 2,376,998 shares
outstanding and a $43.50 reference price, this is a tiny, illiquid name.
The bear case is structural illiquidity and concentration. The shares trade on OTCQX, not a national exchange —
the 10-K explicitly notes "OTCQX… provides limited liquidity. Consequently, you may not be able to sell your
common stock at or above the price you paid." A 2.4M-share float means a single seller can move price 5-10% on
a normal day. The loan book is concentrated in north-central Illinois agriculture, commercial real estate, and
small-business lending — a regional recession or farm-belt commodity slump would hit credit quality fast.
Loan-to-deposit at 100.87% leaves no slack for funding stress. As a community bank, TYFG cannot match the
deposit pricing of larger competitors if rates spike again, and digital banks continue to skim younger Illinois
depositors. The 17.4% dividend payout ratio is conservative, but bank regulators can suspend dividends at any
time under capital-distribution rules referenced in the 10-K.
The bull case is straightforward small-bank value. Earning 9% on book at 0.80x book is mathematically a 11.3%
earnings yield with capital ratios well above regulatory well-capitalized thresholds. NIM expanded 44bp
year-over-year as legacy low-yield assets reprice; with another 12-18 months of asset reset, NIM could push

-- 150 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 36

toward 3.6-3.8%, lifting ROA toward 1%. Catalysts: continued rate-cycle benefit, possible NASDAQ uplisting
that would unlock institutional ownership, and ultimate consolidation in Illinois community banking — a buyer
would likely pay 1.2-1.5x book given ROE trajectory. Capital allocation discipline is evident in the conservative
payout, modest balance sheet leverage, and 85-year operating history without a forced recapitalization.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three filing-level details deserve weight. First, FSM (mortgage banking) gives TYFG fee income that is not
captured in the headline NIM picture but is highly cyclical with mortgage-rate volatility — 2026 rate cuts would
mean a refi tailwind. Second, the 10-K notes the bank has unused FHLB-Chicago borrowing capacity
collateralized at varying advance rates against the loan book (76% of book value of 1-4 family real estate, 62% of
HELOCs, etc.) — meaningful contingent liquidity not visible to a screen. Third, OTCQX listing is a self-imposed
constraint, not a regulatory one; management has stayed off NASDAQ to avoid Sarbanes-Oxley-class compliance
costs, but a future board could uplist. Tiny float means any institutional discovery is a re-rating catalyst. Insiders
likely own meaningful percentage of shares — the typical Illinois community-bank holdco pattern.

DISTRESSED ARB | Score: 62/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Chicago Atlantic Real Estate Finance is a commercial mortgage REIT that originates and holds senior secured
loans collateralized primarily by real estate, equipment, and licenses of state-licensed cannabis operators. The
company is externally managed by Chicago Atlantic REIT Manager and listed on NASDAQ. The screen flagged
REFI at P/B 0.81, P/E 7.1 on a $250M market cap. The 2025 10-K shows loans held for investment of $411.1M
outstanding principal with original-issue discount of $2.1M and current-expected-credit-loss reserve of $5.1M, for
a carrying value of $403.9M. Weighted-average maturity is 2.2 years, and the portfolio's yield-to-maturity is
16.3% on a weighted-average basis (range 9.7% to 26.9%) inclusive of coupon, OID, unused fees, exit fees, and
other yield-enhancing items. The current expected credit loss reserve increased ~$716K year-over-year, signaling
some incremental credit deterioration but not a portfolio break.
The bear case begins with one fact: cannabis remains illegal under U.S. federal law. The 10-K states bluntly that
"cannabis remains illegal under federal law," and a federal enforcement turn (or simply a delayed Schedule III
rescheduling) could collapse tenant economics across multi-state operators. State-level price compression in
mature markets like California, Massachusetts, Michigan, and Colorado has already squeezed wholesale margins,
raising borrower default risk. The 10-K notes that certain assets cannot be used as collateral or transferred due to
state cannabis-licensing restrictions — a material constraint on workout recovery. Loan concentration risk is real:
portfolio of ~30 loans means a single $25-30M position can move the dividend. External management means a
permanent fee leak (base + incentive) regardless of REFI shareholder outcomes. Banking-access risk persists
pending SAFE Banking, which has stalled in Congress for years.
The bull case is a 16.3% portfolio yield collected by a REIT trading at 0.81x book — even with conservative
underwriting, the spread between asset yields and the cost of equity is wide. Short 2.2-year duration means the
book repositions quickly if conditions worsen; the manager has done two rounds of new-vintage originations at
attractive coupons since rates rose. Catalysts include DEA Schedule III rescheduling (would re-rate the entire

-- 152 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 38
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
cannabis-adjacent capital structure), SAFE Banking passage (would lower borrower funding costs and improve
credit), Section 280E tax relief, and continued institutional starvation of the sector that lets REFI write loans
others cannot. Capital allocation: high payout ratio (REIT requirement), no acquisition appetite, and a clearly
defined "credit-first" underwriting process that has produced minimal realized losses through 2025.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Several filing-level points matter. First, REFI co-invests with affiliates of its external manager, including private
vehicles ("Other investment vehicles managed by our Manager or affiliates… may co-invest with us or hold
positions in a loan that we hold"). Co-investment can be a feature (deal flow access) or a problem (allocation
conflict), and the 10-K's allocation policy is what to read carefully. Second, REFI generally will not take a
position junior to a Manager-affiliate position — a meaningful protection. Third, "Capitalized PIK Interest" of
$6.2M flowed through the loan-balance roll-forward in 2025: a portion of stated income is non-cash, which
inflates GAAP NII relative to distributable cash. Fourth, the 16.3% IRR is a portfolio-level WAVG including OID
and exit fees that may not be realized if loans repay early or default. Fifth, REFI is not a bank — it cannot access
the Fed's discount window if cannabis credit spreads blow out.

ANALYST SUMMARY
RCI Hospitality is a Texas-domiciled holding company that operates two principal segments: Nightclubs
(adult-entertainment clubs under brands such as Rick's Cabaret, Jaguars Club, Tootsie's Cabaret, XTC Cabaret,
Club Onyx, and Hoops Cabaret) totaling 59 club locations, plus 11 Bombshells Restaurant & Bar units — a
military-themed casual-dining concept. The screen flagged RICK at P/B 0.82, P/E 20.0 on a $215M market cap.
The fiscal-2025 (year ended September 30, 2025) 10-K shows Nightclubs segment revenue down ~0.6% with
income from operations up 20.1% (same-store sales -2.1%), while the Bombshells segment posted revenue down
29.2% but income from operations swinging to a $177K profit from a $10.8M loss prior year (same-store sales
-13.6%). Total long-term debt is $214.6M. Management spent $11.9M on treasury-stock purchases and $2.5M on
dividends in FY2025, paid down $20.5M of debt, and explicitly targets fiscal 2029 results of $400M revenue,
$75M free cash flow, and 7.5M shares outstanding.
The bear case is governance turbulence on top of operational drag. The 10-K discloses that long-time CEO Eric
Langan and CFO Bradley Chhay "subsequently stepped down from those positions in November 2025" amid a
New York lawsuit naming both individuals and the company. Langan was the architect of the buyback machine
and the operating philosophy; his departure is a material change that the headline screen does not capture.
Bombshells has been a serial value-destroyer with cumulative impairments referenced in the 10-K (including
$38.5M in 2024 across goodwill, SOB-license, property, ROU, and tradename impairments). Adult-entertainment
regulation, local zoning fights, and labor-classification litigation (independent-contractor entertainer disputes)
remain perpetual tail risks. The 86% drop in net income from 2022's record to 2025 indicates the business is well
off peak. Continuing NASDAQ listing requires ongoing compliance — already flagged as a risk in the filing.
The bull case rests on RCI's structural cash-generation profile. Operating cash declined only 23% from the 2022
peak versus a 77% drop in net income — that gap is GAAP impairments rather than real economic harm. The
company aggressively closed underperforming Bombshells, which improved the segment from a $10.8M

-- 154 of 771 --

operating loss to break-even. Buybacks of $11.9M plus debt paydown of $20.5M plus dividends totaled $34.8M
of capital returned and de-levered in a single year — significant for a $215M cap company. Catalysts include
continued Bombshells stabilization, opportunistic club M&A; at 3-4x EBITDA (the Langan playbook), buyback
acceleration if the multiple stays compressed, and a refreshed management team that may or may not maintain the
same capital-allocation discipline. Insider ownership remains meaningful.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The most important missed item is the November 2025 management departure — Langan was the buyback
machine, and a screen looking at trailing capital-return data is implicitly underwriting his continued tenure. A
second item: the 10-K notes that certain commercial bank debts "are guaranteed by the Company's former CEO"
— Langan's personal guarantee may need replacement now that he has stepped down, potentially triggering
refinancing at higher rates or covenant changes. Third, the SOB (Sexually Oriented Business) license is a state
and local asset that has historical impairment exposure but also represents real scarcity value; new licenses are
nearly impossible to obtain in major markets, so existing operators have a regulatory moat. Fourth, the fiscal-2029
target of 7.5M shares outstanding (from current ~9M) implies a 17% share-count reduction over four years —
credible only if cash generation holds and the new CEO maintains the buyback cadence. Texas-domicile means
director and officer indemnification is broad.

CYCLICAL TROUGH | Score: 49/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Motorcar Parts of America is a remanufacturer and supplier of aftermarket automotive hard parts — alternators,
starters, brake calipers, brake master cylinders, brake boosters, turbochargers, wheel hubs, and rotating-electrical
components — sold primarily into the U.S. retail aftermarket through the largest national chains. The fiscal-2025
(year ended March 31, 2025) 10-K confirms the customer roster: Advance Auto Parts, AutoZone, Genuine Parts
(NAPA), and O'Reilly Auto Parts, distributing through approximately 26,000 retail outlets. The screen flagged
MPAA at P/B 0.83, P/E 124.8 on a $220M market cap (the elevated P/E reflects depressed trailing earnings, not
premium quality). Fiscal 2025 highlights from the 10-K: net sales increased 5.5% to a record $757.4M, gross
profit increased 16.1% to $153.8M, and gross margin expanded 1.8 percentage points to 20.3%. Share count is
19,435,706 as of June 2, 2025. The company operates in the U.S., Mexico (~5,000 employees, primarily
production), Canada, Malaysia, and China, with ~5,300 production employees in total and ~4,900 Mexican
production workers covered by a local union.
The bear case is severe customer concentration combined with persistent margin pressure. The 10-K states: "Sales
to our three largest customers in the aggregate represented 86%, and sales to our largest customer represented
39% of our net sales during fiscal 2025." That is monopsony-grade exposure. The same disclosure flags ongoing
pressure from major customers to "offer lower prices," extend payment terms, and accept core-return adjustments
— structural margin headwinds that recur every renewal. MPAA's business model requires carrying massive
inventories of "Used Cores" plus remanufactured stock, making working capital chronically heavy and
inventory-days extended. Recent fiscal years saw GAAP net losses with diluted EPS reported at -$0.99, -$2.51,
and -$0.22 across fiscal 2025/2024/2023 (potential common shares anti-dilutive). Tariff exposure on Chinese
components and Mexican production is real and rising under shifting trade policy. Foreign currency exposure
(peso, ringgit, RMB) adds non-operating volatility.

-- 156 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 42
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
The bull case is operational inflection: revenue at a record $757M, gross margin recovery to 20.3% (up 1.8pp),
and gross profit up 16% — all evidence the working-capital reset and price actions are flowing through. The
aftermarket parts category benefits from rising vehicle age, deferred new-car purchases, and post-warranty repair
demand — all secular tailwinds independent of new-vehicle SAAR. Catalysts include continued margin
restoration (a return to mid-teens operating margin would translate to $50-70M operating income on $760M
sales), normalization of inventory financing costs as rates recede, and potential strategic interest from larger
industrial roll-ups. Capital allocation: the 10-K discloses a modest share-repurchase program with remaining
authorization at year-end, plus debt paydown as cash generation improves. With only 8 holders of record listed
(likely most shares in street name), institutional concentration is meaningful.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Several filing-level points reshape the thesis. First, the trailing P/E is misleading because GAAP losses include
large non-cash items (FX revaluation, core-accounting adjustments, mark-to-market on supply-chain financing).
EBITDA and operating cash flow have been positive while GAAP net income was negative — the
working-capital cycle drives the disconnect. Second, MPAA uses extensive non-recourse accounts-receivable
factoring with its three large customers; this is a hidden financing line that is not "debt" on a screen but functions
like one and re-prices when SOFR moves. Third, the 39%-of-revenue largest customer is not named in the public
filing, but trade press strongly suggests AutoZone — losing or even renegotiating that single program would be
company-defining. Fourth, the company invested in diagnostic and EV-component capabilities (e.g., test
equipment for hybrid/EV motors and inverters) that gives it a foothold as drivetrain technology shifts; this
optionality is invisible on a P/B screen.

VALUE TRAP RISK | Score: 48/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
UTG, Inc. is a holding company whose operating subsidiary, Universe Life Insurance Group (UG), administers a
closed and run-off block of traditional life insurance policies headquartered in Stanford, Kentucky and
Springfield, Illinois. Total assets were $491.4 million at year-end 2025, with shareholders' equity of $232.7
million producing book value near $73.85 per share against ~3.15 million shares outstanding. The franchise is
unusual for a life insurer: rather than holding a typical fixed-income-heavy portfolio, UG concentrates its
investment book in equity securities (62% of total investments) and limited liability companies that own real
estate, mortgages, and oil & gas royalties. This means GAAP "earnings" depend heavily on realized investment
gains, which collapsed from $63.3 million in 2024 to $23.4 million in 2025, dragging EPS from $15.80 to $5.42.
The bear case is structural: UTGN is effectively a private investment vehicle controlled by majority shareholder
Jesse T. Correll and his First Southern entities, with related-party arrangements throughout the 10-K. The
insurance liabilities are decaying — premiums and policy fees were just $7.3 million — so the equity is
functionally a leveraged bet on a Correll-managed concentrated equity, real estate, and royalty portfolio. Public
minorities have no real influence, governance is insider-friendly, and the auditor (Kerber, Eck & Braeckel) is a
regional firm. Reported earnings are lumpy, hard to model, and largely a function of marks on illiquid LLC
investments. The 2024 results were "(as restated)," indicating prior-period accounting issues. P/B ~0.84 looks
cheap, but the discount to book has persisted for years.
The bull case rests on Correll's long-term track record allocating insurance float into hard assets and on the
optionality of the oil & gas royalties (now 30% of investment real estate, up from 20%). If you believe in Correll
the way Berkshire shareholders believe in Buffett, UTGN is a private compounder trading at 0.84x reported book,
with thinly-traded shares, modest dividends, and a buyback program funded out of philanthropy-adjusted retained
earnings. The company is overcapitalized, regulators leave it alone, and an eventual liquidation or going-private
transaction at or near book would deliver a clean ~20% pop. Small float means even modest accumulation can

-- 158 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 44
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
move shares.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K explicitly defines a "related party" group around Correll and First Southern Funding/Bancorp (which
itself owns 95% of First Southern National Bank). UTG owns common stock and other interests in these affiliates,
and the LLCs that hold real estate are nested under UG to "provide additional protection to the policyholders."
Park Avenue Life Insurance Company (PALIC) coinsures substantially all of UTG's pre-1996 paid-up life book.
The investment strategy — Level 3 LLCs at $89.2 million fair value, equity securities at $265.7 million, oil & gas
royalties — is essentially impossible for an outside analyst to mark. The 2024 figures are restated. This is a "trust
the operator" stock, not a screenable bank.

BELOW-BOOK QUALITY | Score: 64/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
LCNB Corp. is the holding company for LCNB National Bank, a Lebanon, Ohio-headquartered community bank
with roughly $2.27 billion in average assets, $1.84 billion in deposits, and a wealth management division
providing trust, fiduciary, and investment management services. Net interest income for 2025 was $70.4 million,
producing a tax-equivalent NIM of 3.50% — a meaningful expansion from 2.91% in 2024 as fixed-rate assets
repriced and deposit costs stabilized. EPS rebounded to $1.63 diluted on 14.09 million weighted average shares,
against book value supporting a P/B near 0.86. Capital is healthy, with the company falling under the Basel III
framework as a covered bank holding company. Net charge-offs were just $273 thousand in 2025 —
extraordinarily clean credit.
The bear case starts with deposit composition: non-interest-bearing demand deposits averaged $468 million
versus $1,374 million interest-bearing — only ~25% NIB, well below sticky-deposit franchises like Glacier or
Hingham. Long-term debt of $104.4 million (down from $155 million) still represents real wholesale funding
dependence. The 2024 acquisition of Eagle Financial Bancorp added complexity and goodwill that pressures
tangible book. NIM expansion is rate-cycle dependent — if the Fed cuts further and asset yields lag, the 3.50%
margin compresses. Ohio community banking is competitive (Park National, Civista, First Financial Bancorp),
and LCNB has no obvious moat in deposit gathering. The wealth management line is meaningful but not
disclosed at AUM-scale specificity that would let an analyst value it as a separate asset.
The bull case is the cleanest credit in the batch combined with a real franchise. NCOs of $273K on a ~$1.6 billion
loan book is a 0.02% loss rate. ACL/loans rose to 0.80% from 0.70%, building cushion. Capital ratios remain
well-capitalized with CET1 well above regulatory floors. The dividend has been maintained, the buyback
program is in place, and the wealth management division provides fee income that smooths cyclicality. At 0.86x
book and 10.2x earnings — with NIM still expanding off a low base and credit pristine — this is the kind of
"boring quality" community bank that re-rates to 1.0–1.1x book in any normalization of regional bank sentiment.

WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
LCNB's 10-K reports the "ratio of allowance for credit losses to total non-accrual loans" at 763.88% — meaning

clean and management is reserving conservatively. The Wealth Management Division offers full trust
administration and IRA management through LCNB National Bank, providing a fee-income stream rare among
$2 billion community banks. Related-party loans to directors and officers are disclosed as ordinary-course with
normal collectability — not the red-flag pattern of self-dealing seen at some smaller controlled banks. The Federal
Reserve has authority to block dividends if capital is impaired; LCNB management explicitly references
maintaining a target Tier 1 ratio before paying dividends.

ANALYST SUMMARY
Finward Bancorp is the Munster, Indiana-based holding company for Peoples Bank, an Indiana community bank

Management Group oversaw $416.9 million in assets under management at year-end 2025, up $24.0 million

the persistent discount to book.
The bear case is that returns on capital are subscale. ROA below 0.4% and ROE near 5% mean the bank earns
about its cost of equity in good quarters and below in bad ones — the textbook definition of a "destroying value"
community bank. The 10-K discloses a memorandum of understanding requiring prior regulatory approval before
paying cash dividends — a meaningful red flag on regulatory standing and a constraint on capital return. ACL to
nonperforming loans of 156.84% (vs. 123.10% prior year) is fine but not pristine. Total deposits were essentially
flat at $1.755 billion, suggesting a stagnant deposit franchise in a competitive Chicago-suburb market. The 18.4x
P/E reflects depressed earnings, not a premium.
The bull case is straightforward NIM normalization plus regulatory cleanup. NIM jumped 46 basis points to
3.00% in a single year — if it can climb another 50–75 basis points toward peer averages, EPS roughly doubles
purely on operating leverage. Wealth Management at $416.9 million AUM provides fee income and optionality.
Net charge-offs collapsed to just $187 thousand in 2025 from $2.06 million in 2024 — a 91% improvement
signaling that the legacy credit issues that landed FNWD in regulatory crosshairs are clearing. At 0.86x book, the
market is pricing continued mediocrity; if the MOU is lifted and the dividend resumes meaningfully, a re-rate to
1.0–1.1x book delivers 15–30% upside on top of NIM-driven EPS growth. The 2014 buyback authorization

ANALYST SUMMARY
ENB Financial Corp is the holding company for Ephrata National Bank, an OCC-chartered national bank founded
April 11, 1881 and headquartered in Ephrata, Lancaster County, Pennsylvania. The bank serves the heart of
Pennsylvania Dutch country with a customer base anchored by the Amish, Mennonite, and broader Plain
communities — a rare deposit franchise built on multi-generational trust, low loan-to-deposit utilization, and
exceptionally sticky non-interest-bearing demand accounts. As of December 31, 2025, total deposits stood at
$1.873 billion, with non-interest-bearing demand at $649 million (~35% of deposits) — meaningfully better than
most $2 billion peers. Book value supports ~5.69 million shares outstanding held by approximately 850
shareholders. Net interest margin expanded 32 basis points to 3.19% in 2025, and net income reached $21.6
million.
The bear case starts with the December 2024 acquisition of Cecil Bancorp (Maryland), which added $218.7
million in assets, $153.5 million in loans, and $187.4 million in deposits — meaningful integration risk for a bank
with no recent M&A; track record, and dilutive to the famously homogeneous Lancaster franchise. ENBP trades
on the OTCQX (limited-volume), creating real liquidity friction for institutional buyers. The 2025 P/E of ~6.5 is
partly distorted by a single-year boost from NIM expansion and a lower provision; normalized earnings sustain a
higher multiple. Average non-interest-bearing deposit growth of just 3.6% suggests the deposit franchise is
mature, not growing. Pennsylvania community banking faces headwinds from regional consolidation by
Mid-Penn, Univest, and Penns Woods.
The bull case is one of the strongest deposit franchises in the small-bank universe trading at 0.87x book.
Lancaster County's Amish/Mennonite community has the lowest loan demand and highest demand-deposit
balances of any U.S. demographic — ENBP's 35% NIB ratio reflects this structural advantage. Net charge-offs
were a trivial $92 thousand on a roughly $1+ billion loan book, indicating the credit culture is intact. With Cecil
acquisition synergies still ahead and NIM having room to expand toward 3.4–3.5%, EPS could grow

WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Lancaster County, PA customer base is genuinely differentiated. The Amish and Mennonite communities
operate on cash-basis principles, hold large checking balances for farm and business operations, and transact
through a small network of trusted local institutions — with Ephrata National being one of the dominant
relationship banks for nearly 145 years. The bank also operates ENB Insurance, a full-service insurance agency
offering commercial and personal lines, providing stable fee income. Average non-interest-bearing demand
accounts grew $21.5 million in 2025, described as "the Corporation's cheapest source of funding for balance sheet
growth." The Cecil acquisition extends the franchise into Maryland's Cecil County, geographically adjacent to
Lancaster's southeast border.

ANALYST SUMMARY
MainStreet Bancshares is the Fairfax, Virginia-headquartered holding company for MainStreet Bank, operating
seven branches in the affluent Northern Virginia / Washington D.C. metro (Herndon, Fairfax, McLean,
Clarendon, Leesburg, Middleburg, plus a leased D.C. branch). At year-end 2025 the company had $2.21 billion in
total assets, $1.8 billion in net loans, $1.9 billion in deposits, and stockholders' equity of $218.6 million. Tangible
book value per common share rose to $25.52 (from $23.77) against a market price of $20.36 — a 20% discount to
tangible book. EPS rebounded sharply to $1.76 from a $(1.60) loss in 2024, as NIM expanded from 3.13% to
3.46% and the company began winding down its money-losing Avenu Banking-as-a-Service platform.

remain elevated due to "shutting down the segment, such as canceling contracts." Non-performing loans of $31.5

and ENBP run near 0.1-0.2%). The efficiency ratio of 74.15%, while improved from 110.85% in 2024, remains
well above the ~55-60% community-bank benchmark. The bank operates only 6 offices with 171 FTE — a small
operating base for a $2.2 billion balance sheet, suggesting the Avenu wind-down hasn't yet produced peer-level
cost efficiency. Northern Virginia commercial real estate — particularly office near D.C. — faces secular
headwinds from federal-workforce reductions and remote work.
The bull case is a clean cyclical-trough setup. Bank capital ratios are robust: Tier 1 leverage 13.28%, CET1
15.05%, total risk-based 16.08%. With Avenu shut down, 2026 reported earnings should reflect the underlying
community bank only — a much cleaner story trading at 0.80x tangible book. NIM expanded 33 basis points in
2025 with further runway as the wholesale deposit mix rolls. Net charge-offs were 0.00% in 2025 versus 0.25% in
2024 — credit is healing. The $0.40 annualized dividend has been maintained through the loss year. Northern
Virginia remains one of the wealthiest U.S. metros, and MainStreet's commercial banking franchise is genuinely
differentiated. If NPAs work out and efficiency drops below 65%, 2026-2027 EPS could reach $2.50-3.00,
