CASH FORTRESS | Score: 71/100
ANALYST SUMMARY
Daily Journal is a unique two-headed beast: a small legal/business publishing operation (the Traditional Business
— 	Daily 	Journals 	in 	CA/AZ, 	judicial 	profiles, 	court 	rules 	services) 	plus 	Journal 	Technologies, 	a
SaaS-and-services court case-management software business that now produces approximately 80% of
consolidated revenue (vs 76% in fiscal 2024). Sitting on top is the legendary investment portfolio originally
assembled and managed by Charlie Munger until his death on November 28, 2023. As of September 30, 2025 the
marketable-securities portfolio had a market value of approximately $493.0 million on an adjusted cost basis of
approximately $139.1 million — a cumulative unrealized gain of $353.9 million before estimated taxes of $91.4
million. The portfolio remains concentrated in just six companies, generated $7.4 million in dividends and interest
in fiscal 2025, and total assets stood at $548.1 million.
The bear case is that the Munger magic is genuinely irreplaceable, and the company says so explicitly. The 10-K
states the board "expects" the portfolio to be managed and harvested primarily to fund Journal Technologies
development going forward — which is the language of a company that no longer believes the
marketable-securities arm will compound at superior returns. The operating businesses are mediocre on their own:
Journal Technologies is a perpetually-money-losing-to-breakeven public-sector SaaS implementation business
with long sales cycles, professional-services dependence, lumpy lawsuits and competitive bidding processes. The
Traditional Business is a melting ice cube — print legal newspapers, public-notice advertising regulation under
attack. Six-stock concentration risk is enormous; a downturn in any single major holding will whip net income
and book value violently. Steven Myhill-Jones is CEO and Chairman per the FY25 signature page — leadership
transition still being digested.
The bull case rests on the gap between book value (where the portfolio is marked) and intrinsic value of Journal
Technologies if it can finally scale. P/B 1.95 understates the situation: the marketable securities are at fair-value
mark-to-market, but Journal Technologies — a sticky, switching-cost-heavy public-sector SaaS franchise serving

-- 217 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual — Vol 3 — April 2026 	Page 103
SMALL-CAPITAL OPPORTUNITIES MANUAL — VOL 3 	CONFIDENTIAL
courts, prosecutors, and justice agencies — has zero intangible value on the balance sheet beyond accumulated
investment. If Journal Technologies converts even modestly to a steady-state SaaS margin profile, the operating
value of that segment alone could approach 9-figure incremental value. Net cash represents approximately 64% of
market cap. Munger trained the discipline — there is no leverage, no dilutive issuance, no empire-building. As
long as a successor portfolio manager simply doesn't destroy the $493 million pile, downside is well-bounded.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "P/E 8.0" is essentially meaningless because reported earnings are dominated by unrealized gains on
marketable securities — fiscal 2025 saw $134.3 million of pretax unrealized gains versus only $4.2 million from
operating income. Strip out the portfolio mark-to-market and Journal Technologies + Traditional are roughly
breakeven. Deferred tax liability on unrealized gains is approximately $91.4 million, so true after-tax net asset
value attributable to the portfolio is closer to $400 million than $493 million. The portfolio uses margin
borrowing collateralized by securities to fund Journal Technologies acquisitions and working capital — a Munger
trick — and is therefore subject to margin calls in a sharp drawdown. The company has paid no dividends in fiscal
2025 or 2024 and has no current plans to. Six-stock concentration: per published 13-Fs, the portfolio still tilts
heavily toward US bank holdings.

Volume 4
Monday Deployment: 50 Actionable Special-Situation Ideas
Built specifically to deploy ~$100,000 across 50 positions on Monday morning. Every name has a 5-year price
chart, live financial snapshot, deep analyst write-up sourced from the most recent SEC filing, and a recommended
position size. Positions are designed to sum to ~$100K with no single position above $4K and tail-risk binary
positions sized at $1K.
TABLE OF CONTENTS
• A. SUB-CASH & CASH-RICH OPERATING BUSINESSES — 15 stocks | ranked by net cash / market cap
• B. RECENT SPINOFFS (LAST 12-18 MONTHS) — 15 stocks | ranked by recency + asymmetry
• C. ACTIVE ACTIVIST CAMPAIGNS — 15 stocks | ranked by activist credibility + thesis clarity
• D. HOLDING COMPANY / NAV DISCOUNTS — 5 stocks | trading at deep discount to indicative NAV
HOW TO READ EACH PAGE
• Header: ticker, company, special-situation type, recommended position size
• 5-year price chart (live from Yahoo Finance) with 5Y high/low markers
• 12-cell live financials strip (mcap, cash, debt, EV, revenue, NI, FCF, P/E, EV/EBITDA, gross margin, dividend
yield)
• Three-paragraph analyst summary
• Filing-level alpha paragraph ("What a screen can't see")
• Concrete catalysts with dates
• 10-criterion Munger checklist scored 0-10
• Recommended dollar position size with rationale

SUB-CASH OPERATING | Score: 67/100 | POSITION SIZE: $3,000 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A debt-free Greek tanker/drybulk owner trading at roughly book value of cash. Imperial Petroleum closed FY25
with $179.1M of cash plus time deposits, zero bank debt, and just declared a $10M open-market buyback in
February 2026 — all on a market cap that has at times sat below the cash line. The catalyst stack: a 26-vessel fleet
on the water by year-end 2026, no covenants to choke capital returns, and a controlling shareholder who has
finally pivoted from dilution to repurchase.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Imperial Petroleum (NASDAQ: IMPP), an Athens-based ship-owning company controlled by CEO Harry Vafias,
transports petroleum products, crude oil and dry bulk cargo. The fleet stood at 19 vessels by year-end 2025 (seven
MR product tankers, two Suezmax tankers, and ten drybulk carriers) with one Post Panamax delivery in January
2026 lifting the count to 20 — and a contracted pipeline to reach 26 vessels by year-end. FY25 produced revenue
of $161.0M (up 9.2% YoY), net income of $50.0M, EBITDA of $71.0M and operating cash flow of $80.8M. The
screen flags net cash > market cap (~$179M vs ~$158M, ratio 1.13x). At a P/E of ~3.4x reported earnings and
~3.0x adjusted, IMPP is among the cheapest profitable cash-rich names in the public market — even after a brutal
2024–25 share dilution that took weighted-average count from 30M to 38M.
The bear case is real and well-documented in the 6-K. Tanker rates are violently cyclical and "ongoing attacks by
Houthis in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden" remain a live risk explicitly named by management. Vafias-affiliated
entities provide the company's commercial management, vessel management and ship brokering — and the press
release shows $5.9M of related-party voyage/operating/management fees in FY25, with another related-party
"Investment in related party" of $13.0M sitting on the balance sheet. The float is dual-class with Series B
preferred granting outsized control to Vafias; minority holders have been diluted repeatedly via warrants, ATM
offerings and a $60M registered direct in 2025. The drybulk pivot also added depreciation, lifting D&A; from

VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
$17.0M to $25.9M and pulling reported net income flat YoY despite the revenue growth.
The bull case rests on three pillars. First: $179M cash + zero debt = downside protection that is genuinely hard to
vaporize even in a tanker downturn. Second: management explicitly disclosed current cash of "about $198
million" as of the March 6, 2026 press release — i.e., cash is building, not bleeding. Third: capital allocation
finally turned shareholder-friendly. The $10M buyback (251,625 shares already repurchased for $0.9M as of
disclosure) plus declared 8.75% Series A preferred dividends signal that further common dividends or buybacks
are plausible if the share price keeps trading at a discount to NAV. Six more vessel deliveries in 2026 = scaled
earnings power into a tanker market that, per the CEO, was "firm particularly in the second half of 2025."
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "cash" line is now mostly time deposits ($173.3M) — only $5.8M is actual cash and equivalents — because
management has been laddering proceeds to harvest interest income ($7.3M in FY25). That's positive for
return-on-cash but means the company is one bad rate cycle from giving back its non-operating earnings buffer.
Second hidden item: the $13.0M "Investment in related party" generates a $760K dividend stream and is not truly
liquid. Third — and the genuine alpha for filing-readers — Vafias's earlier vessel acquisitions used seller
financing booked at imputed-interest discounts, which is why FY25 has a $2.3M "Interest expense – related
party" line despite zero bank debt. All of this was repaid in 2025; going forward, the income statement should
look cleaner. Finally, the buyback authorization is small ($10M) relative to the cash pile — it is a price-support
gesture, not a recapitalization.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Six contracted vessel deliveries through 2026 lifting earnings power on the existing fleet utilization base of
~91.8% in Q4 25.
• Continued execution of the $10M repurchase program (only $0.9M used as of March 6, 2026 disclosure).
• Q1 2026 6-K — first quarter as a fully-debt-free 20+ vessel operator; tanker rates and any incremental dividend
declaration the key items.
• Geopolitical wild card: any escalation/de-escalation in Red Sea routing impacts ton-mile demand directly.

SUB-CASH OPERATING | Score: 64/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A profitable Taiwan-based AI/AR beauty SaaS — IPO'd 2022 via SPAC — trading at $172M of cash and time
deposits against a $177M market cap. As of March 18, 2026, the founder (CEO Alice H. Chang) and parent
CyberLink filed a preliminary non-binding take-private offer at $1.95 per share. That is the asymmetry: you are
buying a profitable, growing business at sub-cash with a live insider bid sitting on the table.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Perfect Corp. (NYSE: PERF) builds AI- and AR-powered virtual try-on, skin-analysis and image-generation tools
sold to beauty/fashion brands (B2B) and to consumers via the YouCam app suite (B2C subscriptions). FY25
revenue was $69.2M (+14.9% YoY), driven by $61.1M of "AI- and AR- cloud solutions and subscription"
revenue (+13.5%). Net income was $4.6M; operating cash flow was $13.3M. The screen flag — net cash
$161.6M against market cap $177M (91% ratio) — is grounded in $126.0M cash and equivalents plus $36.3M
six-month time deposits and $10.2M U.S. Treasuries (per the Q4 release). 908,000 active YouCam subscribers
and 859 brand clients (135 "Key Customers" generating >$50K trailing twelve months). Spun out of Taiwan
Stock Exchange-listed CyberLink, which remains a major shareholder and lessor.
The bear case begins with the dual-class structure: per the 20-F, "each holder of Class B Ordinary Shares is
entitled to ten (10) votes per share" and CEO Chang holds 100% of Class B = 66.6% voting power. That control is
now being weaponized via the take-private offer at $1.95 — a price below the prior trading range and at a
discount to net cash. Filings flag that the dual-class structure may make Class A "ineligible for inclusion in any of
these indices." Operating loss of $1.7M for FY25 reflects the company's reliance on financial income and a $2.0M
goodwill impairment from January 2025's Wannaby acquisition. App-store dependency (Apple/Google) is
explicit. Key Customer count just fell from 142 to 135 in a single quarter, hinting at B2B churn even as B2C
subscriber count declined 4% sequentially.

The bull case: even excluding the Wannaby write-down, Perfect would have generated operating income; the

is a forcing function for a higher price.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

accounted for less than 3% of our total revenue" — China exposure is small, removing one common

take-private easier to justify. Insider activity to watch: Chang, Wei-Hsin Tsen and Weichuan Liu collectively own
minor Class A stakes — but Chang's full Class B block (16.79M shares = 16.5% economic, 66.6% voting) drives
the deal math.

initiative.
• Continued cash build vs. SBC dilution — the gap between cash flow ($13.3M) and reported net income ($4.6M)
is largely SBC, so a tighter SBC budget would re-rate the multiple.

SUB-CASH OPERATING | Score: 58/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,000 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A failed-Phase-3 oral-insulin biotech that has quietly transformed into a specialty financier. Oramed's
"profitability" is an artifact: $89M of FY25 financial income from its Scilex notes, warrants and royalty deal —
not from drug development. Net cash is ~80% of market cap, the company just paid a special dividend funded by
Scilex repayments, and it is actively returning capital while shareholders still treat it as a binary biotech.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Oramed Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: ORMP) is an Israeli holding company with two distinct lives.
Drug-development life: oral insulin candidate ORMD-0801 missed both primary and secondary endpoints in the
ORA-D-013-1 Phase 3 trial. The company plans a 60-patient U.S. trial in H2 2026 to validate "responder"
subpopulations and has handed clinical management to a JV (with HTIT, though HTIT failed initial closing
conditions) and to Lifeward (POD platform transferred January 2026). Investment-vehicle life: in 2023 Oramed
lent $101.875M (Tranche A) to Scilex Holding; later added $25M Tranche B plus warrants and a 4% royalty on
ZTLido. As of March 26, 2026, Scilex had repaid $69.2M of Tranche A and $13.0M of Tranche B; per company
communications cumulative returns reached approximately $118M on the original $99.5M cost basis. FY25
revenue of $2.0M (versus zero in FY24), operating loss of $14.1M, but financial income of $89.5M produced
reported net income of $64.0M.
The bear case is direct. The drug pipeline failed and is being de-prioritized in favor of small responder-subgroup
trials; per the 10-K, "we expect to discontinue oral insulin clinical activities for T2D." The Scilex creditor
exposure is concentrated and risky — Scilex itself has disclosed "substantial doubt about its ability to continue as
a going concern" in its filings. The 10-K explicitly warns "Scilex is unable to service its obligations under the
Note and defaults on such Note, it could have a material adverse effect on our business." Operating cash burn of
$9.1M in FY25 will continue. Working capital fell from $137.5M to $114.2M YoY — the cash pile is shrinking

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 10
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
even as the company harvests Scilex repayments, because of the dividend payable and continued investments in
Alpha Tau, Lifeward, and real-estate ventures (Hapisga, Profit Sharing Loan).
The bull case rests on the realisation pattern. Scilex has actually paid — $118M on $99.5M cost basis already,
and Oramed retains roughly $27M of remaining notes and a $12M convertible note (convertible at $36/share at

you're paying market cap below cash. The company has aggressively repurchased shares: 899,609 in 2025 at

Board-authorized $20M Buy-Back Program was extended to June 2026. A January 2026 cash dividend was
announced after the Scilex Option Agreement payoff.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K's "Investments at fair value" section is where the alpha lives. Oramed elected the fair-value option for
the Scilex notes "in order to reduce operational complexity of bifurcating embedded derivatives" — meaning the
carrying value of those notes already reflects management's expected repayment scenarios, weighted across
multiple paths. Translated: the $89M financial income line is part real cash interest and part mark-to-fair-value
re-rating as Scilex actually pays. The company has also become a hidden real-estate financier — September 2024
Profit Sharing Loan Agreement and March 2025 Hapisga loan are property project finance, not biotech. April
2025 Alpha Tau equity stake and November 2025 $3M Lifeward secured note are further capital allocations away
from the original drug thesis. Insider activity: a flurry of Form 4 filings in March 2026 (Section 16 transactions on
March 16, 18 and 31, 2026) signals active trading by officers/directors — review these before sizing.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Remaining Scilex Tranche B amortization payments through October 2026 — additional ~$30M of expected
cash inflow if paid on schedule.
• Special-dividend follow-on: management has shown willingness to declare cash distributions when Scilex
repays.
• 60-patient responder-subgroup U.S. trial start in H2 2026 (binary biotech optionality, but the cash already pays
you to wait).
• Continued buyback execution under the $20M program (extended to June 2026).

CASH FORTRESS | Score: 60/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,000 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A 60-year-old, family-controlled Florida maker of asphalt-plant equipment with $136M of cash and marketable
securities (cash 68% of market cap), zero debt, and a backlog that just rebuilt to $57.4M as of Q1 fiscal 2026 from
a December trough of $23.6M. The bear case (IIJA expiration September 30, 2026) is real but priced in; the bull
case is a profitable cyclical with a fortress balance sheet, a chairman transition, and a board with no excuse not to
return capital — except its long-stated refusal to do so.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Gencor Industries (NYSE-American: GENC) designs and manufactures hot-mix asphalt plants, soil-remediation
equipment and related combustion systems, sold to highway contractors in the U.S. and Canada. Fiscal 2025 (year
ended September 30, 2025) net revenue was $115.4M (+2.0% YoY), gross profit $31.7M (27.5% margin),
operating income $14.0M, and net income $15.7M ($1.07 diluted EPS). The screen flag — net cash $147M
against market cap $216M (68% ratio) — is conservative: the 10-K states "the Company had $26,587,000 in cash
and cash equivalents, and $109,714,000 in marketable securities" plus "working capital was $197.7 million."
Marketable securities are managed by a third-party investment firm and include corporate bonds, ETFs, mutual
funds, equities and government securities. 318 full-time employees, no major union exposure outside Marquette,
Iowa. The company is controlled by the Elliott family: Class B holders elect 75% of the board, and EJ Elliott
alone owns 87.9% of Class B (plus 13.3% of Common); his son Marc Elliott (now Chairman effective January 1,
2026) holds another 8.3% of Class B.
The bear case is well-laid-out in the 10-K. First, "the Company has identified material weaknesses in its internal
control over financial reporting" — an explicit ICFR failure as of September 30, 2025. That is an audit-committee
level red flag in any size company. Second, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act "is scheduled to expire on
September 30, 2026"; backlog already collapsed from $72.2M (Sept 2024) to $28.2M (Sept 2025) before partially

-- 230 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 13
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
recovering to $57.4M in Q1 fiscal 2026. Third, the Company "does not expect to pay cash dividends for the
foreseeable future" and the Class B/Class A structure means the Elliott family fully controls capital allocation —
minorities have no leverage. Fourth, marketable-securities exposure to equities and ETFs introduces
realized/unrealized P&L; volatility ($2.0M+ historically) into reported earnings.
The bull case: $136M of liquid securities + $26.6M cash on $216M of market cap means the operating business is
being valued at roughly $80M for a company that did $14M of operating income and $15.7M of net income — a
sub-6x EV/EBIT for a profitable, debt-free industrial. IIJA reauthorization is the dominant catalyst; even partial
reauthorization would re-rate the multiple. The new chairman (Marc Elliott) has the latitude to re-think capital
return policy after his father's December 2025 retirement from the board. Even without a dividend, the cash hoard
compounds at Treasury yields and provides downside protection through the cyclical valley.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K bears two filing-specific items most screens miss. (1) Customer concentration: in fiscal 2024 "one

customer) but is structural to a market with a small number of large highway contractors. (2) Revenue is
recognized "over time when the performance obligation is satisfied by transferring control of the equipment" with
"revenues and costs recognized in proportion to actual labor costs incurred" — a percentage-of-completion
methodology that creates earnings volatility and makes the material-weakness ICFR finding more concerning
than at a typical reseller. Third, the marketable-securities portfolio is actively managed by an external manager —
meaning a chunk of the "cash" is exposed to equity beta and credit risk. Insider ownership: Elliott family +
officers + Foundation = 17.3% of Common and 100% of Class B — outside holders cannot force capital return.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• IIJA reauthorization or successor-bill negotiations through Q3 calendar 2026 — the single largest event for the
entire customer base.
• Q2 fiscal 2026 10-Q (~April-May 2026) — backlog trajectory after the $57.4M Q1 print.
• Material-weakness remediation update; failure to remediate could attract activist attention.
• Marc Elliott's first full year as Chairman — any change in dividend or buyback posture would be a substantial
re-rating event.

services is "a criminal offense that may be penalized by a fine between AED50,000 (US$13,615) and
AED1,000,000 (US$272,294) and/or imprisonment of up to two years." Regulators rely on local telecom carriers
blocking unlicensed services rather than prosecuting users — a fragile equilibrium. Founder/CEO Tao Yang

being UAE-centric.
The bull case: this is a profitable, cash-rich operating business at sub-cash on operating earnings power. EV
(mcap minus net cash, ex-buyback impact) is ~$360M against $148M of net income — a sub-3x EV/earnings
multiple on a 43%-net-margin platform with eight years of operating history in the world's fastest-growing youth
demographic. Management is actually returning cash: $56.6M repurchased in 2025 + $150M newly authorized =
$206M of demonstrated/promised capital return on a $1.11B market cap (~18%). Saudi Arabia presence is being
formalized with a Riyadh office and a Saudi eLeague partnership — local strategic positioning that is hard to
replicate. UAE corporate-tax rate cuts already pulled the FY25 effective tax rate from 9.4% to 2.6%.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three filing-level items. (1) Deferred revenue dropped from $58.1M to $55.2M (current) but a new $1.8M
non-current deferred revenue line appeared — a small but novel monetization shift. (2) "Long-term investments"
of $82.0M sit alongside the $754.6M of "near-cash" — these are wealth-management-product positions whose
mark-to-market drives the lumpy "Investment (loss) income" line ($3.3M income FY25 vs. $2.8M loss FY24).
The actual cash story is therefore slightly less liquid than the screen flag suggests. (3) Share-based compensation
fell sharply from $14.7M to $10.3M YoY — a discipline signal that supports the dilution-management story
behind the buyback program. (4) The company decided to cancel all 6.23M ADSs repurchased in 2025 —
meaning these are retired, not held in treasury for re-issuance, which permanently lifts EPS and stops the
"buyback only to fund SBC" complaint.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Execution of the new $150M share repurchase program (24-month window from March 9, 2026).
• Q1 2026 results (~May 2026) — first quarter post-Ramadan, plus launch of "desert-themed SLG title" in Q2 26.
• Saudi eLeague 2026 traction and any monetization tied to the Saudi Esports Federation partnership.
• Continued tax-rate compression as the UAE preferential-rate subsidiary regime matures (tax expense already
$4.0M FY25 vs. $13.9M FY24).

BIOTECH BINARY | Score: 58/100 | POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
Net cash of $109.9M against a $167M market cap — the market is pricing BDTX-1535 (the only meaningful
asset post-restructuring) at roughly $57M of enterprise value, with a Phase 2 readout in NSCLC already showing
60% ORR / 86% CNS ORR in 43 frontline patients. Cash runway extended into 2H 2028 after the Servier
BDTX-4933 license. Asymmetry comes from the gap between in-line enterprise value and the addressable
EGFR-mutant NSCLC market.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Black Diamond is a clinical-stage precision oncology company built around its MasterKey allosteric kinase
inhibitor platform. After an October 2024 restructuring that cut headcount and refocused the pipeline, the entire
investment case is now BDTX-1535 — a fourth-generation EGFR inhibitor designed for non-classical EGFR
mutations and osimertinib-resistant disease. The screen flags it because the GAAP TTM net income (~$22M) is
non-operating: it reflects upfront and milestone recognition from the Servier BDTX-4933 deal, not commercial
revenue. The $109.9M net cash represents two-thirds of market cap, and the company guided runway into 2H
2028 as of the Q3 2025 print ($135.5M cash, equivalents, and investments).
The bear case is straightforward biotech binary risk. BDTX-1535 has shown encouraging early Phase 2 numbers,
but durability of response (DOR) and progression-free survival (PFS) — the data that actually drive registration
— won't read out until Q2 2026. The EGFR-mutant NSCLC space is crowded: J&J;'s Rybrevant (amivantamab)
is approved and combined with lazertinib is the new standard of care challenger; Takeda's Exkivity was
withdrawn from market in 2023 (a sobering precedent); sunvozertinib and furmonertinib are advancing as well. A
miss on durability or competitive labeling tilts the equity toward cash value with optionality discount.

The bull case rests on three pillars. First, BDTX-1535 attacks a differentiated niche — non-classical / compound
EGFR mutations and osimertinib-resistance — where current therapies underperform. Second, FDA feedback in
Q4 2025 on a pivotal path could unlock a registrational design in 1H 2026, accelerating the value-recognition
timeline. Third, downside is mathematically protected: $110M net cash on a $167M cap = $1.66/share of cash
floor, so a clean clinical wash leaves ~30% downside before management discretion erodes it. The Servier
BDTX-4933 milestone tail provides additional non-dilutive optionality.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

BDTX-1535 is NOT positioned in EGFR exon 20 insertion (where Rybrevant and the now-withdrawn Exkivity
sit) — the company explicitly targets PACC/compound mutations and osimertinib-resistance, a smaller but less
crowded niche. Finally, the restructuring shed the rest of the pipeline, so a single Phase 2 readout dictates equity
outcome.

HIDDEN COMPOUNDER | Score: 76/100 | POSITION SIZE: $3,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
Munger's old holdco trades at a $749M market cap with a marketable securities portfolio worth ~$443M
(mid-2025 mark) and a growing court-software business (Journal Technologies) compounding revenue +12%
YoY in Q1 FY26. Stripping the portfolio at fair value, the operating business is on offer at roughly $300M for
$60M+ of growing software revenue. The market dislikes the GAAP optics from quarterly mark-to-market on
concentrated bank stocks — that's the inefficiency.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Daily Journal is two businesses stitched into one entity. (1) The Traditional segment publishes legal and business
newspapers in California (declining, low-margin, but cash-positive). (2) Journal Technologies sells eSuite — case
management, e-filing, and jury management software to courts, prosecutors, and public defenders across the US,
Canada, and Australia. Q1 FY2026 (Oct–Dec 2025) put consolidated revenue at $19.5M (+10% YoY), with
Journal Tech at $15.2M (+12%). Steven Myhill-Jones is Chairman and CEO post-Munger. The screen flagged the
name on $477M net cash / 64% of market cap and a 8.0 P/E — both numbers driven by a securities portfolio
originally constructed by Charlie Munger.
The bear case has three legs. First, the investment portfolio is heavily concentrated in financials — Wells Fargo
(~48%), Bank of America (~40%), Alibaba ADR (~10%), US Bancorp (~2%) per the Q4 2025 13F (~$277M
reported value at filing date) — and quarterly mark-to-market drives wild GAAP swings (Q1 FY26 showed an
$11.7M unrealized loss flipping a $10.9M underlying profit into an $8M reported loss). Second, Journal
Technologies revenue is concentrated in long-cycle government contracts; implementation timelines slip, deferred
revenue can be lumpy, and competition from Tyler Technologies and Thomson Reuters is real. Third,
post-Munger capital allocation discipline is unproven — the portfolio rebalancing risk is non-trivial.

The bull case is the rare combination of a real, growing software business inside a holdco trading well below

WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

importantly, no screen captures the cultural inheritance: DJCO is one of the few public companies where
management has demonstrated for decades they will hold concentrated positions through volatility rather than
diworsify. That's the actual moat.

SUB-CASH OPERATING | Score: 70/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
Recent IPO (March 2025), a Singapore-domiciled oilfield equipment maker selling specialty connectors and
surface wellhead systems primarily into Saudi Aramco under a 10-year supply agreement. $128.7M cash at end of
H1 FY26 vs. $190M market cap = 67% cash, plus the company is profitably generating operating cash ($26.4M in
H1 FY26). The underfollowed micro-cap discount is the asymmetry — institutional analysts haven't picked it up
post-IPO.
ANALYST SUMMARY
OMS Energy Technologies designs and manufactures premium connector systems and surface wellhead
equipment for the upstream oil and gas industry. The company operates manufacturing in Singapore, Indonesia,
and Saudi Arabia, and is qualified under Saudi Aramco's IKTVA (In-Kingdom Total Value Add) localization
program — a structural moat that only a few non-Saudi suppliers have earned. The screen captures it via $119M
net cash on a $190M cap (63% cash), a TTM P/E of 6.5, and $29M of TTM net income. H1 FY2026 (six months
to September 30, 2025) revenue was $82.8M with $14.6M net profit. The H1 number declined vs. unusually
strong prior-year comp driven by exceptional Saudi call-off volumes in H1 FY25.
The bear case starts and ends with customer concentration: Saudi Aramco accounted for 67% of FY25 revenue,
and the long-term supply agreement allows termination on 30 days' notice. That is an extreme single-customer
dependency in a region where geopolitical risk is non-trivial. Secondary risks: oil & gas capex cyclicality, lumpy
call-off orders that complicate revenue forecasting (already visible in the H1 normalization), recent-IPO insider
lockup expirations creating supply, and the small-float / illiquid trading that makes both entry and exit harder. As
a foreign private issuer (20-F filer), disclosure cadence is also lighter than US issuers — only semi-annual
financials.

The bull case: cash is nearly two-thirds of market cap and growing, the company is profitable, the Saudi Aramco

order signals continued spending under the umbrella agreement. With $128.7M cash plus $61M of trailing
operating leverage, the entire enterprise value is roughly $61M for a business throwing off $25–30M of net
income. That is a sub-3x EV/earnings on a profitable, IKTVA-qualified specialty oilfield supplier with multi-year
contractual visibility. Even with a haircut for customer concentration, this is a structurally cheap micro-cap.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Three filing-level points the screen misses. First, the IKTVA qualification (and Indonesia's TKDN equivalent) is a

de-qualification would require a multi-year unwind. Second, the H1 FY26 net cash growth came partly from
$28.9M of IPO proceeds — strip those and operating cash generation is still positive but the runway extension is
structural, not one-off. Third, OMS reports under IFRS as a foreign private issuer; "net cash" and "EBITDA" are
calculated differently than US GAAP screens assume. Backlog disclosure is qualitative ("sizeable") rather than
quantified — a notable gap.

BIOTECH BINARY | Score: 60/100 | POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
ZEVASKYN (pz-cel) FDA-approved April 28, 2025 — first and only autologous gene-modified cell therapy for
RDEB wounds. Abeona ended 2025 with $191M cash (incl. short-term investments) on a $307M market cap,
profitable for the year on the back of a $155M PRV sale ($152M net gain). The asymmetry: market is pricing in
essentially zero commercial value while a real, approved orphan therapy is just starting its launch ramp.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Abeona is now a commercial-stage gene therapy company. The lead asset, ZEVASKYN (prademagene
zamikeracel, "pz-cel"), is an autologous, gene-modified cell therapy for recessive dystrophic epidermolysis
bullosa (RDEB) — an ultra-rare, devastating skin-blistering genetic disease. The FDA approved ZEVASKYN on
April 28, 2025; commercial launch began in Q4 2025 with the first commercial patient treated in December. Total
2025 revenue was $5.8M ($3.4M license + $2.4M product), reflecting only one commercial patient. The screen
captures the name on $166M net cash / 54% of market cap and a 5.4 TTM P/E driven entirely by the $152.4M
PRV sale gain — not recurring operations. Underlying operating burn remained meaningful (mid-double-digit
millions annually).
The bear case: ZEVASKYN is a complex autologous therapy requiring biopsy, gene modification ex vivo, and
graft application at qualified treatment centers. Each treatment is logistics-heavy and surgical — slow ramp is the
base case. The addressable US RDEB population is roughly 750–1,000 patients, with maybe 200–300 with severe
enough wounds to warrant the procedure (only ~30 patients were initially identified by the company).
Reimbursement at the multi-million-dollar list price requires payer education for what is a one-time-per-wound
therapy. Competition exists — Krystal Bio's Vyjuvek (a topical gene therapy approved 2023) addresses the same
disease with very different administration. Real underlying loss without PRV is ~$80M annually; cash funds
operations into 2027 base case.

-- 244 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 27
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
The bull case: the entire enterprise value is roughly $140M ($307M cap minus $166M net cash) for an
FDA-approved gene therapy in an ultra-rare disease with multi-million-dollar pricing power. If Abeona treats just
20–30 patients per year at peak, run-rate revenue would be $50–100M+ — and the launch infrastructure
(treatment center activations) is the gating item, not approval or efficacy. Beyond ZEVASKYN, the AAV204
capsid platform offers preclinical optionality. The PRV sale already de-risked cash through the launch ramp
window; another 1–2 years of treatment center activations and patient throughput data could justify a
multi-hundred-million dollar revaluation if the launch executes.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 5.4 P/E is non-recurring — it's the PRV sale. Strip out the $152.4M one-time gain and Abeona is structurally
loss-making until ZEVASKYN reaches commercial scale. The screen also doesn't show that "approved gene
therapy with $191M cash" is fundamentally different from a Phase 2 binary: the binary is now operational (can
Abeona activate treatment centers and execute the surgical workflow at scale?) rather than scientific (does the
therapy work?). Treatment center activation pace and Q1/Q2 2026 patient counts are the actual leading indicators
— not press releases. Underlying cash burn (~$80M/yr) means runway is roughly 24 months absent further
milestones; that's the real clock.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026) — first full quarter of commercial ZEVASKYN sales

BIOTECH BINARY | Score: 64/100 | POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
Compugen monetized a portion of its rilvegostomig royalty stream to AstraZeneca for $65M upfront (+$25M on
BLA acceptance) in December 2025, extending cash runway into 2029 — and retained the majority of the royalty
interest plus up to $195M in remaining milestones. With $145.6M cash on a $274M market cap, enterprise value
is ~$129M for a partnered TIGIT discovery platform whose lead asset is in 11 ongoing Phase 3 trials run by
AstraZeneca. The asymmetry: meaningful Phase 3 readouts are 2026–2027 events the market hasn't priced.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Compugen is an Israeli computational antibody discovery platform — its proprietary algorithms predict drug
targets from genomic data, and the company validates and out-licenses them. The crown jewel is
COM701/rilvegostomig: a TIGIT/PD-1 bispecific antibody licensed to AstraZeneca, now the most advanced
anti-TIGIT asset in industry, with 11 Phase 3 trials across NSCLC, GI, and endometrial cancers. A second asset,
COM902 (anti-TIGIT mAb), is partnered with Gilead. The screen captures Compugen on $142.7M net cash /
52% of market cap and a 7.6 P/E — the GAAP profitability comes from the December 2025 royalty monetization
with HealthCare Royalty, not recurring operations. As of December 31, 2025, cash was $145.6M and
management guides runway into 2029.
The bear case: TIGIT as a target has been a graveyard. Roche's tiragolumab failed multiple Phase 3s; Merck's
vibostolimab data has been mixed; Gilead's domvanalimab is ongoing but watched with skepticism.
AstraZeneca's rilvegostomig is the bispecific bet, but the broader class signal-to-noise is poor and a high-profile
Phase 3 miss would compress the platform multiple immediately. Secondary: Compugen has limited control over
partner-driven Phase 3 timing, the royalty stream Compugen retained is now diluted (sold a portion), and the
company is a foreign private issuer (Israel) with semi-annual reporting cadence under the 20-F regime, plus
regional geopolitical overlay.

SUB-CASH OPERATING | Score: 76/100 | POSITION SIZE: $3,000 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A 5G/cloud-native network assurance vendor that just delivered 17.2% revenue growth, record operating margins,
$109.9M in cash with no debt — and trades at roughly half its market cap in net cash. Customer concentration is
the moat (multi-year Tier-1 carrier contracts) and the risk (top three = ~86% of revenue). When a Tier-1 telecom
embeds your software in their 5G core, you don't get ripped out cheaply.
ANALYST SUMMARY
RADCOM is an Israel-headquartered software company that sells "service assurance" — the systems that monitor
whether AT&T;'s, Rakuten Mobile's and Verizon's mobile networks are actually delivering performance to
subscribers. The screen flagged it because cash and short-term deposits of ~$109.9M sit against a ~$218M market
cap (49% of mcap), the company is profitable (TTM net income ~$12M), and it just printed a record FY2025 with
$71.5M revenue (+17.2%). Gross margin reached ~76.8%. Six consecutive years of growth. 2026 guidance:
8–12% revenue growth (~$78.6M midpoint). It's a small-cap software company priced like a melting ice cube but
acting like a steady compounder.
The bear case starts with concentration: ~86% of 2025 revenue came from the top three customers (AT&T; and
Rakuten Mobile are public; Verizon and Vodafone have been historical names). Lose one, lose roughly a quarter
to a third of revenue overnight — and these are deals that ramp slowly, so the offsetting wins take 18–36 months.
Lumpy bookings make any single year of guidance vulnerable to a slipped contract. RADCOM is also
Israel-based: geopolitical disruption, currency (shekel) exposure, and a thin float (~$220M mcap, modest daily
volume) all amplify volatility. Slowing growth guidance (8–12% vs. 17% delivered) suggests management sees a
deceleration into 2026 absent a new Tier-1 win.

The bull case: the cash cushion makes you nearly indifferent to a one-year revenue hiccup. Stripping cash, the

Catalysts include incremental Rakuten Symphony wins, expansion at existing customers as they roll out 5G
standalone cores, and potential capital return — the cash hoard is large relative to operating needs. Downside is
cushioned by the cash; upside is operating leverage.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

deeply embedded software contracts inside Tier-1 5G core deployments, not transactional sales. AT&T; has been
a customer for years; Rakuten Mobile chose RADCOM as its assurance vendor at the beginning of its greenfield
mobile build and stayed as it crossed 10M subscribers in December 2025. Switching costs in network assurance
are extreme: the software ingests live network telemetry across thousands of nodes, and rip-and-replace creates
blind spots no carrier accepts mid-rollout. The screen also can't see the operating margin trajectory — record
margins in 2025 imply most incremental revenue is dropping to the bottom line, which compounds quickly on a
sub-$80M revenue base.

• Expansion deals at AT&T; / Rakuten as 5G standalone deployments scale
• Capital allocation announcement — buyback expansion or special dividend on $109.9M cash pile

SUB-CASH OPERATING | Score: 72/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A profitable, growing Chinese auto-parts maker trading at a P/E of ~3.9 with 45% of its market cap in net cash, on
track to a record $730M revenue year — yet the market values the entire equity at $129M because "China + auto
+ small cap" is three strikes. Q3 2025 EPS grew 77.8% YoY. The company already paid a $0.80 special dividend
in 2024, proving the cash is real and repatriable.
ANALYST SUMMARY
CAAS is a Hubei-based supplier of power steering systems (hydraulic, electric, and the hybrid "iRCB" intelligent
electro-hydraulic circulating ball steering for heavy vehicles) operating through eight Sino-foreign joint ventures.
Customers include FAW, Dongfeng, BYD, Beiqi Foton, Chery, Stellantis and Ford. Screen flagged it for net cash
of ~$58M against a $129M market cap (45%), TTM net income of $33.5M and a P/E under 4. Q3 2025 net sales
were $193.2M (+17.7% YoY); nine-month sales hit $536.5M (+16.1%) and management raised full-year 2025
guidance to $730M. Cash and short-term investments were $167.3M (~$5.54/share) at quarter-end. North
America +77.3%, Brazil +30.5%. By April 22, 2026 the company files audited 2025 numbers.
The bear case is the China discount, which exists for reasons: VIE-adjacent corporate structure (Sino-foreign JVs
with minority partners on the operating subsidiaries); RMB-to-USD repatriation risk via PRC capital controls;
opaque governance for US holders; auditing/PCAOB overhang on US-listed China names; customer
concentration in a viciously competitive Chinese auto market where local OEMs compete on price; EV transition
risk to legacy hydraulic steering content; and geopolitical risk of US-China tariffs/delistings. Sales to Chery
dropped in 2025 even as the rest of the book grew. The dividend was a one-off "special," not recurring. A $25M
dividend is a small thank-you on a $58M net cash hoard sitting in PRC subsidiaries.

CASH FORTRESS | Score: 74/100 | POSITION SIZE: $1,500 (of $100K)
THE SETUP
A best-in-class work-management SaaS that grew revenue 27% to $1.232B in 2025, generated $322.7M of free
cash flow, and sits on $1.5B of cash — 44% of its $3.4B market cap. This is a deep-value manual but MNDY is
here for one reason: when a high-quality SaaS compounder gets de-rated to the point that nearly half the price is
cash, it stops being a "growth stock" and starts being a Munger-style cash fortress with a free option on continued
execution.
ANALYST SUMMARY
monday.com sells a horizontal "Work OS" — visual workflows, dashboards, CRM, dev, service modules — to
~250K+ paid customers globally. FY2025 revenue $1.232B (+27%), Q4 $334M (+25%). Customers >$50K
ARR: 4,281 (+34% YoY); >$100K ARR: 1,756 (+45%); >$500K ARR: 87 (+74%). NDR was 116% in both
>$50K and >$100K cohorts. Adjusted FCF $322.7M (26% margin). 14% non-GAAP operating margin in 2025.
Cash and equivalents ~$1.5B with no debt. The screen flagged it for cash >40% of mcap; the 2026 guide is
$1.452-$1.462B revenue (18-19% growth) and $275-$290M adjusted FCF. The Q4 print + soft guide drove the
de-rating that made this name screen-eligible at all.
The bear case is competitive intensity and growth deceleration. Asana, Atlassian, ClickUp, Smartsheet, Notion
and Microsoft Loop all overlap — and AI-native upstarts threaten the entire workflow-builder category.
Management already embedded weak no-touch SMB channel performance into 2026 guidance, plus 100-200bp
FX headwinds from shekel appreciation (55% of staff in Israel). 18-19% growth from a 27% base is a real
slowdown. Israel HQ creates geopolitical, FX, and talent risks. Multiple compression continued through 2025 as
the market re-priced unprofitable-to-marginally-profitable SaaS. SBC remains material (typical SaaS dilution
dynamics) — the "cash fortress" optic understates dilution if shares grow ~3-4%/yr.

The bull case: at $3.4B mcap minus $1.5B cash = ~$1.9B EV on $1.232B revenue and $322M adjusted FCF.

became the fastest product to $1M ARR in company history. Enterprise traction (>$500K customers +74%)
suggests upmarket motion is working. Catalysts: AI/Vibe monetization scaling, large buyback authorization on
the cash pile, or a multiple recovery if 2026 guide proves conservative. Downside is cushioned by the FCF + cash
balance.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen captures "cash 44% of mcap" but misses that nearly all of that cash is operating cash flow generated,

>$500K ARR, +74%) that mid-market software companies rarely achieve without acquisition. And monday.com
runs a multi-product strategy (Work OS + CRM + Dev + Service + Vibe) where each module is a separate
land-and-expand vector — the screen gives no credit for cross-sell optionality. Finally, the Israeli
founder-operator model (similar to Wix, Fiverr, Check Point) historically outperforms US-HQ SaaS on capital
efficiency.

A Cyprus-domiciled mobile gaming holdco (the former Russian "Nexters"), 39% of its $289M market cap in
cash, P/E ~4.2, and just declared a $3.31 special dividend in February 2025 (~$60M, ~20% yield at the time).
Hero Wars revenue is in decline (bookings -13% in 2025) and the entire investment thesis hinges on whether the
new Pixel Gun 2 launch in early 2026 stabilizes the franchise. Optionality at a deep discount, with capital-return
precedent.
ANALYST SUMMARY
GDEV is a mobile games holding company headquartered in Limassol, Cyprus, which evolved from Nexters in
June 2023 after relocating away from Russia. Its primary asset is Hero Wars (cumulative $1.7B revenue, 185M+
installs, ~93% of 2025 revenue). Screen flagged it for net cash ~$112M against $289M mcap (39%), TTM net
income ~$69M, and a P/E of 4.2. Q3 2025 revenue $98M (-12% YoY); 9-month revenue $315M vs. $323M prior
year. But profit improved sharply: 9-month profit went from ~$24M to ~$55M as marketing pulled back to a
smaller, higher-value paying audience. EBITDA $64M for first nine months. February 2025: special dividend of
$3.31/share (~$60M). Remains debt-free.
The bear case is structural. Hero Wars bookings down 4% in 2024, down 13% in 2025; monthly paying users
down 18% in 2025. The franchise is harvesting, not growing — and 93% revenue concentration in one game is a
single-asset risk on a depleting asset. The Russian-heritage problem is not fully behind the company: even after
relocation, sanctions risk, key-person risk, and reputational discounts remain. The special dividend was explicitly
described as one-time — management said no recurring dividends are anticipated. The earlier 2023 buyback
authorization (up to 20M shares at $2) has been more theatrical than executed. New game pipeline is thin — Pixel
Gun 2 is the headline launch, but mobile game launches have notoriously low hit rates and 2026 timing creates
execution risk. Cash gets dividended out faster than it's reinvested.

Filshie Clip female sterilization device, neonatal blood-pressure monitoring (Deltran), electrosurgical disposables,
vacuum-assisted delivery systems, and obstetric vacuum cups. Manufacturing is in Utah, Ireland, England and
Australia. Screen flagged it for $85.5M net cash (38% of mcap), TTM net income ~$11.3M, and profitability with
low SBC. FY2025 revenue $38.5M (-5.8%), GAAP EPS $3.483 (-12.1%), gross margin maintained above 57%.
In 2025 the company repurchased 148,935 shares for $8.36M (avg $56.10) — about 4.5% of shares — and paid
$4.0M in dividends. The 2025 weakness was driven by a PendoTECH OEM customer slowdown and a Chinese
distributor cancellation that produced a written-off receivable.
The bear case is concentration and growth. PendoTECH (a single OEM) and a now-burnt Chinese distributor
relationship caused the 2025 revenue dip. A $38.5M-revenue medical device company has limited operating
leverage, no high-growth product line, and faces gradual ASP/competitive erosion in legacy categories like
Filshie Clip (which itself faced FDA-related labeling controversy historically). Kevin Cornwell is 80+ and has
been CEO since 1992 — there is real key-person and succession risk; the 1998 change-of-control agreement is a
tell. The float is tiny (~$220M mcap, low daily volume), making both entry and exit costly. International exposure
(Ireland, UK, Australia, China) creates FX drag. There is no realistic 2x re-rating catalyst; this is a "compound at
high single digits + buyback" story, not a multibagger.

-- 261 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 44
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
The bull case: this is a textbook Munger company hiding in a screen. 57%+ gross margin specialty medical,
family-controlled (Cornwell ~5.2%, ~190K shares ~$11M+ insider stake), zero debt, $85.8M cash growing
despite buybacks, ~zero stock-based compensation (almost unheard of), and a 30+ year CEO who treats
shareholders like partners. Strip the cash and you pay ~$137M for ~$11M of net income — ~12x earnings on a
57%-GM annuity that has paid uninterrupted dividends and bought back stock for years. The 2025 dip is cyclical
(one OEM, one distributor) not structural — a normalization in 2026 plus the smaller share count creates EPS
upside.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The screen sees "P/E ~20" and gets bored. The 10-K reveals one of the rarest combinations in micro-cap
healthcare: a CEO who has been in the seat since 1992, owns ~5% of the float, takes a $152K base salary, no
stock options awarded, and runs the cash like a private owner — buying back 4.5% of shares in a single year
($8.4M) at $56.10 average while the company simultaneously paid $4M in dividends and grew the cash balance.
There is no SBC dilution silently eating returns. The filings also show that 2025 weakness is identifiable and
idiosyncratic (one OEM + one distributor write-off), not a market-share story. Manufacturing across four
countries (Utah, Ireland, UK, Australia) creates FX drag but also tariff insulation. The Filshie Clip and IUPC
franchises throw off cash with minimal reinvestment.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• 2026 normalization off the PendoTECH/Chinese distributor trough (easy comps)
• Continued buyback at depressed prices (4.5% retired in 2025; pace likely continues)
• Special dividend or accelerated buyback if cash hits $90M+ on a $223M cap

B. RECENT SPINOFFS (LAST 12-18 MONTHS)
15 stocks | ranked by recency + asymmetry
Stocks created by parent-company separations within the last 12-18 months. Forced selling by parent-company
indexers, complexity discounts, and mismatched investor bases create the classic Greenblatt setup. Each entry
details the spin mechanics and what's still mispriced.

SPINOFF (from SITE Centers) | Spin Date: 2024-10-01 | Score: 78/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500
THE SETUP
CURB is the rarest of REIT spinoffs — the parent (SITC) kept the troubled legacy strip-center portfolio and spun
the high-quality "convenience real estate" piece OUT with roughly $500M of net cash. SITC dividend/value
holders received small-cap CURB shares with no dividend history, no index inclusion, and no sell-side coverage,
and most dumped them in October-November 2024. What's left is a pure-play roll-up vehicle with dry powder
equal to ~17% of market cap chasing a $70k-property TAM.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: SITE Centers Corp. (SITC)
• Spin date: 2024-10-01 (~6.5 months ago)
• Distribution ratio: 2 CURB shares per 1 SITC share
• Initial trading: opened around $22 on 10/1/24; currently $28.23
• Key reasons for forced selling: SITC was a yield/value REIT held by dividend funds; CURB launched with a
token initial dividend, no index membership, sub-$3B mcap, and a "growth REIT" profile that was the polar
opposite of its parent's holder base
ANALYST SUMMARY
Curbline owns and acquires "convenience" strip centers — small (typically <50,000 sq ft) unanchored or
shadow-anchored centers on high-traffic corridors, leased to service tenants (quick-serve restaurants, salons,
medical, financial services). At spin it owned 79 properties, almost entirely in wealthy Sun Belt and coastal
suburban submarkets contributed by SITC. The company was launched with roughly $500M of net cash and an

-- 264 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 47
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
undrawn credit facility — capitalized for offense rather than survival. Initial occupancy was in the mid-90s with
positive leasing spreads. Management (David Lukes, Conor Fennerty — formerly the SITC team) explicitly
framed the spin as a vehicle to roll up a fragmented niche that traditional shopping-center REITs ignore because
the asset size is too small to move the needle.
The bear case is straightforward: at ~23x AFFO and a ~3.5% initial yield, CURB optically screens as expensive
vs. peers like Kimco (KIM) or Regency (REG) at high-teens AFFO multiples. Cap rates on convenience centers
have compressed as private buyers have figured out the niche, so accretion math depends on disciplined sourcing.
Tenant base skews to small-business operators that can be cyclical, and a ~$3B mcap can quickly raise more
equity (dilutive if not deployed at spreads). Sponsorship risk: insiders are essentially the same SITC management
whose track record at the parent is mixed.
The bull case rewards the optionality. Adjusted for ~$500M net cash, the operating multiple is closer to
high-teens AFFO — in line with peers but on a higher-growth, lower-leverage vehicle. Each $200-300M of
acquisitions at 7-8% cap rates funded by 5-6% cost of capital drives meaningful AFFO/share growth;
management telegraphed an annualized acquisition pace of ~$500M+. Once seasoning passes, RMZ/MSCI REIT
index inclusion creates a passive bid. A first material dividend hike alongside a 2025 investor day would re-rate
the equity toward the "growth REIT" cohort (CUBE, EXR, REXR style multiples).
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 details that matter: (1) the spin was structured as a tax-free distribution AFTER SITC sold roughly $1.7B
of "non-Curbline" assets and pushed the proceeds into CURB as cash — meaning CURB launched debt-free with
the proceeds of SITC's 2023-24 disposition program, an unusual and very founder-friendly capitalization. (2)
Tenant base in the contributed portfolio is heavily service-oriented (food service, beauty, medical, financial), with
the top-10 tenants under 20% of ABR — far less concentration than typical strip-center REITs. (3) There is a
transition services agreement with SITC for accounting and IT (limited duration, ~12 months) but no long-term
operational dependency — CURB took the operating bench with it. (4) Management compensation is heavily
skewed to AFFO-per-share growth and TSR, aligning with the deployment thesis.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Quarterly acquisition volume disclosure (target: $500M+ annualized)
• First material dividend increase, likely with Q4 2025 earnings
• RMZ / MSCI US REIT Index inclusion once 12-month seasoning passes (Oct 2025)
• Initial Street coverage expansion beyond the 4-5 existing analysts
• Possible secondary equity raise once cash is largely deployed (could be late 2025/2026)
MUNGER CHECKLIST

SPINOFF (from TC Energy) | Spin Date: 2024-10-01 | Score: 76/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500
THE SETUP
South Bow is the Keystone liquids pipeline system spun out of TC Energy (TRP) — a ~94%-contracted,
take-or-pay heavy-crude system that for years was buried inside a Canadian gas-utility conglomerate. TRP
holders bought TRP for natural-gas utility yield and reflexively dumped SOBO on receipt; meanwhile SOBO is
too Canadian for US MLP indices and not in core Canadian dividend ETFs, creating dual orphan status. Result:
~7% yield on contracted infrastructure trading at a discount to US C-corp pipeline peers.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: TC Energy Corporation (TRP)
• Spin date: 2024-10-01 (~6.5 months ago)
• Distribution ratio: 1 SOBO share per 5 TRP shares
• Initial trading: opened around $26-28 USD on 10/2/24; currently ~$32
• Key reasons for forced selling: TRP held by Canadian utility/dividend funds wanting natural-gas
regulated-utility exposure, not crude pipelines; Keystone leak history (2022 Milepost 14 release in Kansas)
created litigation/ESG overhang; Canadian domicile + NYSE listing kept SOBO out of both US MLP/utility
indices and many Canadian income funds
ANALYST SUMMARY
South Bow operates the Keystone Pipeline System — the 4,900km crude oil pipeline running from Hardisty,
Alberta to Cushing, Oklahoma and the US Gulf Coast — plus the Marketlink system (Cushing to Houston/Port
Arthur) and the Houston terminal complex. Importantly, the spin assets exclude Keystone XL (the cancelled

-- 267 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 50
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
segment whose 2021 termination drove a multi-billion impairment at TRP). What's left is a fully built-out,
in-service pipeline with long-term Transportation Service Agreements (TSAs) and Minimum Volume
Commitments (MVCs) covering the vast majority of throughput. Management has guided to ~94% contracted
revenue with weighted-average contract life north of seven years, plus a substantial book of contracts with
30-year tails on certain MVCs. Initial annual EBITDA guidance was approximately $1.0B USD, with
maintenance capex modest and growth capex disciplined.
The bear case: Keystone is a single asset with concentrated reputational risk — the December 2022 Mill Creek
spill dumped 14,000 barrels in Kansas and remains in litigation/regulatory remediation. Heavy crude differentials
and Canadian production growth (TMX expansion now in service) could divert volumes. SOBO inherited
substantial debt from TRP — net debt around $4.8B against ~$1B EBITDA, a 4.5-5x leverage ratio that's normal
for midstream but limits dividend growth. The 2025 Cushing-area capacity story is mature; throughput growth
from here depends on Western Canadian heavy oil supply, which faces Trans Mountain competition.
The bull case: contracted cash flows are utility-like, and the dividend (initially USD $2.00 annualized, ~6-7%
yield) is well-covered with a low-60s payout ratio on DCF. Trans Mountain reaching capacity actually reinforces
Keystone's value as the only other major egress option to the US Gulf. Each clean operating quarter compresses
the discount vs. C-corp midstream peers (PAA, ENB, PPL) trading at 10-12x EV/EBITDA while SOBO is closer
to 8-9x. Settlement of historical leak liabilities removes overhang. As US dividend funds/closet MLP investors
discover an unlevered-by-MLP-K1-headache 7% yielder, multiple compression toward peers is plausible.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN't SEE
Form 10 details: (1) the inter-co debt allocation — TRP loaded SOBO with roughly USD $4.8B of debt at
separation, sized to maintain investment-grade ratings (BBB area) and to send cash back to the parent; this is a
classic "parent dumps debt on the cleaner asset" mechanic but the cash flow comfortably services it. (2) Transition
Services Agreements with TRP cover IT, treasury, and certain accounting functions for up to 24 months — South
Bow is building these in-house but the headline G&A; run-rate will normalize higher in 2025-26. (3) Cross-border
tax structuring: SOBO is a Canadian corporation paying its dividend in USD with a 15% Canadian withholding
tax for US holders (recoverable in taxable accounts via foreign tax credit, lost in IRAs unless held below the
IRA-treaty threshold). This nuance alone keeps many US retail income investors away. (4) The Keystone XL
cancellation suit settlement and remaining environmental remediation costs are quantified in the risk factors at
modest levels.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Q1 and Q2 2025 earnings demonstrating clean operations and contracted cash flow stability
• First post-spin dividend increase (likely 3-5% raise alongside 2025 guidance)
• Resolution / quantification of remaining 2022 Mill Creek spill liabilities
• Sanctioning of any Blackrod or Hardisty-area terminal expansion
• Index/ETF inclusion as US dividend funds add to position once seasoning passes
MUNGER CHECKLIST

utility-like

regulated-utility holdco). MDU's shareholder base — utility income funds — sold the cyclical construction stub

data-center mix, and direct exposure to grid-modernization and IIJA spending — i.e., the classic "boring parent
dumps high-growth stub onto income investors who won't touch it" setup.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: MDU Resources Group (MDU)
• Spin date: 2024-10-31 / 2024-11-01 (~5.5 months ago)
• Distribution ratio: 1 ECG share per 4 MDU shares
• Initial trading: opened around $59 on 11/1/24; currently ~$129 (more than doubled)
• Key reasons for forced selling: MDU is a utility/dividend holdco — institutional and retail income holders sold
cyclical construction reflexively; ECG launched with no dividend; sub-scale (~$3B mcap at spin) relative to peers

ANALYST SUMMARY
Everus Construction Group operates two segments: Electrical & Mechanical (E&M;) — commercial,
institutional, industrial, and data-center construction services — and Transmission & Distribution (T&D;) —
overhead/underground utility line work and substations. MDU built the business via 30+ years of bolt-on

-- 270 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 53
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
growing toward $2.8B. Critically, the data-center work mix has expanded rapidly inside E&M; as hyperscaler
buildouts ramped through 2024 — this segment carries margins above the legacy commercial mix and provides
multi-year contracted visibility. The company spun with minimal debt (no parent debt push-down), modest
pension obligations, and a clean balance sheet enabling tuck-in M&A.;
The bear case: this is fundamentally a cyclical specialty contractor exposed to project-level execution risk, surety
bonding, and labor cost inflation. EBITDA margins in the 5-7% range are structurally lower than IT services or
software; a single mis-bid project can wipe out a quarter. The stock has already more than doubled since spin, so
the easy "forced-selling discount" trade is largely played out — at ~$129 vs. $59 at open, the multiple is no longer
screaming cheap. Data-center capex is at cyclical highs and a hyperscaler pause (or AI capex digestion) would
compress backlog growth quickly. Comparable peers like MTZ, PRIM, and EME have run with the same theme.

multiples. Index inclusion (S&P; MidCap 400, Russell 1000 reweights) once 12-month seasoning passes adds a

balance sheet plus ~$200M+ of cash means ECG has dry powder for tuck-in acquisitions in a fragmented
contractor universe. (2) Customer concentration disclosed in the Form 10 shows no single customer above ~10%
of revenue, but the top-10 includes hyperscalers and major utility customers — meaning data-center mix is real
and contracted, not just project-by-project bidding. (3) Transition Services Agreement with MDU is short-dated
(~12-18 months) for IT, payroll, treasury — minimal long-term parent dependency. (4) The Form 10 explicitly
references "sponsor M&A;" as a use of capital and management compensation is heavily weighted toward
EBITDA growth and TSR — strong alignment with the deployment thesis.

• Quarterly backlog disclosures with explicit data-center mix breakout

• First tuck-in M&A; using clean balance sheet
• Index inclusion (S&P; MidCap 400 candidate) post 12-month seasoning (Nov 2025)
• Initiation expansion from construction-services analysts at major banks
• Possible initiation of a token dividend or buyback authorization in 2025

Magnera produces engineered materials and nonwovens used primarily in hygiene products (baby diapers, adult
incontinence, feminine care), wipes, healthcare/medical, food packaging, and specialty industrial applications.
The combined company runs roughly 45 facilities globally with leadership positions in spunmelt nonwovens,
airlaid (wipes substrate), and specialty papers. Pro-forma revenue is approximately $3.0B with EBITDA around

working capital release and footprint rationalization. Customers include the global hygiene majors — Procter &

concentration risk.
The bear case is meaningful: leverage is high at roughly 4.5-5x net debt / EBITDA, refinancing risk exists with a
chunk of debt at high coupons coming due in 2027-2029, and Glatfelter's pre-merger track record of operational
issues (Spezialpapiere fire, cost overruns) leaves a stigma. Customer concentration with P&G; and
Kimberly-Clark (collectively likely >25% of revenue) creates pricing pressure as those customers continually
re-bid contracts. Nonwovens is commoditized at the low end and faces low-cost Asian competition. EBITDA
margins in the low teens are not impressive vs. specialty packaging peers in the high teens. A delay in synergy
realization or a working-capital surprise would tip the leverage ratio into ratings-downgrade territory.
The bull case rests on three legs: (1) cost synergies — only ~30% realized at spin, with the bulk of plant
rationalization and procurement savings landing in 2025-26 and dropping straight to EBITDA; (2) deleveraging
— every $50M of debt paydown materially shrinks equity risk and unlocks multiple expansion at this leverage
level; (3) refi optionality — current high-coupon debt (8%+ on portions) could refinance 200+ bps lower in a
stable rate environment, adding meaningful FCF. EV/EBITDA below 6x vs. specialty packaging peers (BERY
itself, SEE, AMCR) at 9-11x leaves significant re-rating room. With ~$50M of EBITDA improvement and a
1-turn multiple expansion, equity could double from current levels.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10/S-4 details: (1) Capital structure — Berry pushed approximately $1.5-1.7B of debt down into MAGN at
separation (the classic "RMT debt dump") in the form of senior secured notes and term loans; the cash transfer
back to Berry was the entire economic point of the structure for BERY shareholders and explains the leverage. (2)
Customer contracts with the hygiene majors are typically 2-3 year MSAs with quarterly volume true-ups; pricing
typically passes through resin (polypropylene) costs with a 1-quarter lag, which protects margins on the way up
but can squeeze on the way down. (3) Transition Services Agreement with Berry covers shared services for up to
24 months — G&A; run-rate will normalize higher in 2025-26 as MAGN stands up standalone systems. (4)
Glatfelter's prior environmental remediation liabilities (legacy paper mills) transferred into MAGN — disclosed
in risk factors at modest amounts but worth monitoring.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Quarterly synergy realization updates (target: $55M+ run-rate by end of 2026)
• Refinancing of post-spin debt at lower coupons (likely opportunistic in 2025-26)
• Divestiture of non-core mills (specialty paper assets ex-Glatfelter)
• First analyst initiations from packaging coverage teams at major banks
• Working capital release in 2025 driving FCF inflection
• Possible activist involvement given micro-cap + cheap valuation + clear self-help
MUNGER CHECKLIST

SPINOFF (from Select Medical) | Spin Date: 2024-11-26 | Score: 79/100 | POSITION SIZE: $2,500
THE SETUP
Concentra is the largest occupational-health platform in the US (549 medical centers + 156 onsite employer
clinics) — a high-margin, recession-resistant B2B niche that was buried inside Select Medical's hospital-multiple
discount. The separation was unusual: a partial IPO in July 2024 followed by the November 2024 distribution of
SEM's remaining ~80% stake, creating two waves of forced selling. SEM holders own SEM for inpatient rehab
and acute care, not workers'-comp clinics — so the spun shares went straight to the bid.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: Select Medical Corporation (SEM)
• Spin date: 2024-11-26 (~5 months ago); preceded by IPO July 26, 2024 at $23.50
• Distribution ratio: ~1.05 CON shares per SEM share at distribution
• Initial trading: IPO'd July 2024 at $23.50; opened post-distribution around $20-21; currently ~$23
• Key reasons for forced selling: (1) two-step IPO + spin structure created two distinct forced-selling waves; (2)
SEM holders bought SEM for inpatient rehab/critical care, not outpatient occ-health; (3) boring B2B niche means
no retail interest; (4) limited bulge-bracket coverage relative to the $2.9B mcap; (5) carve-out IPO leakage to fund
SEM dividend pre-spin
ANALYST SUMMARY
Concentra operates the largest network of occupational-health centers in the US — 549 medical centers across 41
states plus 156 onsite clinics located inside employer facilities (manufacturing plants, distribution centers,
corporate campuses). The business is dominated by two recurring revenue streams: workers'-compensation injury

-- 276 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 59
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
care (treating job injuries, billed to employers' workers'-comp carriers) and employer services (DOT physicals for
commercial drivers, drug screens, pre-employment physicals, vaccinations, etc.). Approximately 75-80% of
revenue comes from employer-paid services rather than insurance reimbursement, which means pricing is
negotiated B2B on annual contracts rather than dictated by Medicare/commercial payer rate schedules. Revenue
is approximately $1.8B with adjusted EBITDA margins in the high teens. The company serves over 200,000
employer clients with no single client above ~3% of revenue.
The bear case: workers'-comp medical spending has been in secular decline as US workplace injuries (especially
in industrial sectors that are shrinking as a share of GDP) have trended down for decades. DOT physicals are
stable but not a growth market. Same-clinic visit growth has been low-single-digit for years; the growth story
depends on M&A; roll-up of independent operators and onsite-clinic expansion, both of which require execution.
The company carries meaningful debt from the spin (~$1.5B in term loans/notes) — leverage is around 3.5x
EBITDA, manageable but limits dividend growth. Carve-out IPO comp structures often unwind in the first 12-18
months as management turns over.
The bull case: this is a utility-like, hyper-recurring revenue business at a healthcare-services-discount multiple.
DOT physical mandates and workers'-comp regulatory requirements create non-discretionary, regulation-driven
demand. Margin profile is mid-teens EBITDA with modest capex (~3% of revenue) — strong FCF conversion.
The TAM for onsite clinics is vast and growing as large employers consolidate health benefits with single
vendors. CON is the only public pure-play in occupational health, making it the de facto consolidator in a highly
fragmented market (~10,000+ independent occ-health practices). At 8-9x EV/EBITDA vs healthcare-services
peers (USPH, ACHC, ENSG) at 11-13x, the discount is real and the catalyst path (M&A;, onsite wins, first
investor day) is concrete.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10/S-1 details: (1) Capital structure — Concentra raised approximately $585M in IPO proceeds in July
2024, of which roughly $523M was used to repay intercompany debt to SEM (effectively a dividend recap), then
issued ~$1.5B of senior notes and term loans pre-distribution, also largely upstreamed to SEM; this is a classic
"parent strips cash from spin" mechanic that explains why the leverage is what it is. (2) Long-term agreements
with SEM include shared services (IT, certain HR, real estate) for up to 24 months at cost-plus pricing; the
headline G&A; run-rate will normalize as Concentra stands up standalone functions. (3) Workers'-comp
regulatory framework is state-by-state — about 25 states have "employer choice" of provider (favorable to CON)
vs ~25 with "employee choice" (less favorable); this granularity is in the risk factors and matters for the M&A;
geographic strategy. (4) Top employer clients include Fortune-100 manufacturers and logistics companies;
200,000+ clients with no single client >3% — true diversification. (5) Management holds meaningful equity
post-spin and the comp plan is heavily weighted to EBITDA growth and same-center volume.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Quarterly same-clinic visit growth disclosure (key sentiment driver)
• Onsite-clinic enterprise wins (announced employer contracts, especially Fortune-500 wins)
• M&A; roll-up of independent occ-health clinics (target: $50-100M of revenue per year)
• First post-spin investor day (likely H2 2025)
• Index inclusion candidate (S&P; MidCap 400) once 12-month seasoning passes (Nov 2025)
• Dividend initiation or buyback authorization

THE SETUP
A $14B-revenue, $47B-backlog government services giant trading at a 3-4 turn EBITDA discount to LDOS/SAIC
purely because (a) it was born from a Reverse Morris Trust merger that confused the screens, (b) PE-sponsor

• Parent: Jacobs Solutions (NYSE: J)

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 62
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
• Forced selling: (1) Jacobs index funds dumped a non-engineering, government-services stub that didn't fit J's
mandate; (2) PE secondary overhang from two sponsors with a clock; (3) defense-services PMs who anchor on
LDOS/SAIC's cleaner balance sheets passed on the leverage.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Amentum is the post-merger combination of Jacobs' Critical Mission Solutions plus parts of its Cyber &
Intelligence unit with the legacy Amentum business — yielding the second-largest pure-play federal services
contractor behind Leidos. End-markets are DoD platform sustainment, DoE environmental management
(Hanford, Savannah River), intelligence community digital modernization, NASA space ops, and overseas base
operations. Q1 FY26 revenue was $3.2B with adjusted EBITDA of $263M (8.1% margin) and adjusted diluted
EPS of $0.54 (+6% y/y). Backlog hit $47.2B (~3.3 years of revenue), and book-to-bill remained >1.0x. The
strategic logic of the spin was clean: Jacobs wanted to be a pure higher-margin engineering & consulting firm;
CMS+C&I; fit better merged with Amentum where scale unlocks larger IDIQ awards. PE sponsors gained a
public exit path.
Bear case: federal services is a low-margin (~8%), low-growth, contract-recompete business where a single lost
re-bid (e.g., Hanford Mission Essential Services Contract) can vaporize a year of EBITDA. The opening 4.0x
leverage limits buyback / M&A; optionality and means a defense-budget hiccup hits equity hardest.
Continuing-resolution noise and a recent shutdown drove a Q1 revenue miss that the company had to talk
through. The PE overhang is real — two sponsors have clear motive to monetize, and every secondary is a price
ceiling. Government-services peers trade at 11-13x EBITDA partly because they earn it with cleaner balance
sheets and longer track records as standalones; AMTM's history as a standalone public company is barely a year,
and pro-forma "core" margins are still being argued. Reverse Morris Trust deals also carry tax-protection
covenants (no >50% change-of-control window) that constrain strategic flexibility for ~2 years post-spin.
Bull case: the leverage delta is the entire story. From ~4.0x to 3.4x in a year, with management guiding <3x by
FY26 year-end. Each turn of deleveraging at peer multiples is roughly $10-12 of stock. The Moody's upgrade has
already lowered cost of debt. Backlog of $47B against $13-14B revenue is one of the best book-to-bill setups in
federal services. Catalysts are tight and dated: quarterly leverage updates, the inevitable PE secondary that "clears
the deck," and a likely S&P; MidCap promotion once the float settles and sponsor stakes drop below thresholds.
Truist's $42 PT (implying ~50% upside from $27.58) assumes simply closing half the gap to LDOS on
EV/EBITDA. Government services is also one of the most administration-agnostic federal spending buckets —
DoE cleanup, IC modernization, base ops survive every budget regime.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 / S-4 specifics screens miss: (1) The $911M pre-spin cash payment from CMS to Jacobs is the entire
reason the balance sheet looks scary — it was financing-driven, not operational. (2) The 4.5% contingent share
escrow tied to FY24 operating-profit targets means real share count is fluid; pro-forma EPS denominators differ
across data feeds. (3) Reverse Morris Trust tax-protection covenants prohibit any transaction that would cause J
shareholders to own <50% of AMTM for two years — explicitly blocking change-of-control until late 2026. (4)
Transition Services Agreement with Jacobs covers IT, accounting, treasury and HR, with stand-up costs running
through "separation expense" lines — these obscure ~$50-80M of recurring opex once normalized. (5) The PE
sponsors hold registration rights and have a clear playbook: marketed secondaries every 2-3 quarters until they're
out. Every secondary is a discrete catalyst — the price-discovery moments are PRINTED, then the overhang lifts.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
