SPINOFF (from Howard Hughes Holdings, HHH) | Spin Date: 2024-08-01 | Score: 71/100 |
POSITION SIZE: $2,750
Tier: SPINOFF — FORCED SELLING
THE SETUP
Classic micro-cap orphan: ~$290M market cap holding the Seaport district in lower Manhattan, the 35-acre
Tropicana Las Vegas land assemblage, the Las Vegas Aviators (Triple-A baseball + ballpark), and a few minority
interests — assets that took Howard Hughes literally decades to assemble and would be impossible to replicate.
HHH holders received 1 SEG per 9 HHH and dumped instantly. With 250 Water Street monetized at $143M in
February 2026 and variable-rate debt repaid (no meaningful maturities until 2038), the operating company finally
has a clean balance sheet to work with.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: Howard Hughes Holdings (NYSE: HHH)
• Spin date: August 1, 2024 — ~21 months old
• Distribution ratio: 1 share SEG per 9 HHH shares
• Initial trading: opened in the high-$20s; current ~$22.71 (52-week range $16.52-$28.34)
• Pershing Square / Bill Ackman: largest institutional holder at ~5.02M shares (~$99M, ~40% of institutional
float) via Pershing Square Capital
• Forced selling reasons: (1) NYSE-American listing means no S&P; or Russell inclusion → mechanical
index-fund dump on Day 1; (2) tiny absolute size ($290M) — instantly below threshold for nearly every
institutional mandate; (3) operating losses with no near-term path to GAAP profitability scared off retail; (4)

-- 282 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 65
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
HHH itself is a real-estate name, not entertainment — wrong-shareholder problem; (5) economic dilution to HHH
was small enough that few HHH PMs even bothered with diligence.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Seaport Entertainment is the orphaned entertainment, hospitality and sports stub Howard Hughes carved out so
HHH could be a pure master-planned-community real-estate company. The crown jewels: (1) the Seaport historic
district in lower Manhattan — Pier 17, the Tin Building food hall (with chef Jean-Georges), and surrounding
street-level retail; (2) the Las Vegas Aviators Triple-A baseball franchise plus the Las Vegas Ballpark; (3) air
rights and undeveloped acreage at the former Tropicana site on the Las Vegas Strip (where MLB's Athletics are
building their new stadium next door, with construction underway); (4) various minority interests including the
Jean-Georges restaurant group stake. Q4 2025 revenue was $29.5M (+7% y/y pro-forma) with a $36.9M net loss.
The post-spin balance sheet was scary; after the $143M sale of 250 Water Street to Tavros in February 2026, the
company netted ~$76M and crucially repaid all variable-rate debt — no meaningful maturities until 2038.
Bear case: Seaport district has been a money-losing project for HHH for a decade — Pier 17 concerts and the Tin
Building have never produced sustainable cash flow, and management has been "two years from profitability"
since the original Howard Hughes carve-out from General Growth in 2010. The Las Vegas Aviators are a single
Triple-A franchise — fun but hardly transformational economics. The Tropicana land sits unmonetized and
entitled-but-undeveloped; an air-rights JV requires the right counterparty and capital. Operating losses run
$60-100M annually with no clear inflection. NYSE-American listing means the stock will never get index-fund
support, capping multiple expansion. Micro-cap means a single bad quarter triggers a stop-loss cascade. Ackman's
stake is supportive but he's not actively pushing — Pershing Square has many fish to fry.
Bull case: a real sum-of-parts gets you $40-50/share, easily double the current price. Tropicana land alone (35
acres on the Strip, adjacent to the Athletics stadium under construction) is worth $300-500M on plausible per-acre
Strip comparables — that's the entire current market cap. The Aviators + Ballpark is a $80-150M asset by recent
MiLB transaction comps with stadium real estate. Seaport district at any reasonable cap rate is another
$200-400M. Net debt is now manageable post-250-Water sale. The 2038 debt maturity wall buys management 14
years to monetize. Catalysts are concrete: a Tropicana JV or sale, accelerated Aviators monetization as Las Vegas
Strip foot traffic compounds with the A's stadium opening, and the optionality of Ackman pushing for a sale or
strategic transaction. NAV-discount closing alone is the trade.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 / 10-K specifics: (1) Howard Hughes seeded SEG with $25M cash plus ~$60M from a rights offering
Pershing Square backstopped — the rights offering was the FORCED SELLING mechanism reverse-engineered
(Pershing got more shares cheap, signaling commitment). (2) The Tropicana land is held with NO debt — the
air-rights value is "free" on the balance sheet; cap-rate analysis gives no credit. (3) Transition Services Agreement
with HHH is short (12 months) and largely unwound — no parent drag. (4) Board includes Anton Nikodemus
(CEO, ex-MGM) and Pershing Square representation, signaling activist-friendly governance. (5) The 250 Water
Street sale didn't just bring cash — it eliminated the largest variable-rate debt facility, removing the only
near-term bankruptcy-risk pixel from the chart. (6) The Tin Building is a Jean-Georges JV — SEG's economics
from it are a profit-share, not gross revenue, masking unit economics on screens.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Tropicana site JV or outright sale (most likely H2 2026 as A's stadium construction progresses)
• Quarterly proof of operating-loss narrowing now that variable-rate debt is gone

First-ever publicly traded "land-banking REIT" — Lennar dropped $5.5B of land plus $1B of cash into Millrose,
distributed it to shareholders, and now MRP earns contracted option-fee yield as Lennar (and increasingly other
builders) takes down homesites on a just-in-time basis. Market is mis-pricing this as a homebuilder when it's
structurally a finance company with a secured-credit-style book. ~14 months post-spin, paying $0.76/quarter
($3.04 annualized) — a ~10% yield at the current price. BTIG initiated Buy at $35.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: Lennar Corporation (NYSE: LEN)
• Spin date: February 7, 2025 — ~14 months old
• Distribution ratio: pro-rata to LEN A and LEN B holders; Lennar retained ~20% (33.2M Class A shares,
non-voting initially) for subsequent disposition
• Initial trading vs current: opened ~$25-26 with heavy first-week selling pressure; current ~$31.03 — already
re-rated, but still cheap on yield
• Forced selling reasons: (1) LEN holders are homebuilder-equity investors with zero appetite for an
externally-managed REIT; (2) novel business model (no direct comp) made it un-modelable for LEN sell-side
analysts who simply marked it for distribution and moved on; (3) Kennedy Lewis external-management structure
carries fee-drag stigma that REIT-dedicated investors penalize; (4) early share distribution was un-indexed, so on
Day 1 every passive vehicle holding LEN had to sell SEG mechanically; (5) Lennar's residual 20% creates a
known supply overhang (subsequent spin/split-off/sale planned).

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 68
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
ANALYST SUMMARY
Millrose is the first publicly traded land-banking REIT. The structure: Lennar contributed ~$5.5B of homesite
land plus $1B cash and ~142,000 controlled homesites. MRP holds the land; Lennar (and now other
homebuilders, per the December 2025 exchange and onboarding announcements) pays MRP a monthly option fee
for the right to take down lots on a defined cadence. The economics resemble secured term lending: Lennar is
contractually committed to the takedown schedule, and the underlying land collateral is high quality. 2025 results:
$600.5M total revenue, $2.44 EPS, $2.58 AFFO/share, $5.5B deployed in new land, $3.4B takedown proceeds,
31,000+ homesite closings. Q4 dividend of $0.75 ($124.5M total) and Q1 2026 dividend of $0.76 ($126.2M) — a
clear sign of a 10-12% yield REIT that the market hasn't priced. Externally managed by Kennedy Lewis Land and
Residential Advisors at a 1.25% per annum fee on Tangible Assets. 2026 base case: grow invested capital outside
the Lennar Master Program Agreement by $2B, reaching ~$10.5B total with >40% non-Lennar.
Bear case: external management is a permanent ~125 bps drag and creates conflict-of-interest perception — every
dollar of asset growth pays Kennedy Lewis. The novel structure invites short-seller attention asking "why isn't
this just a homebuilder finance subsidiary?" Homebuilder cycle risk is fully embedded — if Lennar (or onboarded
peer builders) slows takedowns in a recession, MRP's revenue cliffs even though contracts are "binding" (in
practice, builders renegotiate). Lennar's residual 20% is overhang until cleared. Dividend is large but funded by
recycling principal as much as operating earnings — any stress on takedown cadence and the dividend gets cut,
triggering yield-investor exodus. REIT structure means raising equity to grow — secondary offerings are likely
and a price ceiling. No track record as a standalone — every quarter is the first of its kind to model. Direct-comp
absence cuts both ways: it can stay cheap because nobody knows what to mark it against.
Bull case: a 10-12% ROE recurring-fee compounder with a contracted, high-visibility revenue stream and an
aligned anchor customer (Lennar 20% + multi-year MPA). At ~$31 the stock yields ~9.8% in cash distributions
while growing the asset base. Onboarding non-Lennar builders is the surprise upside — every new builder is
incremental capital deployment at attractive spreads with no incremental fixed cost. December 2025 Lennar
exchange offer cleared part of the overhang; remaining stake is a known, manageable supply. As a 2026
first-full-year reporter, MRP gets discovered by REIT-dedicated investors who couldn't model it last year. BTIG's
$35 PT is a starting point; if the market re-rates it as a 7-8% yield REIT, the stock prints $40+. Index inclusion
(Russell, eventually MSCI REIT indices) is a 2026-2027 catalyst.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form S-11 / 10-K specifics: (1) Kennedy Lewis fee is 1.25% of Tangible Assets — at $10B+ assets, that's
$125M+ annually, paid externally. The conflict: Kennedy Lewis is incentivized to grow assets, not necessarily
ROE. (2) Lennar retains consent rights on the Manager until its asset contribution drops below 10% of total assets
for six consecutive months — meaning Lennar effectively governs the manager relationship for years. (3) Master
Program Agreement with Lennar specifies takedown windows, option fees, and pricing mechanics — this is the
load-bearing contract; a Material Adverse Change clause exists but is narrow. (4) Lennar's 33.2M Class A
residual shares are non-voting initially with a planned subsequent transaction (split-off, secondary, or sale) — a
known catalyst on a clock. (5) Most of the dividend is technically a return of capital that recycles as Lennar takes
down lots — distinguishing "true" yield from principal-return is critical and screens conflate them. (6) Tax-status
as REIT requires 90% distribution of taxable income — the $0.76 dividend is structural, not discretionary.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Q1 2026 results (May 6) — first full-year-as-public earnings cycle confirms the recurring-fee model
• Onboarding additional non-Lennar builders (already disclosed in pipeline) — each new contract is a discrete
print

SPINOFF (from Western Digital, WDC) | Spin Date: 2025-02-24 | Score: 78/100 | POSITION SIZE:
$1,750
Tier: SPINOFF — QUALITY-AT-DISCOUNT (post-rerate)
THE SETUP
Sandisk was carved out of Western Digital at the bottom of the NAND cycle in February 2025 at ~$28. The stock
has since printed >$900 — a ~30x move — driven by the AI-demand-driven NAND supercycle (bit demand
growing 20-22%, supply 15-17%) and the High Bandwidth Flash (HBF) co-development with SK Hynix. The
forced-selling thesis is mostly over; what remains is a cyclical-supercycle thesis where forward EV/EBITDA still
doesn't price a multi-year shortage extending to 2028. Position size cut deliberately because we're well past the
easy money.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: Western Digital (NASDAQ: WDC)
• Spin date: February 24, 2025 (separation effective Feb 21) — ~14 months old
• Distribution ratio: 1/3 share of SNDK for every 1 WDC share held
• Initial trading vs current: opened ~$48; ran to a $27.89 low in April 2025 (forced-selling flush); now $920+
(~30x off the lows) — recent ~$921
• Forced selling reasons: (1) WDC had announced plans to retire ~28.8M SNDK shares via debt-for-equity
exchanges, creating known overhang and incremental supply; (2) WDC index funds had to sell SNDK on Day 1
(different ticker, different sector classification); (3) NAND was at cycle bottom with negative gross margins
memory in NAND 2024 — anyone who could sell did; (4) WDC fundamental holders are HDD-cycle investors,
not NAND-cycle investors — completely different end-markets, customers, and pricing dynamics.

-- 288 of 771 --

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 71
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
ANALYST SUMMARY
Sandisk is the only US-listed pure-play NAND flash memory company post-separation. The business: NAND
wafer production via the long-running joint venture with Kioxia (Japan) — a structural cost-share arrangement
that lets both partners stay at the leading edge with half the capex burden — packaged into consumer SSDs (the
SanDisk and WD brands), enterprise/data-center SSDs, and embedded/automotive solutions. Q3 FY26 revenue
guide is $4.4-$4.8B with gross margin guidance of 65-67% — versus 26% in the prior-year quarter and a 7.1%
trough in FY23. The cycle has flipped: contract NAND prices were up 85-90% q/q, Goldman models +100-120%
NAND pricing in 2026, and the demand-supply gap (22% vs 16-17%) implies persistent shortage through 2028
per multiple sell-side desks. Free cash flow generation in H2 2025 was $1.2B, used to pay down
separation-inherited debt. The HBF (High Bandwidth Flash) co-development with SK Hynix targets 8-16x HBM
capacity at comparable bandwidth — if it ships H2 2026 it directly competes for AI-accelerator memory sockets.
Bear case: NAND is the most cyclical commodity in semiconductors. The peak-to-trough on gross margins has
historically been 50+ points. At 65%+ GM you are at-or-above prior cycle peaks; mean reversion alone is brutal.
Capex discipline lasts only as long as everyone is profitable — Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron and Kioxia all add
capacity into shortages, eventually flooding the market. The $1.165B installment payment to Kioxia (2026-2029)
is a real cash outflow tied to JV restructuring. HBF is a technology bet against entrenched HBM (Samsung/SK
Hynix DRAM stacking) — first-mover doesn't always win, and AI accelerators are tightly co-designed with
specific memory architectures. The stock has 30x'd in 14 months — every "AI-adjacent" momentum holder is in,
and any cycle wobble (China recession, Apple iPhone air pocket, hyperscaler capex pause) triggers a violent
reversal. NAND prices have historically over- and under-shot by 50%+ — picking the cycle top here is a coin
flip.
Bull case: even at $920, forward EV/EBITDA on FY26-FY27 numbers under sell-side cycle-peak assumptions is
below 6x — meaning the market still implicitly assumes mean reversion within 18 months. If the structural AI
shortage extends to 2028 (consensus among NAND-dedicated desks), three more years of peak earnings prints,
with FCF used to delever and buy back stock. HBF is the genuine wild card — if it captures even 10% of what
becomes a $50B+ AI memory market by 2028, that's a $5B/year revenue lever at gross margins north of 60%.
S&P; 500 inclusion is pending (already meets size and float thresholds) — a mechanical demand catalyst. Sandisk
brand has consumer pricing power that pure-merchant NAND players (Micron NAND, SK Hynix Solidigm) lack.
The Kioxia JV is the deepest moat — fab capacity that takes 3-5 years and $30B+ to replicate.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 / S-1A / TSA specifics: (1) The Kioxia JV is the entire cost-of-goods structure — Sandisk doesn't own
fabs outright, it shares them. Screens looking at "fab utilization" miss this. (2) The $1.165B installment payment
to Kioxia (2026-2029) shows up as committed contractual cash obligations, not opex — it's an artifact of the
separation/JV renewal. (3) Transition Services Agreement with WDC runs up to 18 months covering quality
assurance, IT, procurement, logistics, finance, HR, engineering, marketing, operations, sales, manufacturing,
R&D; — broad scope means standalone-cost emergence is just unwinding now (2026). (4) WDC's debt-for-equity
exchange plan with ~28.8M SNDK shares creates discrete overhang events — these are predictable and
discountable. (5) Board includes legacy WDC and JV-relationship members; CEO David Goeckeler is a known
semis operator. (6) HBF JDA with SK Hynix is structured as a joint development with ip-sharing on the
architecture — economics are partner-friendly but not exclusive.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• Quarterly contract NAND price prints (each one validates or breaks the supercycle thesis)
• HBF product roadmap milestones — sample shipments to AI-accelerator OEMs in H2 2026

THE SETUP
Classic small-cap spin set-up: IAC dumped Angi after years of trying to fix HomeAdvisor + Angi's List,
dual-class structure collapsed to one share/one vote, and IAC's index-fund holders sold without diligence. With
~$387M of cash on a ~$320M market cap, the enterprise value is approximately zero — you get the marketplace
business, the brand, and the operational reset for free. New CEO Jeff Kip + Joey Levin as Executive Chairman
provide focused leadership. Already repurchased ~10% of shares post-spin. 2026 expected return to revenue
growth and $145-150M adjusted EBITDA.
SPINOFF MECHANICS
• Parent: IAC Inc. (NASDAQ: IAC)
• Spin date: April 1, 2025 (record date March 25, 2025) — ~12 months old
• Distribution ratio: ~0.5251 shares of Angi Class A per IAC share
• Initial trading vs current: opened ~$8.50; current ~$7.40 (down modestly post-spin as the reset plays out)

activists or strategic buyers

pushed it below institutional minimums; (4) limited float plus prior years of operational pain made it un-coverable

by sell-side; (5) loss-making at the operating line scared off momentum funds.
ANALYST SUMMARY
Angi is the home-services marketplace combining HomeAdvisor and Angie's List, finally separated from IAC's
holdco complexity. The business: lead generation and matching between homeowners with home-improvement
projects (everything from plumbers to roofers) and contractor "pros" who pay subscription + per-lead fees. The
history is brutal — five-plus years of declining revenue, churn-heavy pro retention, lead-quality complaints, and a
series of strategic resets at IAC. New management under CEO Jeff Kip with Joey Levin as Executive Chairman
has explicitly pivoted from "lead volume" to "jobs done well" — killing unprofitable cohorts, raising lead quality
bars, and accepting near-term revenue decline (-12% to -16% in 2025) for sustainable margin recovery. 2025
guidance: $135-150M adjusted EBITDA, $55-100M operating income. 2026 guide: low-single-digit revenue
growth (1-3%), $145-150M adjusted EBITDA. Key cap-structure fact: ~$387M cash at the end of Q1 2025,
~$416M total liquidity, market cap ~$320M — enterprise value is approximately ZERO or negative. Already
executed a ~10% buyback in 2025.
Bear case: home-services lead generation is structurally challenged. Angi has been losing share to Google's Local
Services Ads, Yelp, Thumbtack, and direct-to-pro platforms for half a decade. AI-driven search disruption
(Perplexity, ChatGPT, Google AI Overviews) potentially commodifies the matching function — Angi's brand is
no defense if homeowners get answers from an LLM. Pro churn is the death-knell metric and there's no
compelling evidence it's permanently fixed. Operating losses persisted into spin; the EBITDA guidance includes
meaningful add-backs and depends on continued cost cuts that have a floor. Joey Levin signaled "I'm done with
this" by spinning it — the smartest operator in IAC's universe gave up. Micro-cap means no analyst coverage, no
liquidity, no path to multiple expansion without a transaction. The cash pile invites bad capital allocation — Angi
has a history of value-destructive M&A.;
Bull case: the EV-near-zero math is the entire trade. ~$300M+ of net cash on a ~$320M market cap means you're
paying nothing for a business that should generate $145M+ adjusted EBITDA in 2026 — implying
single-digit-million enterprise value against $145M EBITDA, an absurd disconnect if even half the operational
reset sticks. The buyback authorization is already aggressive and shrinking the float meaningfully. New CEO +
cleaner governance + no IAC drag = first time in years the team can focus. Optionality on AI-driven matching
efficiency is essentially free — if AI improves the lead-quality / job-completion rate, the whole flywheel
reignites. Strategic transaction is plausible: a private equity buyer looking at "broken consumer marketplace +
cash + sub-1x revenue" would pay double the current price; a Yelp / Thumbtack / Google could be acquirers.
Sentiment reset alone — going from "left for dead" to "stable + cash-rich + EBITDA-growing" — is worth
multiple turns of expansion.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Form 10 / 8-K / proxy specifics: (1) The dual-class collapse to one-share/one-vote at spin is the SINGLE most
underappreciated detail — every prior activist push was blocked by IAC's super-voting; that defense is GONE,
making strategic transactions immediately viable. (2) Joey Levin moved from IAC CEO to Angi Executive
Chairman — when one of the smartest holdco operators puts on an executive hat at the orphan, the signal value is
enormous. (3) Cash distributed at separation gives Angi ~$387M cash; the parent took the haircut, not the spinco.
(4) Tax-free distribution under Section 355 means no overhang from tax liability. (5) Buyback already aggressive
— 10% of shares retired in months — implies management views EV as undervalued. (6) Pro-retention metrics
are the load-bearing KPI; quarterly disclosure of cohort retention is the gating signal for thesis confirmation. (7)
No meaningful debt at separation — pristine balance sheet is rare for a "broken" spinco.

WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K reveals the new credit agreement is meaningfully friendlier than what existed inside Lionsgate: a
$300M senior secured Term Loan A plus $150M revolver at SOFR+300, four-year tenor, covenants that loosen
with deleveraging. That gives Hirsch optionality to do tuck-in M&A; or self-tender if the equity stays below $20.
The 5.5% senior notes are pre-payable and cheaper than Starz could refinance today — an embedded option.
Filings also confirm Starz is now a Canadian domicile, which lowers the effective tax rate and complicates any
U.S. acquirer's structuring (suggesting a sale would likely come with a control premium to overcome friction).

The largest cement, ready-mix and roofing supplier in North America was just spun out of a Swiss parent whose
European holders sold on receipt because they wanted EU/EM exposure, not U.S. construction. AMRZ generated
$1.5B of free cash flow in 2025, posted 1.1x net leverage at year-end, and management proposed a $1B buyback.
At ~9x EBITDA vs. U.S. pure-play peers (MLM, VMC, EXP, SUM) at 13-18x, the dual-listing index orphan

margin), free cash flow $1.7B, with 13%/16%/15% three-year CAGRs respectively. The company emerged with a

have already monetized via re-rating. Building-envelope (roofing) is now ~25% of profit and margin profile
resembles ROOF/IBP rather than a cement bag, which sell-side cement analysts systematically under-value.

across more than 200 communities, on the back of $4.7B of cumulative network investment over 45 years. 2025

Service Fund mechanism) — quasi-guaranteed revenue not visible in any peer screen. The 200+ community
footprint includes proprietary subsea fiber (TERRA) that no competitor can replicate; replacement cost easily
exceeds the current EV.

The freshest spin in the batch — Honeywell's high-margin specialty materials business (refrigerants, electronic
materials, advanced fibers) carved out at $3.9B sales and 25%+ EBITDA margins, with the European Patent
Office reaffirming the foundational HFO-1234yf process patent in February 2026 (a key bear-case overhang
flipped). At ~6 months seasoned the float is still re-pricing, generalist coverage is sparse, and the 1-for-4

Electronic & Specialty Materials (~28%). The R&AS; segment makes the Solstice family of HFO low-GWP

1,000+ process and use patents — meaning even after composition patents expire, the manufacturing process and

advanced fibers (used in body armor, ropes, ballistic composites) is reported inside ES&M; but is functionally a
defense/industrial business with totally different cycle dynamics — sell-side chemicals analysts mis-classify it.
Lastly, the $1B note coupon of 5.625% due 2033 is meaningfully cheaper than where SOLS could refinance today
on a standalone basis — embedded value.

ANALYST SUMMARY
Tripadvisor operates three segments: Brand Tripadvisor (legacy hotel meta-search/reviews), Viator (experiences
booking — fastest growing OTA category globally), and TheFork (European restaurant booking, ~70k
restaurants). Viator is the crown jewel — high-growth, asset-light, taking share from GetYourGuide and Klook.
TheFork is the European #1 in restaurant bookings. Starboard targeted TRIP because the public market values the
consolidated entity at ~1x revenue when peer experience platforms (Airbnb Experiences embed, Klook private
rounds) trade at 4-6x, and standalone TheFork comps (Booking's OpenTable peer set) suggest $700M-$1B value
alone.
Bear case: Liberty TripAdvisor (LTRPA) holds a ~28% supervoting stake that effectively blocks any
change-of-control without Greg Maffei's blessing — historically Liberty has held assets long after they should
have been monetized. Hotel meta-search is in secular decline as Booking and Expedia squeeze ad pricing and
Google grabs share with free hotel modules. Viator faces tightening competition from Airbnb Experiences
relaunch and Klook's IPO war chest. TheFork has never generated material EBITDA. A messy Liberty unwind
could push any sale 18-24 months out, during which core revenue keeps eroding.
Bull case: Starboard's settlement is the unlock. Two directors are in, two more on deck, and a formal TheFork
review means a process is committed — not aspirational. Liberty's own structure is rumored to be undergoing
simplification (Maffei stepped back from Liberty Media chair), which could remove the supervoting overhang.
Viator alone, growing 20%+ at scale, is worth $1.5-2B in a strategic sale to Booking, Expedia, or Airbnb. If
TheFork sells for $500M-$700M and Viator goes for $1.5-2B, residual hotel meta is essentially free option value
at current prices. Path to $18-22 over 12-18 months.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The two Starboard-aligned directors are not random — Andrew Cates is a former IAC/Match operator and Dhiren
Fonseca is ex-Expedia leadership; both have lived inside the OTA / travel meta-search structure and know exactly
which assets are stranded. The board expansion to 10 (with two more slots reserved) is a textbook Starboard
staircase — they get to four directors before the 2026 AGM without a contested vote. Liberty's supervoting
structure is the single biggest screen-invisible factor: the Series B common stock (held by Liberty TripAdvisor)
carries 10 votes per share. Any deal needs Liberty alignment — but Liberty itself trades at a steep discount to its
TRIP stake, giving Maffei strong incentive to monetize.

• June 2026 annual meeting — slate confirmation
• TheFork strategic review outcome (mid-2026 expected)
• Two additional Starboard board nominees
• Liberty TripAdvisor governance restructuring / potential collapse
• Q2 2026 earnings — Viator booking volume disclosure

BILL Holdings provides AP/AR automation and embedded payments for ~480,000 small businesses, plus the
Divvy spend management platform and Invoice2go. The core thesis was always "QuickBooks for payments" —
automate B2B paper checks, take a clip on every transaction. Revenue growth has decelerated from 70%+ in 2021
to mid-teens, gross dollar volume growth slowed, and the float-income tailwind from rates is reversing. Starboard
targeted 	BILL 	precisely 	because 	the 	company 	executed 	the 	playbook 	of 	tech-bubble 	overspend
(Divvy/Invoice2go acquisitions at peak), got fat on float income, and now trades at trough multiples while still
generating ~$200M+ FCF and sitting on ~$1.6B cash.
Bear case: Embedded finance landscape is brutal — Stripe, Adyen, Brex, Ramp all expanding into BILL's lane;
banks (JPM, BofA) pushing free AP automation to retain SMB deposits. Take-rate compression is the existential
risk: even with volumes up, revenue per dollar of TPV is structurally falling. Float income headwind from Fed
cuts directly hits high-margin revenue. Strategic buyer pool is narrow — Intuit (regulatory issues), SAP (just
bought a competitor), Visa/MA (too small). Sale process could fail to clear at premium and Starboard ends up
with operational reset that takes 2+ years.
Bull case: Cash-rich balance sheet ($1.6B+ cash, manageable convert debt) means even at zero growth this prints
meaningful FCF yield. Starboard's pressure has already triggered cost discipline — opex growth slowing visibly.
PE buyers (Vista, Thoma, Silver Lake) are well-positioned for take-private at $55-65 given the cash + recurring
revenue stream. Even absent a sale, simply removing growth-at-any-cost spending and returning capital re-rates
the multiple. Re-rate path to $50-60 in 12 months on capital return alone; $65-75 on take-out.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
Founder/CEO Rene Lachapelle has gradually reduced his role and insider sales by management have been chunky
in 2024-2025 — a signal that incumbent leadership may not fight a sale. The Divvy acquisition (paid $2.5B in
stock in 2021) is essentially impaired but never written down — Starboard will likely pressure the board to mark
it. Customer concentration in mid-market accounting firms (Bench shutdown earlier showed channel risk) is
hidden in segment reporting. Float income — buried in "interest income on customer funds" — was ~$170M in
FY24 and falling fast as Fed cuts hit; this disclosure is the silent earnings shoe-drop.

• 2026 annual meeting director election (likely contested vote)
• Potential strategic review announcement
• Q1/Q2 FY26 earnings (volume inflection on Fed cuts)
• Cost reduction / capital return announcements
• Possible take-private bid from PE consortium

• Status: Pre-proxy escalation phase. Annual meeting in 2026 will be flashpoint. The Kelce angle generates

ANALYST SUMMARY
Six Flags Entertainment is the post-merger entity combining legacy Cedar Fair and Six Flags (closed July 2024).
It owns 42 amusement parks, water parks, and resort properties across North America — including flagship Cedar
Point, Knott's Berry Farm, Magic Mountain, and Six Flags Great Adventure. The merger thesis was $200M of
synergies, scale benefits in vendor purchasing, and combined seasons-pass cross-selling. Reality: integration has
been painful, attendance disappointing in 2025 summer season, ~$5.2B post-merger debt load is the elephant in
the room, and the stock has roundtripped to below the merger close. JANA targeted FUN because regional theme
parks remain a stable cash-flow asset class historically attractive to PE (Apollo previously owned Six Flags,
Blackstone owns Merlin Entertainments, KKR owns multiple regional parks globally).
Bear case: $5.2B of debt at ~6.5% blended cost = $340M+ annual interest, leaving thin equity cushion if 2026
attendance disappoints again. Weather risk is real (heat domes, hurricane season). Consumer discretionary stress
at low-end demographic (Six Flags Cleveland, NJ, Texas). PE buyers — particularly Apollo (former owner) —
were burned on Six Flags' last LBO and may not pay up. Strategic buyers (Disney, Comcast/Universal) don't need
42 regional parks. Without a buyer, JANA campaign devolves into operational fix that takes years and the debt
load compounds.
Bull case: Industry consolidation logic is powerful — combined SIX/FUN footprint covers ~50M+ unique annual
visitors and the season pass database is one of the most valuable assets in regional entertainment. PE sponsors
love stable park EBITDA (recurring season passes, in-park spend, real estate option value). Rate-cut cycle helps
the LBO math meaningfully. Travis Kelce ambassador + new marketing playbook can drive 5-10% attendance lift
on cultural relevance alone. At 8x 2026E EBITDA the stock is $30; at 10x it's $40. Even absent sale, operational
reset under JANA-friendly board gets to mid-$20s.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
JANA's choice of Glenn Murphy is a tell — Murphy was instrumental in the Frontier sale to Verizon and chairs
Lululemon; he is a "make it sellable" director, not an operator. Dave Habiger has board experience at multiple
PE-backed exits (Sonic Foundry, Echo Global, GoDaddy). Together they signal JANA is set up for a process, not
a long fight. The Kelce angle is more substantive than gimmick — Travis Kelce's Eras tour-style cultural reach is
exactly the demographic Six Flags has lost since 2019. The real estate underneath the parks is materially
undervalued on the balance sheet — at distressed sale, the land alone in Southern California / NJ / Ohio is worth
meaningful equity value. Apollo's prior ownership is the hidden asset of the bull case: they know the asset cold
and could move fast if invited.
CATALYSTS (next 6-12 months)
• 2026 annual meeting (proxy contest possible)
• Formal sale process announcement (most likely Q2/Q3 2026)
• Q2 2026 attendance data (summer season — make-or-break)
• Potential PE bid (Apollo, Blackstone, KKR all positioned)
• Refinancing of 2027/2028 debt maturities — forcing function

ANALYST SUMMARY
Fiserv is one of two global merchant-acquiring duopolists (with FIS) — operating Clover (SMB POS), Carat
(enterprise digital commerce), the merchant acquiring book from the First Data acquisition, and a vast core-bank
processing business serving thousands of community banks. Revenue base is ~$20B with mid-teens operating
margins. In 2025 the stock collapsed 67% on a combination of: (1) Argentina exposure pressure, (2) Clover
growth deceleration to mid-teens (vs market expecting 25%+), (3) competitive pressure from Stripe/Toast/Block
in SMB POS, and (4) credibility loss after multiple guidance cuts. JANA targeted FI because the assets remain
world-class — Clover alone is arguably worth $25-35B as a standalone — but management credibility and
conglomerate discount have crushed the multiple to a level that prices in continued execution failure.
Bear case: Payments processing has structural revenue-per-transaction compression as competition intensifies —
Stripe undercutting on enterprise, Toast winning restaurant SMB, Block winning micro-merchants. Clover's
growth deceleration may not be cyclical — it may be share loss. Argentina/LatAm exposure remains a tail risk.
Mike Lyons is a banker by background (ex-PNC President), not a payments operator — turnaround takes time
and could disappoint. The "One Fiserv" platform consolidation is a multi-year IT project with high execution risk.
Sub-$50 retest possible on next negative print.
Bull case: At ~12x EPS the stock prices in permanent multiple compression that doesn't match the asset quality.
Lyons brings discipline, focus, and willingness to divest non-core (the Output Solutions printing business and
parts of biller services are obvious carve-out candidates worth $3-5B). Capital return runway is enormous —
$4B+ annual FCF, modest leverage, share count down 30% over 5 years. JANA's friendly posture is a tell: they
don't make $150M bets on $30B caps without high conviction in the operator. If 2026 stabilizes and 2027 returns
to algo (8-12% EPS growth), multiple re-rates to 15-17x = $80-100. Carat IPO/spin-off is the moonshot — could
unlock $20B+ of latent value.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

administration SSA Commissioner) — the timing now looks especially well-marked. Mike Lyons' compensation
package (disclosed in 2025 proxy supplement) has aggressive 3-year stock price targets that imply management
believes $90+ is achievable. The "Output Solutions" business — printing and mailing financial statements for
banks — is a stranded legacy asset clearly destined for divestiture; JANA almost certainly identified this in their
work. The Clover business is reported as part of "Merchant Solutions" but standalone disclosure has improved
over the past year — setting the stage for a possible spin or tracking-stock structure that the market hasn't priced
in.

ANALYST SUMMARY
Avantor is the #2 global distributor of lab supplies, chemicals, and instruments to pharma/biotech, healthcare, and
education customers — competing principally with Thermo Fisher's Fisher Scientific channel. The business was
rolled up by New Mountain Capital and IPO'd in 2019; today does ~$6.7B revenue with mid-teens EBITDA
margins. Engine Capital targeted AVTR because: (1) margins are 400-500bps below Thermo's distribution
segment despite similar product mix, (2) the post-COVID destocking cycle in biopharma was particularly
punishing for Avantor's mid-stream position, (3) ~$5.5B of debt creates equity convexity if EBITDA inflects, and
(4) the Education and Services categories are obvious divestiture candidates worth several billion to focused
buyers (private label, education-only distributors).
Bear case: Lab supply distribution is a mature, low-growth, capital-intensive business with structural pricing
pressure from large pharma customers consolidating spend. Thermo's scale advantage compounds — their
integrated proprietary product portfolio (Gibco, Invitrogen, Applied Biosystems) gives them margin leverage
Avantor cannot match. The $5.5B debt load means a small EBITDA miss translates to large equity drawdown —
leverage cuts both ways. Cost programs are typically half as effective as announced. PE buyers (Carlyle, KKR,
Bain) all looked at AVTR previously and passed. New CEO is a strong operator but inherits a structurally
challenged asset.
Bull case: Biopharma capex cycle is bottoming — biotech funding has stabilized, large pharma is restarting R&D;
spend, and inventory destocking is largely complete. Operating leverage is enormous — every 100bps of margin
expansion = ~$300M of EBITDA = $2-3B of equity value at current multiples. Engine Capital's intrinsic value
bridge ($17-19 sale, $22-26 standalone) is anchored to peer multiples (Thermo distribution at 13-14x EBITDA vs
AVTR at 8-9x). Ligner's Cytiva pedigree is exactly right for the role — he ran a similarly structured business. PE
sponsors like Carlyle/KKR have done multiple lab supply deals (Catalent, IQVIA) and at $8 the equity check is
small enough to be digestible. Re-rate to $13-15 on operational milestones; $17-22 on sale outcome.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Engine Capital letter (August 2025) explicitly named the Education segment and the Pharma Services
category as divestiture candidates with named potential buyers — a tell that Engine has done the SOTP work and
identified actual willing acquirers, not just hypothetical. New CEO Ligner came directly from Cytiva, which
Danaher itself extracted enormous margin from (~30%+ EBITDA margins on a similar mid-stream lab business)
— the playbook is well-established. Insider buying by directors in late 2025 / early 2026 was modest but notable

act as forcing function — refinancing risk pressures the board to monetize. New Mountain Capital still owns a
meaningful stake from the IPO and is a natural ally for a sale process.

ANALYST SUMMARY
Monro operates ~1,200 company-owned auto-service stores across 32 states under Monro, Mr. Tire, Tire Choice,

service. Comp sales have been negative for multiple consecutive quarters as consumers defer discretionary
maintenance and tire OEMs lose pricing leverage. Stock at $17.87 trades at roughly 0.5x revenue and ~6-7x
trough EBITDA — vs. Driven Brands at higher multiples and Mavis Tire (private) reportedly transacting at 10x+.
Icahn's rationale: real estate-heavy footprint plus 1,200 service bays in a fragmented market are worth materially
more in private hands than the public-market quote.
Bear case: Monro's comp problem isn't cyclical — it's structural. Fleet electrification erodes oil-change revenue.
Tire 	DTC 	channels 	(Discount 	Tire, 	Costco, 	online) 	are 	taking 	share. 	Labor 	inflation 	crushes 	a

losing battles in melting-ice-cube businesses. Microcap liquidity ($540M cap) means any forced exit by Icahn

returns. Sale-leaseback of owned real estate could fund a $200M+ buyback at this depressed multiple. Mavis or

repeatedly. Even a modest re-rating to 8x normalized EBITDA implies $25-30/share. The poison pill is a
negotiating posture, not a permanent block — Icahn's mere presence forces a strategic review. Path to re-rate: (1)
board settlement granting 1-2 seats, (2) sale-leaseback announcement, (3) sale process by year-end 2026.

it could be a clean carve-out. Third: the poison pill triggers at 15%; Icahn is at 14.79%, deliberately one-tick
below. That's a tell — he's signaling "I'm not trying to buy you, I'm trying to get your attention" while preserving
optionality. The 145-store closure list is not public; the filing tax loss alone could be material.

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 111
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
• Status: Active proxy contest. Investor presentation released April 14, 2026. Vote expected at June 2026 annual
meeting
ANALYST SUMMARY
WEX runs three segments: Mobility (fleet fuel cards — ~60% of revenue, the crown jewel), Corporate Payments
(B2B accounts payable automation), and Benefits (HSA/FSA administration). The fleet card franchise generates
~50% EBITDA margins with sticky enterprise contracts (Wells Fargo, the U.S. government, large logistics
fleets). At $173.50 the stock trades at ~10x forward EPS and ~8x EBITDA — a discount to FLEETCOR/Corpay
(~13x) and to its own pre-2023 multiple (~17-20x). The mispricing is a reaction to (a) interchange fee
compression litigation/regulation, (b) EV transition fears for fleet fuel revenue, (c) declining consumer fuel prices
reducing per-transaction interchange.
Bear case: WEX's fleet card moat erodes structurally as commercial fleets electrify. Charging at depot/home
circumvents the point-of-sale model entirely; even if WEX builds an EV charging network product, unit
economics are worse. Interchange fee caps (Visa/Mastercard settlement, potential Durbin extension to credit)
compress the spread permanently. Corporate Payments segment has never lived up to its acquisition premium
(eNett, Optal). Health benefits is a low-margin admin business. Activists in payments have a mixed record (Elliott
at Salesforce-style outcomes are rare); a proxy fight ending in a board settlement may not move the underlying
earnings power.
Bull case: Fleet electrification is a 15-year curve, not a 3-year one — gas-powered commercial fleets dominate
through 2030+. WEX has signed EV charging partnerships and is positioned to ride either fuel type. Sub-10x
earnings on a high-quality, recurring-revenue fintech is anomalous; PE buyout precedent (FLEETCOR's
Stonepeak/Apollo flirtation, Edenred's continued accretion) makes WEX a legitimate take-private candidate at
$230-260. Impactive's nominees bring fintech/payments operating experience the current board lacks. Path to
re-rate: (1) ISS/Glass Lewis recommend in Impactive's favor → settlement granting 1-2 seats, (2) accelerated
buyback announcement, (3) strategic review or PE bid.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K discloses WEX's float income — interest earned on customer balances — has become a meaningful
EBITDA contributor in the high-rate environment. As rates fall in 2026-2027, that tailwind reverses; the company
has been quiet about how much of recent earnings is rate-driven vs. core fee growth. Second alpha point: the
Benefits segment houses an HSA custodial deposit base of several billion that is essentially a free-deposit bank —
this is structurally undervalued inside a payments multiple and could be spun or sold to a HSA Bank or
HealthEquity 	for 	premium. 	Third: 	Impactive's 	April 	14 	presentation 	reportedly 	highlights 	specific
underperformance vs. self-set guidance — the kind of "promises broken" data that typically wins ISS support.
Finally, board reduction from 10 to 9 is a tell that the board fears the proxy math.
CATALYSTS
• ISS / Glass Lewis recommendations (May 2026)
• 2026 annual meeting vote (June 2026)
• Q1 / Q2 2026 earnings prints
• Potential settlement granting Impactive 1-2 seats
• Strategic review announcement / PE inbound

Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 114
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
• Status: Active engagement, no formal proxy contest. Starboard pushing publicly via letters; management
responsive but pace of additional leases is the contested issue
ANALYST SUMMARY
Riot operates two business lines today: (1) bitcoin mining at Rockdale (Texas) and other sites — historically the
entire revenue base, exposed to BTC price + halving cycle + difficulty, and (2) data center hosting — newly
emergent, anchored by the AMD lease. Riot's strategic asset is power: 1.7GW total contracted with the ERCOT
grid, including 1GW of expansion capacity at Corsicana, Texas — built originally for mining but architecturally
convertible to liquid-cooled HPC. At $18.11 the equity is ~$6.9B; net of cash and BTC holdings the EV is
~$5-6B against 1.7GW. AI data center comps trade at $20-40M/MW (CoreWeave, Iris Energy AI segment,
Applied Digital). Even at $15M/MW, capacity alone is worth $25B — 4-5x the current EV. The market is
refusing to pay because (a) lease conversion is unproven at scale, (b) capex per MW for HPC is much higher than
for mining, (c) Riot's mining cash flow is volatile.
Bear case: AI data center economics require liquid cooling, high-redundancy power, fiber to multiple carriers,
and Tier III+ certification — none of which Riot's mining shells were built for. Conversion capex could be
$5-10M/MW, requiring multi-billion in financing that dilutes equity heavily. Hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google,
Meta) prefer building their own; Riot's natural customers are Tier-2 AI compute (CoreWeave, Lambda,
neo-clouds) — those tenants are themselves financially fragile. AMD lease is real but small relative to capacity;
one swallow doesn't make a summer. Bitcoin price downturn (e.g., post-halving difficulty squeeze) crushes
interim cash flow. Starboard's history with technical pivots (Box, Salesforce) is good; with energy/infrastructure
pivots, less proven.
Bull case: The grid-power scarcity is real and getting worse — ERCOT interconnect queues are 5+ years out for
new builds. Riot's Corsicana site is one of a handful of multi-hundred-MW shovel-ready sites in North America.
Even one additional hyperscaler lease (Microsoft, Oracle, Meta rumors all circulating) at $250-400M annual rent
transforms the equity story from "miner" to "data-center landlord with optionality." Path to re-rate: (1) second
hyperscaler announcement → multiple expansion from miner-comp to data-center-comp, (2) potential REIT spin
of HPC assets at premium valuation, (3) sale of mining business to Marathon/CleanSpark to fund full conversion.
Starboard's letter explicitly invokes $1.6B EBITDA potential — math implies $40-60 stock if executed.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K discloses Riot's power purchase agreements include a controversial demand-response program with
ERCOT — Riot gets paid to curtail mining during grid stress, generating ~$100M+ in some quarters. That
ERCOT relationship is the real moat: it took years to negotiate and would transfer to any HPC tenant. Second
alpha point: Corsicana's substation interconnect is already energized for 1GW — moving from "permitted" to
"energized" cuts 24-36 months off any HPC competitor. Third: the AMD lease is structured as a hosting deal, not
a real estate lease — meaning Riot retains operational responsibility (and margin) on power management, which
is a higher-margin model than pure colocation. Fourth: Starboard's February letter reportedly cited specific MWs
of stranded capacity that should be repurposed — a level of operating detail that suggests deep insider
engagement, not a drive-by activist.
CATALYSTS
• Additional hyperscaler / AI tenant announcements (Q2-Q4 2026)
• Q1 2026 earnings (May 2026)
• Bitcoin price action / 2024 halving cycle dynamics

reflects the Netflix bid value plus minor optionality. Comps: Disney trades at higher multiples on Studios + parks;
Paramount-Skydance pro forma is the closest analog; standalone cable/network multiples have collapsed to 3-4x
EBITDA.
Bear case: The Netflix deal is what shareholders are going to get — Paramount's balance sheet post-Skydance
merger is stretched, and Larry Ellison's appetite for sweetening has limits. The cable network stub, if the deal
closes, is a melting ice cube: linear ad revenue declining double-digits annually, sports rights inflation crushing
margins, no obvious buyer for cable assets at any price. Zaslav's track record on capital allocation
(Discovery-AT&T; merger, $50B in net debt acquired) is poor; betting on board accountability when Zaslav
controls the proxy machine is a lottery ticket. Ancora's $200M stake is too small to drive board change at a $69B
mega-cap; their wins (Norfolk Southern, U.S. Steel) involved larger stakes and operating-vulnerability narratives
that don't apply here.

stub trades to $5-8 plus you own pro forma Netflix shares with synergy upside. Ellison/Skydance has
demonstrated willingness to overpay for media (Paramount itself); a $35-40 counter-bid is not crazy, and a third
party (Comcast, Apple, even Saudi PIF) could enter as a white knight. Path to re-rate: (1) Paramount counter-bid
escalates pre-vote, (2) shareholders reject Netflix deal forcing auction, (3) post-deal stub trades cheap enough to
be acquired by PE.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE

either continuous reorganization or aggressive add-back accounting; either way, true free cash flow is overstated.
Second filing-level alpha: the Netflix deal documents (when fully proxied) will reveal management's tax-deferral
structuring — historically Zaslav's deals (Discovery-Scripps, AT&T; spin) used Reverse Morris Trusts, and any
complexity in the Netflix structure suggests room for Paramount to offer a cleaner alternative. Third: HBO's
content library is on the balance sheet at amortized cost, materially below market value — the Harry Potter, DC,
Friends, Sopranos catalogs alone are worth $20B+ in a content-scarce streaming world. Fourth: Ancora's previous
campaigns (Norfolk Southern, U.S. Steel) succeeded because they identified governance misalignment; their
public letters here will likely surface director conflicts that force at least board engagement.

VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
• Status: Active proxy contest ahead of 2026 annual meeting (May/June expected)
ANALYST SUMMARY
First Industrial owns ~67M square feet of industrial real estate concentrated in 15 major U.S. logistics markets
(Southern California, South Florida, Dallas, Northern New Jersey, Seattle). Pure-play distribution/warehouse
REIT — no office, retail, or specialty exposure. At $63.75 the equity is ~$8.7B; with ~$2.4B net debt the EV is
~$11B. Trades at ~6.0% implied cap rate vs. industrial peers (Prologis, Rexford, Terreno) at 4.5-5.5% — the
spread implies either FR's cash flows are lower-quality, or it's mispriced. Litt's NAV math says mispriced.
Industrial REIT sentiment cratered in 2024-2025 on (a) post-COVID warehouse oversupply, (b) Amazon's
pullback on incremental space, (c) tariff/onshoring uncertainty, (d) capital-rotation away from REITs into AI /
data center comps.
Bear case: The industrial NAV discount is rational, not anomalous. Sublease space across major logistics markets
sits at multi-year highs. New supply continued to deliver through 2024-2025 even as demand slowed; rent growth
in core markets has decelerated from ~10% (2022) to ~3-4% (2025) and could go negative in oversupplied
submarkets. FR's portfolio, while quality, is not the trophy-grade Class-A that Prologis owns; its same-store NOI
growth lags. Litt's track record is real but his last few campaigns (Aimco, Apartment Income REIT) ended in
board-level frustration without deal outcomes. Take-private interest from Blackstone/Brookfield is more selective
in 2026 than in 2021 — REIT M&A; volumes are down significantly. Even if Litt wins his board seat, six months
is a tight deadline for a strategic process.
Bull case: Industrial real estate is the right asset class long-term — onshoring, e-commerce penetration still
rising, last-mile demand structural. The supply wave is digestible: starts have collapsed in 2024-2025 (~50% off
peak), implying tighter market by 2027. FR's portfolio is concentrated in supply-constrained coastal markets
where land scarcity matters most. The NAV discount opens the door to either (a) accretive asset sales at private
market cap rates funding buybacks, (b) full take-private by Blackstone (which already owns ~$70B of industrial
via BREIT), or (c) merger with a peer (Terreno, Rexford). Path to re-rate: (1) Litt board win May/June 2026, (2)
$500M+ asset disposition announcement at sub-5% cap rate proves NAV math, (3) take-private bid emerges by
Q4 2026.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The 10-K discloses FR has been a meaningful net seller in recent quarters, but disposition cap rates have not been
disclosed asset-by-asset — Litt's letter likely benchmarks recent transactions against the implied trading cap rate
to prove the NAV gap. Second filing-level alpha point: FR has a development pipeline of ~$400M+ that's
accretive at completion but a near-term FFO drag — a take-private buyer could finance the lease-up phase with
cheaper private capital, immediately accretive. Third: the company's debt maturity schedule has a 2027-2028 wall
that creates negotiating leverage for the activist (refinancing risk concentrates board attention). Fourth: Land &
Buildings' February 26 letter reportedly includes specific peer-by-peer NAV comparisons — a tactic Litt used
effectively at Taubman to force Simon to raise its bid by ~$1B. Fifth: Blackstone's BREIT has been a significant
buyer of single-tenant industrial in 2025 — they know FR's portfolio intimately.
CATALYSTS
• 2026 annual meeting (May/June) board vote
• ISS / Glass Lewis recommendations on Litt nomination
• Asset disposition announcements (Q2-Q3 2026)
• Strategic alternatives review (six-month deadline ~July/August 2026)

Performance Food Group is a $60B+ revenue broadline foodservice distributor (Vistar, Performance Foodservice,

acquiring Reinhart and Core-Mark. Foodservice distribution is a scale game — route density, private label

(13-14x) and US Foods (~11x) despite comparable growth and identical end markets. Sachem's thesis is that
combining #2 USFD and #3 PFGC creates a credible #2 to Sysco, with $400-600M+ identified synergies
(procurement, route consolidation, private label) — Ferguson, who knows both boards from prior investments, is
uniquely positioned to broker.
Bear case: FTC approval is the binary. A USFD/PFGC combination would create a clear duopoly with Sysco in
broadline foodservice — even a more permissive FTC may demand large divestitures in overlapping metros, and
a deal could be blocked outright. Standalone PFGC carries $5B+ of net debt; if the macro softens (restaurant
traffic weak through 2025), EBITDA could disappoint and the multiple compress further. Independent restaurants
— the highest-margin customer cohort — have been losing share to chains. If Sachem walks or settles for
cosmetic governance changes, the merger optionality decays quickly.
Bull case: Two independent payoff paths. (1) Definitive USFD+PFGC merger announcement at $115-130/share,
even with required divestitures, would reprice PFGC to a deal-weighted ~$110+ today versus $93.50 spot —

PE/strategic LBO target at ~$110-125 (cash flows support 7-8x leverage). Even no-deal, standalone re-rate to 12x
EBITDA on margin improvement is ~$115. Median sell-side PT $116.50 already discounts these scenarios
partially. Ferguson's board presence is the highest-conviction governance signal in the activist universe right now.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The "clean team agreement" between PFGC and USFD is not boilerplate — it is the specific legal mechanism
used when two competitors begin sharing competitively sensitive financial and operational data under
HSR-compliant antitrust counsel supervision, almost always a precursor to definitive merger negotiations. A
screen sees a stake and a board seat; the alpha is that Ferguson's audit & finance committee assignment with
explicit M&A; oversight gives him direct visibility into both sides' synergy modeling. Additionally, Sachem's
prior involvement with USFD (it has held both names) means Ferguson can credibly broker price/structure
between the two boards in a way an outside banker cannot.

• PE LBO bid emerging if the strategic combination is shelved

integration drag. Engine Capital filed September 2025, secured two board seats and a Transformation Committee
in February 2026, and you are buying at <6x normalized EBITDA into multiple bottoming end-markets with
engineering IP that competitors cannot replicate (RAD shock platforms, Live Valve electronic suspension,
OEM-spec'd Ford Raptor and GM Z71 platforms).
ACTIVIST CAMPAIGN
Engine Capital is Arnaud Ajdler's ~$500M-1B activist fund, focused on small/mid-cap operational turnarounds
— track record at Crane Holdings, OneSpan, Hill International, Penn Entertainment, Outfront Media — generally
constructivist with a willingness to escalate to proxy if needed. Co-investor color: Alan Howard (Brevan Howard
founder, deep capital, consistent activist co-invest pattern) and Brock Pierce in the broader investor base.
September 15, 2025 SC 13D demanded two board seats (including activist nominee Alan L. Bazaar), formation of
an advisory committee for operational excellence and margin improvement, and a strategic review. February 2026
cooperation agreement: Bazaar plus one additional director joining; Chairman Mendenhall and director Waitman
retiring at the 2026 annual meeting; Transformation Committee formally established with mandate over
operational, cost, and capital allocation deliverables.
ANALYST SUMMARY

Fox Factory is a four-segment business: Performance-Defined Products (bicycle suspension, the legacy crown
jewel), Powered Vehicles (UTV/snowmobile/motorcycle aftermarket), Aftermarket Applications (lift kits, the
SCA acquisition for Ford/GM/RAM trucks), and Diversified Products (Marucci baseball bats, the controversial
2023 acquisition). FOX is the dominant premium spec for bike and UTV OEMs (Specialized, Trek, Polaris,
Can-Am) — engineered IP and OEM design-in cycles create a real moat in those niches. Trades <6x normalized
EBITDA versus 10-12x historical and ~10-12x for sporting goods peers. Activist target was the operational
discipline — gross margin compression from acquisition integration, weak working capital management, and an
over-extended balance sheet from the Marucci deal.
Bear case: All four end markets are cyclical and all four are weak simultaneously — premium bike industry
inventory destocking has been longer and deeper than expected, UTV/powersports retail is at multi-year lows on
consumer-discretionary fatigue, OEM truck SAAR is decelerating, and Marucci faces baseball bat seasonality
plus integration overhang. Net leverage is elevated post-Marucci ($1B+ of debt against ~$200M EBITDA),
constraining buybacks and creating refinancing risk if rates stay sticky. Marucci was a strategically debated
acquisition (baseball bats are not the same business as suspension engineering); divesting it would crystallize
losses. Bike OEM customer Specialized has had its own balance-sheet problems.
Bull case: Cycle-bottom math with credible operational accountability. (1) Bike channel inventory has been
correcting for 6+ quarters — destocking ends, OEM orders normalize, and operating leverage on FOX's
high-margin PDP segment is meaningful. (2) UTV/powersports replacement cycle and dealer floor restocking
should turn through 2026. (3) Truck OEM platform refreshes (Ford Raptor R, GM ZR2 expansion) drive
multi-year FOX-specific volume. (4) Engine's Transformation Committee with clear deliverables creates
execution accountability — historic margin recovery from 25% gross to 32%+ on operational fix. Re-rate to 8-9x
normalized EBITDA on $250M+ recovered EBITDA equals $30-40 per share — 75-130% upside off $17.37.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Transformation Committee structure with explicit operational excellence and margin-improvement charter is
the buried alpha. Engine forced a formal committee (not just a board seat) with stated quarterly deliverables —
that is the structural mechanism for accountability that screens cannot read. A screen sees a busted growth-stock
chart at $17 with 6x EBITDA; the filing-level alpha is that two long-tenured directors (Mendenhall, Waitman) are
retiring and Bazaar's mandate explicitly covers strategic review, which keeps the divestiture-of-Marucci
optionality on the table — a non-trivial sum-of-the-parts uplift if executed. The OEM design-in pipeline
(Ford/GM truck programs spec'd 24-36 months out) is also invisible in trailing financials.
CATALYSTS
• 2026 annual meeting (likely May) and Transformation Committee initial deliverables
• Q1/Q2 2026 earnings — bike channel destocking completion signal
• Any divestiture announcement (Marucci is the watch-list)
MUNGER CHECKLIST
• Simple, understandable business: 8/10
• Durable competitive advantage: 8/10 (engineering IP, OEM spec-in)
• Honest, able management: 5/10 (Marucci capital-allocation drag)

ANALYST SUMMARY

VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
Adtalem operates four core institutions — Chamberlain University (the largest US nursing program by
graduation), Walden University (online; the asset being acquired), Ross University School of Medicine and Ross
University School of Veterinary Medicine, and American University of the Caribbean — focused exclusively on
healthcare/professional education end markets. Post the 2022 ATI divestiture, the business is high-quality,
cash-generative, and ride the structural tailwind of US nursing and clinician shortages. Q2 fiscal 2026 EPS up
35% year-on-year demonstrates the standalone earnings power. Trades around 14-15x earnings — historically
deserved a premium valuation given 90%+ of degrees are in growing healthcare verticals; activist thesis is that
capital allocation discipline (buybacks over a questionable acquisition) drives the next leg of multiple expansion.
Bear case: For-profit higher education is permanently politically exposed — Department of Education
rule-making, 90/10 rule, gainful employment regulations, and federal investigation overhang at Walden are real
risks even outside the acquisition. If management closes the Walden deal over activist objections, integration
costs, federal investigation contingent liabilities, and brand-impairment risk all materialize. The for-profit
education category multiple ceiling has historically been low. If the strong Q2 earnings momentum decelerates
(enrollment softens, Chamberlain growth normalizes), the standalone re-rate thesis weakens. Activists are 1%
holders — limited proxy leverage if management dismisses them.
Bull case: A clean asymmetric capital-allocation event. (1) If Adtalem walks from Walden using the
federal-investigation MAC clause, the $1.5B is redeployed into buybacks at a ~$3.5B market cap — that is
roughly 40%+ of float repurchasable, mathematically transformative for EPS and the multiple. The $750M
authorization in December 2025 already signaled board willingness. (2) If Adtalem closes Walden cleanly, the
deal does add scale in online programs and credit accretion (~$25/share NPV per the activist analysis) — even if
the market does not fully credit it day-one, execution evidence over 12-18 months drives re-rating. (3) Q3/Q4
fiscal 2026 results plus the Investor Day in February 2026 provide near-term catalysts. Re-rate to 17-18x on $7-8
of forward EPS equals $120-140 per share — 20-40% upside.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The federal investigation into Walden is the legal exit ramp that screens cannot price. Most acquisition
agreements contain a Material Adverse Change (MAC) clause, and a federal regulatory investigation that
materially impacts the target's business is a textbook MAC trigger — Engine and Hawk Ridge specifically cited
the investigation as Adtalem's contractually clean off-ramp. A screen sees an education company doing a $1.5B
deal; the filing-level alpha is that the activists are not asking management to break a contract, they are pointing at
a contractually-built exit door. Additionally, the December 2025 $750M buyback authorization within weeks of
the activist letter is tacit board acknowledgement that capital-return is on the table — this is the leading indicator
that a Walden deal abandonment scenario is being seriously considered internally.
CATALYSTS
• Walden deal close or termination decision (most binary catalyst)
• Federal investigation outcome at Walden (MAC trigger watch)
• Q3/Q4 fiscal 2026 results + Investor Day commentary on capital allocation pace
MUNGER CHECKLIST
• Simple, understandable business: 8/10
• Durable competitive advantage: 7/10 (Chamberlain nursing scale, Ross med-school accreditation moat)
• Honest, able management: 7/10 (executing well standalone; Walden deal is the question mark)

ANALYST SUMMARY
Exor is the holding vehicle of the Agnelli/Elkann family, controlled via Giovanni Agnelli BV (~53% of votes). Its
dominant single asset is Ferrari (RACE) — even after a EUR 3B placement in H1 2025, Exor still holds ~20%
economic / ~30% voting. Stellantis (STLA), CNH, and the now-being-sold Iveco stake (Tata acquired Exor's
27.1% for EUR 3.8B in 2025) anchor the auto/industrial sleeve. Healthcare is the growing pillar — Philips at
18.4%, plus bioMérieux. Lingotto, the family's internal asset manager, holds ~EUR 4.2B in funds and direct
stakes. NAV is reported semi-annually with mark-to-market for listed and book/fund values for unlisted.
The bear case is structural: 53% family voting control kills any takeover premium; the holdco discount has
averaged ~35% over a decade and has widened to ~45-50% as auto cyclicality bites. Ferrari fell 22% in 2025 and
Stellantis fell 19%, dragging NAV per share down 8% to EUR 164.4. Italian/Dutch listing complexity, EUR
currency exposure for USD investors, and the OTC ADR (EXOSF) being a stale-quoted ghost line all add
friction. Lingotto carries fund-level fees, and central holdco overhead (~EUR 70m/yr) is a permanent NAV drag.
There is no catalyst calendar — buybacks are episodic.
The bull case is brutal arithmetic. Ferrari alone, at Exor's ~20% stake and Ferrari's $66B market cap, is worth
~EUR 12-13B vs Exor's entire EUR 16-17B market cap. Everything else — Philips, Stellantis, CNH, Lingotto,
Iveco cash — is approximately a free option. The Iveco sale closing brings ~EUR 3.8B of recyclable cash. Each
EUR 1 of NAV growth at Ferrari translates to roughly EUR 0.50 of EXO price; if the discount even normalizes to
its 10-year average of ~35%, EXO re-rates ~25% before any NAV growth. Buybacks at half NAV are ~2x
accretive per share retired.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Iveco-to-Tata transaction (announced July 30, 2025) hands Exor ~EUR 3.8B of cash mid-2026 — recycled
into either a new EUR-billion buyback or healthcare deployment. John Elkann's letters increasingly emphasize
Lingotto, which now owns stakes in Christian Louboutin, Shang Xia, Welltec, and a venture book — a future
Lingotto IPO would crystallize ~EUR 4B of currently-marked-at-fund-NAV value. The Stellantis CEO transition
(Antonio Filosa took over in mid-2025 after Carlos Tavares departed) creates execution risk but also turnaround
optionality. Note also: Exor took a stake in Royal Philips at distressed levels in 2022-23-25 and is now the largest
shareholder ahead of any margin recovery. The OTC EXOSF symbol is stale — execute via EXXRF (more liquid
OTC ADR) or the Amsterdam EXO listing for any size.
CATALYSTS
• Iveco-Tata sale closes (mid-2026), ~EUR 3.8B cash inflow
• FY2025 Annual Report and any new buyback announcement (March 2026 cycle)
• Stellantis Capital Markets Day under new CEO (H1 2026)
MUNGER CHECKLIST
• Understandable business model: 9/10

ANALYST SUMMARY
Post-split Vivendi is a near-pure listed-stake holdco. The crown jewel is ~10% net economic exposure to
Universal Music Group (UMG) — the world's largest recorded-music company, currently worth EUR ~38B at
UMG.AS. Telecom Italia is a smaller residual stake being progressively trimmed. Gameloft (mobile gaming) is
the only meaningful unlisted asset, marked conservatively. The Bolloré family controls ~29.3% via Compagnie
de l'Odet. Vivendi has self-published monthly NAV since December 2024 to combat info asymmetry, a
corporate-governance positive.
The bear case is the Bolloré overhang and process risk. Bolloré orchestrated the 2024 split partly to escape "de
facto control" rules under French market law. The Court of Cassation in November 2025 overturned the Paris
Court of Appeal finding that Bolloré controls Vivendi — a setback for minority shareholders who had hoped for a
mandatory tender at fair value. The case has been remanded; oral arguments come May 22, 2026. UMG itself has
fallen ~10% YTD 2026 on streaming-growth deceleration, dragging Vivendi NAV. Central run-rate ~EUR
85m/yr is a permanent stub-cost. Gameloft is hard to mark and could disappoint. Liquidity is moderate; VIVHY
ADR is OTC pink with wide spreads.
The bull case is binary catalysts paired with mark-to-market hard NAV. If the May 2026 hearing — or any
subsequent appeal — re-confirms control, Bolloré is forced to launch a mandatory cash offer at a "fair price" that
arbitrators argue should approximate NAV. That alone implies a near-double from current levels. Even without a
tender, continued asset sales (Vivendi has trimmed Telefonica and Telecom Italia in 2024-25) plus the
self-published NAV format compress the discount. UMG's strategic value (Spotify renegotiation, AI music
licensing, possible US listing in 2026) provides NAV-growth optionality. The current ~50% discount to a
transparent, mark-to-market portfolio is the type of asymmetric setup Asset Value Investors (AVI) has been
writing checks for.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The May 22, 2026 Court of Cassation remand hearing is a hard date — minority shareholders Phitrust and Ciam
have been activist on this since 2024, with the AMF originally siding with them (decision later voided). In early
2026, Compagnie de l'Odet (Bolloré's own holdco) sold ~6m UMG shares at EUR 27.50 — a quiet way to extract
value before any forced bid. Vivendi's monthly NAV publication is unique among European holdcos and has
narrowed the discount somewhat since launch but still leaves 50% — historically high. The Gameloft division is
the wild card: rumors of a sale or carve-out have circulated. Note: VIV.PA is the primary line; VIVHY ADR is
OTC pink and quotes can drift relative to Paris. Use limit orders.
CATALYSTS
• May 22, 2026 — Court of Cassation remand oral arguments
• UMG Q1 2026 earnings + any US dual-listing announcement
• Continued monthly NAV publications + further asset sales
MUNGER CHECKLIST

Cannae was spun from Fidelity National Financial in 2017 as Bill Foley's standalone investment vehicle. Foley's
track record (FNF, Black Knight, MSG-style M&A; acumen) gives Cannae an unusual proprietary-deal pipeline.
The portfolio has rotated dramatically — from a public-stake bag (D&B;, Ceridian/Dayforce, Alight) into private
operating businesses, with Black Knight Football Club (BKFC) now the cornerstone. BKFC owns 100% of AFC
Bournemouth (Premier League), 100% of FC Lorient (Ligue 1), majority of Moreirense (Portugal), with strategic
affiliations to Auckland FC, Orlando City SC, Kyoto Sanga. TTM revenue at BKFC ~$267m. Cannae
self-publishes monthly SOTP and has been buying back stock aggressively below NAV.
The bear case centers on related-party governance and asset opacity. Trasimene Capital — a Foley-affiliated
management company — collects fees on Cannae assets, a structure activist Carronade explicitly criticized.
Multiple private buckets (BKFC, Restaurant Group, JANA, consumer brands) are hard for outside analysts to
mark, creating a "trust me" valuation discount. European football economics are notoriously cash-burn-heavy;
Premier League survival is not guaranteed. The 2025 AGM saw the management pay proposal rejected — a
positive for shareholders but a sign of structural conflict. Liquidity is thin (~$680m mcap) and float-shrinkage
further reduces tradability.
The bull case is multi-layered. ~$500m returned to shareholders in 21 months and the float retired by ~34% — a
true buyback machine below NAV. The Restaurant Group is under strategic review (read: likely sale). BKFC is
the asymmetric upside — recent European football transactions (Chelsea minority sale at $3B+ EV; Manchester
United partial sale; Premier League franchise valuations rising sharply) suggest a single comp event could
multi-bag the implied football value. Carronade winning board seats means more shareholder-aligned capital
allocation. With ~$200m+ in cash and Treasuries plus public stakes, the implied private-asset value is
meaningfully discounted.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The February 23, 2026 SOTP refresh confirmed the BKFC mark at $249m invested for ~42% economics —
implying group EV around $590m. AFC Bournemouth's 2024-25 Premier League finish + new TV deal ramp
could re-rate BKFC alone by 30-50% within 18 months. The Trasimene management agreement is worth reading
in proxy footnotes — Carronade's activist letters argue it transfers ~$10-15m/yr of value to Foley affiliates. The
2025 AGM "no" vote on pay is a rare governance win at a Foley vehicle. Note that JANA Partners' Cannae stake
is itself an interesting kicker — Cannae owns equity in an activist firm. Watkins beverage and Minden Mill
bourbon are early-stage consumer bets; Sightline Payments was written down materially — a reminder that not
every Foley deal works.
CATALYSTS

Net Asset Value 	~6,290
NAV per share 	~$80.60
Current share price 	$44.36

ANALYST SUMMARY
IAC is Barry Diller's serial-spinout machine — having previously birthed and separated Match Group, Expedia,
LendingTree, Vimeo, Angi, and others. CEO Joey Levin runs the parent. Post-March 2025 Angi spin, IAC is now
a focused holdco with: (1) a ~25% MGM Resorts stake (acquired in 2020-22 at much lower prices, now ~$2.5B);
(2) People Inc. (the rebranded Dotdash Meredith) — owner of People, Better Homes & Gardens, Investopedia,
Allrecipes, Verywell — generating Q4 '25 digital revenue of $355m (+14% YoY) with full-year adj. EBITDA
guide of $310-340m; (3) Care.com, which swung Q4 EBITDA to $19m (+135%); (4) smaller bets in Vivian
Health and the Daily Beast. ~$700m of net cash sits at the parent for opportunistic capital allocation.
The bear case is the chronic conglomerate discount and a digital-ad cycle. People Inc. is the swing factor — print
revenue fell 23% in Q4 '25 and the digital-ad business is sensitive to a recession. Care.com is rebuilding but still
small. The MGM stake's "unlock" thesis depends on Diller actually monetizing — and he tends to wait years.
Print-magazine assets inside People Inc. depress reported multiples vs pure-digital comps. Spin candidates
(People Inc.) are not yet committed. Diller is in his 80s — succession is real risk despite Levin running ops.
The bull case is mathematical. MGM stake ($2.5B) + net cash ($0.7B) = $3.2B vs the entire $3.4B market cap —
which means the rest of IAC (a top-3 US digital publisher generating $300m+ EBITDA) trades at essentially zero.
People Inc. comparables (NYT at ~25x, Ziff Davis at ~7x, Future PLC at ~8x) imply a $2-3B fair value alone.
IAC has spun out 10+ companies; People Inc. is the obvious next candidate once digital growth reliably outpaces
print decline. Levin retired ~10% of float in 2025 ($37m in Q4 alone) and is buying back below SOTP.
MGM-Osaka opens 2030 — long-tail re-rating optionality.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
IAC bought 1m additional MGM shares in Q4 2025 for $40m — Levin still adding to the position despite the
parent trading below MGM-stake-plus-cash. The Dotdash-Meredith integration is officially "complete" as of late
2025 and management has subtly opened the door to a People Inc. spin in the 2026 letter. The $800m senior notes
refinancing at People Inc. in Q1 2025 cleaned up the capital structure ahead of a potential separation. Care.com's
swing to ~$70m+ run-rate EBITDA makes it a legitimate sale candidate to a strategic (e.g., a benefits platform).
Diller's last shareholder letter explicitly framed IAC as "a portfolio of underappreciated assets" — Diller-speak
for "we're going to surface value." Note: the 10-K (filed Feb 20, 2026) discloses Diller still controls Class B
supervotes, so any breakup requires his consent — but his entire career is breakups.
CATALYSTS
• People Inc. (Dotdash Meredith) spin-off or sale announcement (2026 most likely)
• Care.com sale to a strategic
• Continued buyback tempo + any MGM stake monetization commentary
MUNGER CHECKLIST
• Understandable business model: 8/10
