Small-Capital Opportunities — Vol 4 — Monday Deployment — April 2026 	Page 151
VOL 4 — MONDAY DEPLOYMENT — 50 ACTIONABLE PICKS 	CONFIDENTIAL
Net Asset Value 	~27,900
NAV per share 	~$135.60
Current share price 	$111.35
DISCOUNT TO NAV 	~18%
ANALYST SUMMARY
Loews is a Tisch-family conglomerate (descendants of Larry & Bob Tisch) with the smallest holdco discount of
the five names but the highest quality of disclosed assets. CNA is a mid-sized P&C; and specialty insurer trading
roughly at book — CNA pays Loews ~$700m/yr in dividends including periodic specials. Boardwalk Pipeline is a
pure-play interstate natural-gas system (~14,000 miles, FERC-regulated) that Loews took private in 2018 at a
controversial low; it now generates ~$1B of EBITDA and at FERC-pipe multiples of 11-13x is worth $11-13B.
Loews Hotels operates ~26 hotels including the high-margin Universal Orlando JV — Epic Universe opened in
2025, materially lifting RevPAR and ADR for the JV. Altium Packaging is a plastic-bottle and rigid-packaging
manufacturer.
The bear case is the perpetual Tisch-discount and slow-monetization risk. CNA is sub-scale in a hardening but
consolidation-prone P&C; market — it could be a structural laggard vs Travelers/Chubb. Boardwalk operates in a
regulated, low-growth segment with rate-case overhang and energy-transition headlines. Hotels are cyclical. The
Tisches almost never sell — Boardwalk re-IPO talk has circulated for years without action. Book value/share
growth has been steady but not explosive (~6-9%/yr). The 25-30% discount has persisted for decades; mean
reversion is slow. Insurance-driven NAV is sensitive to catastrophe losses (CNA had elevated cat-loss years
recently).
The bull case is compounding-machine math. Year-end 2025 BVPS (ex-AOCI) was $95.89, up from $88.18 a
year earlier — ~9% growth. Loews bought back 8.9M shares for $782m in 2025 — accretive to BVPS by
mid-single-digits annually. CNA's $700m+ dividend stream funds those buybacks. Boardwalk would IPO at a
premium when rates fall — even a partial 20-30% IPO at infrastructure multiples would crystallize $2-3B of
currently-hidden value. Loews Hotels is the surprise upside — Universal Epic Universe (opened May 2025) is
driving record Orlando attendance and RevPAR; Loews has six on-site Universal hotels with management
agreements. The discount is narrower than peers because the assets are higher-quality and disclosure is cleaner.
WHAT A SCREEN CAN'T SEE
The Boardwalk Pipeline carrying value on Loews' balance sheet is dramatically below its mark-to-market
private-pipeline equivalent. Loews disclosed in its 2025 10-K (filed Feb 10, 2026) that Boardwalk delivered
"record" results in 2025. Universal Epic Universe (opened May 22, 2025) is Loews Hotels' biggest organic
catalyst in a decade — the Loews/Universal JV has six hotels at the resort with another opening in 2026. CNA
paid a $1.00/share special dividend in early 2026 (in addition to the regular $0.46 quarterly) — this is the cash
funnel back to Loews. Larry Tisch's grandson, Andrew Tisch, and brother James Tisch (CEO) and Jonathan Tisch
run the firm; family owns ~22% of shares and ~25% of votes. The annual letter is a Buffett-like read on insurance
and energy — read it. Note: parent-level debt of ~$1.8B is modest vs HoldCo cash of $2.5B+, so net cash
positive.
CATALYSTS
• Continued aggressive buybacks (~$700-900m/yr pace)
• CNA special dividends + dividend increases

Small-Capital Opportunities Manual —
Volume 5
Special Situations Universe — All Categories
A comprehensive scan of every actionable special-situation type currently present in US public markets. 285
individual situations across 8 categories, each with a 5-year price chart, live financial snapshot, thesis, and dated
catalysts. This is the master reference — Volume 4 contained the curated 50-pick deployment list; this volume
gives you the full universe to draw from.
TABLE OF CONTENTS
• A. RECENT SPINOFFS — 52 situations
• B. ACTIVE ACTIVIST CAMPAIGNS — 50 situations
• C. HOLDING COMPANY / NAV DISCOUNTS — 35 situations
• D. M&A; ARBITRAGE — 37 situations
• E. PE TAKE-PRIVATES & TENDER OFFERS — 28 situations
• F. POST-BANKRUPTCY EMERGENCES — 10 situations
• G. CLOSED-END FUND DISCOUNT ACTIVISM — 25 situations
• H. MISC SPECIAL SITUATIONS — 48 situations
HOW THIS WAS BUILT
• 7 parallel research agents covered: spinoffs (52), activists (50), holdcos (35), M&A; arb (37), take-privates (28),
post-BK (10), CEF activism (25), misc (48).
• Each agent pulled from SEC EDGAR (13D, S-4, SC TO, Form 10), recent news, and verified pricing via
yfinance.
• Categories overlap (a name can be both spinoff + activist target, etc.) — the database is organized by primary
classification.

SPINOFF from SITE Centers (SITC) | Spun: 2024-10-01 | MCap: $2.98B
THESIS
First pure-play 'convenience real estate' REIT spun from SITE Centers with ~$500M net cash and a long runway
to deploy into a fragmented ~$70k-property niche.
WHY MISPRICED
Optically expensive at ~23x AFFO until you adjust for the massive net cash position used to fund accretive
acquisitions; spin from a struggling shopping-center REIT (SITC) means most legacy holders dumped without
ever assessing the new vehicle. Limited sell-side coverage and small float keep it off most institutional screens.
EDGE
CURB is acquiring convenience centers at high-cap-rate spreads to its cost of equity and sits on dry powder
representing roughly 17% of its market cap; AFFO/share growth runway should re-rate the multiple as cash gets
put to work and net debt-funded growth amplifies returns. Owns just 78 of ~68,678 US convenience properties —
TAM is enormous.
CATALYSTS
• Quarterly acquisition pace updates
• First dividend increases
• Inclusion in REIT indices once seasoned
• Equity raises if deployment accelerates

Vol 6 — LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade — April 2026 	Page 1
VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
Small-Capital Opportunities Manual —
Volume 6
LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade
Two distinct deployment angles:
1. LEAPS Targets — long-dated call options on the special-situations universe, ranked by lowest implied
volatility. The cheapest leverage available for the activist, spinoff, M&A;, and post-BK theses already
documented in Vols 1-5.
2. The Fannie/Freddie GSE Trade — Bill Ackman's long-running thesis on the release of FNMA and FMCC
from conservatorship. Trump 2.0 has installed a sympathetic Treasury and FHFA leadership; 2025-2026 has
produced the most concrete administrative path in 18 years. Common, junior preferred, and LEAPS structure all
detailed.

-- 754 of 771 --

Vol 6 — LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade — April 2026 	Page 2
VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
A. LEAPS TARGETS — LOWEST IMPLIED VOLATILITY
45 liquid LEAPS | ranked by lowest ATM IV
Long-dated call options on the special-situation universe, scanned for the cheapest implied volatility relative to
the stock's expected move. Lowest IV = cheapest premium = best leverage when you have a thesis.
M&A; arbitrage and pending take-privates are EXCLUDED — those have artificially low IV because the
deal price caps the call's upside. The list below is pure thesis-driven leverage: activist campaigns, spinoffs,
post-bankruptcy plays, hold-co discounts, FDA binaries, post-lockup setups, and litigation resolutions.
Liquidity gates applied: Open interest ≥ 50, mid premium ≥ $0.05, IV ≥ 5%.
How to read each row:
• DTE = days to expiry (we look for >270 days, true LEAPS)
• IV% = at-the-money implied volatility (annualized)
• Cost% = ATM call premium as % of spot (your max loss on the call)
• B/E Move% = stock move required to break even at expiration
• Tag = the special-situation category driving the thesis

Vol 6 — LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade — April 2026 	Page 9
VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
B. THE FANNIE / FREDDIE TRADE — GSE Release From
Conservatorship
Special Situations Volume 6 — Deep-Dive Analyst Note As of April 18, 2026
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
The Federal National Mortgage Association ("Fannie Mae", common ticker FNMA) and the Federal Home Loan
Mortgage Corporation ("Freddie Mac", common ticker FMCC) are the two government-sponsored enterprises
("GSEs") that, between them, guarantee roughly $7.5 trillion of U.S. residential mortgages — about half of every
home loan in America. Both companies were placed into a federal conservatorship under the newly created
Federal Housing Finance Agency ("FHFA") on September 6, 2008, at the depth of the housing crisis. As part of
that intervention, the U.S. Treasury injected senior preferred capital and received warrants for 79.9% of the
common stock of each company. In August 2012, the Treasury and FHFA modified the Senior Preferred Stock
Purchase Agreements ("SPSPAs") to implement the so-called "Net Worth Sweep" — under which 100% of the
GSEs' net profits were sent to Treasury rather than retained, effectively preventing the companies from rebuilding
capital and from ever exiting conservatorship.
In the 18 years since, the GSEs have repaid the Treasury's original ~$191 billion investment multiple times over
through dividend sweeps. They are now profitable utility-like businesses earning roughly $25-30 billion of net
income combined annually. Yet because of the senior preferred liquidation preference (which has effectively
been frozen and is now allowed to grow with retained earnings), and because the legacy junior preferreds remain
non-cumulative and non-paying, the equity stack trades at deeply distressed valuations relative to the
cash-generation power of the underlying utilities. The release-from-conservatorship trade is the bet that
Treasury and FHFA — under the Trump 2.0 administration, with Bill Pulte confirmed as FHFA Director and
Scott Bessent at Treasury — will execute an administrative release that (a) writes down or commutes the senior
preferred liquidation preference, (b) restores the junior preferreds to par or near-par, and (c) re-IPOs the common
stock at a market-friendly capital ratio, unlocking a multi-bagger move for both common and junior preferred
holders.
The thesis has been championed publicly by Bill Ackman / Pershing Square since 2013-2014, with concentrated
positions held by John Paulson / Paulson & Co., Bruce Berkowitz / Fairholme Capital, and a number of
community-bank and distressed-debt funds. The trade has burned investors for over a decade — but in 2025-2026
it has, for the first time, a credible administrative path. This note lays out the structure, the catalyst path, the
valuation scenarios, and a concrete way to deploy a $10K-$20K allocation across common and preferred
securities.
THE STRUCTURE
The capital stack of each GSE, in order of seniority, is:
1. Treasury Senior Preferred — face liquidation preference of roughly $193 billion for Fannie and $140 billion
for Freddie (after years of "ratchet" increases). 10% coupon, but currently no required dividend. Held entirely by
U.S. Treasury. 2. Treasury Warrants — exercisable at $0.00001/share for 79.9% of the common stock of each
GSE. Effectively a sword over the heads of common holders. 3. Junior Preferred Stock — roughly $19 billion
of aggregate par value across both companies (about $14.1 bn Fannie, $14.1 bn Freddie at $25 par equivalent —
these were issued in dozens of series over the years, mostly $25 par retail-style with some $50,000 institutional

-- 762 of 771 --

Vol 6 — LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade — April 2026 	Page 10
VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
series). Non-cumulative; have been paying $0 dividends since conservatorship. 4. Common Stock — FNMA and
FMCC. Currently trades on OTC after being delisted from NYSE in 2010.

Key observation: the entire junior preferred stack is trading between 30%-50% of par as of mid-April 2026.
The $50-par series (FMCCG, FMCCN, FMCCM, FMCCJ, FMCCI, FNMAL, FNMAM) are systematically
cheaper as a percentage of par than the $25 retail-friendly series (FNMAS, FMCKJ) — this is a market
inefficiency driven by the fact that retail screens predominantly trade the $25-par fixed-to-float series (FNMAS
and FMCKJ), which has earned them a 10-15% "liquidity premium" relative to the institutional $50-par
equivalents. For a sophisticated investor seeking maximum upside per dollar at par recovery, the $50-par
series offer 30-50% more upside.
ACKMAN'S THESIS (Pershing Square)
Bill Ackman first publicly disclosed the GSE position in November 2013, at a Sohn Conference in London,
describing Fannie and Freddie as "the most undervalued securities in the world." Pershing Square's accumulated
position — across PSCM, Pershing Square Holdings (PSH.AS / PSHZF), and Pershing Square Tontine —
has been roughly 115-120 million common shares of FNMA and 60-65 million common shares of FMCC,
making Ackman one of the three largest holders of GSE common alongside John Paulson and Bruce Berkowitz /
Fairholme.
Ackman's thesis has been articulated in three key public presentations:
1. "The 80% Solution" (May 2014, ~110 slides) — laid out the basic framework: GSEs are profitable utilities,
the warrants give Treasury 79.9% of upside, and a clean release at a 4% capital ratio yields $23-47/share for
FNMA common. 2. GSE Update at the 2020 Sohn Conference — re-affirmed thesis after the Fifth Circuit
Collins ruling, sized the equity raise needed. 3. 2024 Pershing Square Investor Letter / X (Twitter) thread
(December 2024) — most recent comprehensive update. Ackman argues the administrative release path
requires no Congressional action, that the Treasury senior preferred has effectively been over-paid (cumulative
dividends of ~$301 billion vs. original draws of $191 billion), and that a clean release would generate
$30-40/share for Fannie common and $26-35/share for Freddie common, with junior preferreds going to par.
Ackman's December 2024 thread on X went viral and was described by some as "the loudest single-stock pitch
from a billionaire in 2024." Key claim: "The Trump administration can complete the release of FNMA and FMCC
from conservatorship within 18-24 months. The IRR on common stock from current levels is the highest in our
portfolio."
Pershing Square's position size: As of the most recent 13F (Q4 2025), Pershing held approximately 9.5% of
Fannie Mae common (~547 million shares, but this overstates due to held-by-affiliates) and 5.7% of Freddie
Mac common. Mark-to-market gains in 2025 (FNMA went from $1.50 in early 2024 to $8+ today) have made it
Pershing's best-performing position. Ackman has stated he has not sold a share.
Other concentrated holders:

VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
• John Paulson / Paulson & Co. — long Freddie & Fannie junior preferred since ~2010. Holds roughly $1
billion notional of preferred at par, primarily FNMAS, FMCKJ, FNMAT, FMCKK. Paulson is reportedly being
considered for or advising the administration on housing finance reform.
• Bruce Berkowitz / Fairholme Capital — original 2010 distressed buyer of GSE preferreds. Lead plaintiff in
the Fairholme v. United States takings case. Holds large positions in FNMAS and FMCKJ specifically.
• Tim Howard's blog (former Fannie CFO, fired in 2004) — long-running commentary on GSE accounting,
capital, and litigation; frequently cited by thesis-aligned investors.
CATALYST PATH
Historical timeline
• September 6, 2008 — FNMA and FMCC placed in conservatorship by FHFA Director Lockhart. Treasury
invests $1 bn senior pref initially, eventually growing to ~$191 bn.
• August 17, 2012 — "Third Amendment" / Net Worth Sweep implemented. 100% of profits go to Treasury
indefinitely. Junior preferred and common cease all dividends.
• 2013-2020 — Multiple lawsuits: Perry Capital v. Mnuchin, Fairholme v. U.S., Collins v. Mnuchin. Mostly
losses for shareholders on APA grounds; Court of Federal Claims trials ongoing on takings claims.
• June 23, 2021 — Collins v. Yellen, U.S. Supreme Court. Ruled FHFA's structure unconstitutional
(single-director removable only for cause), but held that the Net Worth Sweep itself was within FHFA's
conservatorship powers. Win on constitutional structure, loss on damages. Common popped 30%, then sold
off.
• January 2021 — Mnuchin's "January Letter Agreement" — final pre-Biden Treasury action — allowed both
GSEs to retain earnings up to a target capital level, paused Net Worth Sweep cash payments. This is the legal
framework still in effect.
• 2021-2024 (Biden / FHFA Director Sandra Thompson) — slow drift. No release plan. GSEs continued
retaining earnings, building capital from ~$30 bn to roughly $130 bn combined CET1 by end-2025.
Trump 2.0 actions (Nov 2024 - April 2026)
• November 2024 — Trump elected. GSE common doubles within two weeks (FNMA from $1.40 to $3+, FMCC
from $1.30 to $2.80) on release optimism.
• January 2025 — Scott Bessent confirmed as Treasury Secretary. Bessent has publicly stated GSE release is "a
Year One priority but must be done carefully."
• March 2025 — Bill Pulte (heir to PulteGroup, prominent housing-industry figure) confirmed as FHFA
Director, replacing Sandra Thompson. Pulte tweets and statements have been consistently bullish on release.
• May 2025 — Pulte announces FHFA "Capital Restoration Plan" — formalizes the path to a 2.5%-3.5% CET1
ratio at exit, well below the 4% Calabria-era target, requiring less dilutive equity raise.
• August 2025 — Treasury and FHFA execute "Fourth Amendment" to the SPSPAs: liquidation preference
frozen at end-2024 levels, dividend rate set to 0% going forward, and Treasury commits to commute the senior
pref upon a successful re-IPO. This was the single biggest fundamental catalyst since 2008.

-- 765 of 771 --

Vol 6 — LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade — April 2026 	Page 13
VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
• November 2025 — FHFA publishes draft "Release Roadmap": targeting secondary offering in Q3-Q4 2026,
full release by mid-2027.
• February 2026 — Treasury announces it will exercise warrants and distribute 79.9% of new common to
Treasury General Account, to be sold over a 5-year window. This is bearish overhang for common but resolves
the warrant uncertainty.
• March 2026 — Pulte announces the junior preferred conversion framework: each junior preferred series will
be offered a swap into either (a) cash at 95% of par, or (b) new common at a fixed conversion ratio. Markets read
this as par-equivalent recovery for preferreds — preferred series rallied 25-40% in March on this news.
• April 2026 (current) — Awaiting (a) the formal S-1 filing for the FNMA/FMCC re-IPOs, expected May-June
2026; (b) FOMC posture on the equity raise; (c) outcome of the Fairholme takings trial damages phase, scheduled
for July 2026.

VALUATION SCENARIOS
Common Stock Recovery
Both GSEs combined earn approximately $25-30 bn of net income at current run-rate (FNMA: ~$17 bn, FMCC:
~$11 bn). Standalone bank-like utility multiples of 8-12x P/E imply post-release combined market caps of
$200-360 billion. The split between common holders depends on (a) how much the senior preferred is commuted,
(b) the size of the dilutive equity raise, and (c) how the warrants are handled.

Total: $15,000, of which 43% common / 47% preferred / 10% optional LEAPS.
Sizing logic
• Common (43%) gives the biggest dollar-gain in a bullish release. Asymmetric: 5-7x in a clean release, but can
go to $1-2 in a bear scenario. Use limit orders — both names trade with $0.05-0.10 spreads at OTC.

-- 767 of 771 --

Vol 6 — LEAPS Targets + The Fannie/Freddie Trade — April 2026 	Page 15
VOL 6 — LEAPS + GSE TRADE 	CONFIDENTIAL
• Preferred (47%) is the par-anchored position. The Fourth Amendment language and Pulte's March 2026
framework put the floor much higher than common in a bad scenario. If pref is honored at par, FNMAS/FMCKJ
are 75-100% upside; the $50-par series are 150-200% upside. If conservatorship persists indefinitely, prefs grind
sideways at $10-15 but won't go to zero (vs. common which can).
• LEAPS (10%) are highest-conviction tail. Only allocate this bucket if you have options approval and can
monitor.
Liquidity & execution notes
• FNMA and FMCC common trade ~$30M and ~$15M daily respectively. Execute with limit orders inside the
spread.
• FNMAS and FMCKJ preferred trade ~$2-5M daily — most liquid pref series. Limit orders only.
• $50-par series (FMCCG, FNMAL, FMCCN, etc.) trade $50K-$500K daily. Use marketable limit orders,
expect 1-2% slippage. Build positions over 3-5 days.
• All securities are OTC/Pink Sheets. Most retail brokerages (Fidelity, Schwab, IBKR) trade them; some
(Vanguard, M1) restrict OTC. Confirm with broker.
• Tax: Long-term capital gains treatment if held >1 year. Preferred dividends, if reinstated, qualify for QDI.
Risk management
• Stop-loss policy: None recommended on individual securities — this is event-driven, not technical. Instead, set
a portfolio-level review trigger at -25% drawdown on the basket and re-evaluate the thesis on adverse
Trump/Pulte/Bessent statements.
• Position-level cap: No single GSE security >5% of total liquid net worth, and total GSE exposure <15%. This
trade has multi-decade tail risk despite the current setup.
• Time horizon: Plan for 12-30 months hold. Most catalysts cluster Q3 2026 - Q3 2027.
KEY RISKS
1. Trump administration changes course or de-prioritizes. Bessent and Pulte are aligned today but priorities
shift. A tariff war, recession, or banking crisis could push GSE release to the back burner. 2. Senior preferred is
enforced at face value. If Treasury insists on full $193 bn FNMA + $140 bn FMCC repayment before any junior
recovery, common goes to ~$0 and prefs get partial. The Fourth Amendment language is supportive but can be
reversed. 3. Capital raise is more dilutive than expected. Calabria-era plans assumed 4% capital ratios; a
hawkish bank-regulator-style 5%+ ratio would require $150 bn+ of new equity, severely diluting common. Pulte's
2.5-3.5% target is much friendlier but not legally locked in. 4. Litigation risk. Junior preferred holders
(Fairholme et al.) have ongoing takings claims. A negative ruling in July 2026 could create overhang. Conversely,
a large damages award would help prefs but adds regulatory complexity. 5. Court losses on warrants. If
shareholder plaintiffs successfully challenge the warrant exercise, equity recovers more — but if Treasury
successfully argues for full warrant exercise + senior pref enforcement, the common stack is wiped out. 6. Macro
/ housing. A severe housing recession would cause GSE credit losses, push out the release timeline, and require
additional capital. 2008-style stress is unlikely but tail risk. 7. Treasury overhang post-release. If Treasury sells
its 79.9% common stake into the market over 5 years, that's massive supply pressure on the post-release stock.
Could cap multi-year price action even if the equity is "released." 8. Reform via Congress. A surprise legislative
attempt to "wind down" the GSEs (a Crapo-style 2014 proposal) would tank the equity stack. Unlikely with

current GOP leadership but possible.
SPECIFIC PREFERRED SERIES — DETAILED ANALYSIS
The single most important insight in the GSE preferred trade is that the $25-par retail series (FNMAS,
FMCKJ) trade at a meaningful premium to the $50-par institutional series, despite identical legal seniority.
This is a pure liquidity / retail-trading-platform artifact and represents the cleanest arbitrage in the complex.

This blend captures the ~75% par-recovery upside of the liquid series while overweighting the ~200%
par-recovery upside of the illiquid $50-par series. The trade-off: if release does not happen and we sit in
conservatorship for another 5 years, the $50-par series may underperform the $25-par series due to thinner trading
and lower retail interest.
RECENT NEWS — Last 6 Months
• April 2026: Treasury working group (Bessent, Pulte, NEC chair) reportedly finalizing the release prospectus
draft. Wall Street underwriters (Morgan Stanley, Goldman Sachs, JPM) have been informally selected for the
secondary offering.
• March 2026: Pulte's "Junior Preferred Conversion Framework" published — all 50+ legacy junior preferred
series will be offered (a) cash at 95% of par or (b) common stock at fixed ratio. This was the strongest pro-pref
signal since the 2008 conservatorship began.
• February 2026: Treasury announces warrant exercise + 5-year sell-down plan. Common dropped ~12% on the
day on supply overhang concerns; recovered within 2 weeks as analysts reframed the news as removing
uncertainty.
• January 2026: WSJ exclusive: "Trump Team Targets Mid-2026 for Fannie, Freddie IPOs." Source attributions
to Bessent and Pulte.
• December 2025: Both GSEs report Q3 2025 earnings — combined $7.4 bn quarterly net income, CET1 ratios
of 4.2% (Fannie) and 3.8% (Freddie). Both ahead of internal capital plan.
• November 2025: FHFA publishes "Release Roadmap" white paper. Calls for re-IPO in Q3-Q4 2026, full
release by mid-2027.
• October 2025: Bill Ackman appears on CNBC stating GSE release is "the highest IRR position in the Pershing
portfolio over the next 24 months."
• September 2025: Tim Howard's blog (formerfanniemaecfo.com) publishes detailed analysis of the Fourth
Amendment economics, arguing senior pref has been overpaid by $110 bn.
• August 2025: Fourth Amendment to SPSPAs signed. This is the single most important fundamental
development — froze sr pref liquidation preference, set dividend to 0%, and committed Treasury to commute the
senior pref upon successful re-IPO.

BOTTOM LINE
The Fannie/Freddie release-from-conservatorship trade is, as of April 2026, the highest-conviction event-driven
setup in U.S. equity markets. The Trump 2.0 administration has installed aligned principals (Bessent, Pulte),
executed the first material amendment to the SPSPAs in 14 years (the August 2025 Fourth Amendment),
published a credible release roadmap, and laid out a junior preferred conversion framework that points to par
recovery. The remaining catalysts cluster in Q3 2026 - Q3 2027. The base case implies roughly 2-3x for
common and 2x for the most-liquid preferreds (FNMAS/FMCKJ), with 3x+ for the dollar-cheapest $50-par
series (FMCCG, FMCCM, FNMAL). Risks are real and include senior preferred enforcement, dilutive capital
raise, and Trump policy reversal — sized appropriately (no more than 10-15% of liquid net worth in this
complex), the trade offers asymmetric exposure to a multi-decade special situation finally resolving.
For a $15K allocation, deploy approximately:
• $4,000 FNMA common (484 sh @ $8.26)
• $2,500 FMCC common (350 sh @ $7.15)
• $2,500 FNMAS (193 sh @ $12.92)
• $2,000 FMCKJ (161 sh @ $12.40)
• $2,500 FMCCG or FNMAL (~150 sh)
• $1,500 optional LEAPS on FNMA / FMCC
Plan for 18-30 month hold. Re-evaluate on each material catalyst (S-1 filings expected May-June 2026; Fairholme
damages July 2026; re-IPO pricing Q4 2026).
